excerpt.en: Maitreya Preaching

viewingNote.zh: 有些造像打动人的，不只是美，而是你站在它面前时，会突然意识到：它不只来 自一个时代，更像从一整片文明交汇的深处走来。这件 8 世纪的吉尔吉特说法弥 勒，就是这样。 吉尔吉特位于今天巴基斯坦北部，处在喀喇昆仑与兴都库什山脉之间。它不是边 缘，而是通道：连接印度、中亚、高原世界，也是佛教传播的重要节点。贵霜衰 落后，吉尔吉特逐渐发展成独立王国，并在 6 至 8 世纪迎来高峰，建立统治体 系，扩建宫殿与寺院。但它始终处在多方势力交汇之中，与克什米尔、吐蕃等政 权时而联盟，时而冲突，最终在 11 世纪被克什米尔并入。 所以，吉尔吉特艺术为什么复杂？因为它的命运本身就是复杂的。你能在其中看 到犍陀罗的遗风、笈多的圆融、克什米尔的精致。也正因此，今天学界往往把吉 尔吉特与克什米尔一带的造像，统归为更大的“克什米尔艺术风格”。 吉尔吉特曾是佛教传播中的重要一环，但随着王国覆灭与环境变迁，许多作品早 已散失。幸运的是，因为与西藏有过交流，少量作品得以进入西藏，并在长期供 奉中被保存下来。这件 8 世纪的说法弥勒，就是这样的幸存者。同题材存世极少， 因此它的价值，不只是“古”，也不只是“美”，而是它本身就是一段文明还能被看 见的证据。 这件作品为什么它会让人第一眼停下来？ 首先是比例。一件佛教雕塑能不能成立，比例极关键。而比例从来不是抽象的， 它总与观看位置有关。到这件弥勒时，我最先感受到的是：它并不像为高处遥望 而做，反而更像放在人经过时，微微抬头便能相遇的位置。 弥勒的头部，是从近乎球体的基础上展开的。头型椭圆，螺发饱满，小脸内收， 五官却被刻意放大。深刻的眉线、细长的眼睛、宽厚的鼻子、收束的嘴部，共同 构成一种很强的精神力度。这张脸最迷人的地方，在于它的矛盾性：整体是圆润、 柔和、丰盈的，可眉眼和口部的刻线又非常清晰，于是柔和里始终带着一丝锐利。 远看时，它散发出的不是单纯的慈悲，也不是单纯的威严，而是一种柔中带刚的 气质。 有一次，我从侧面与它对视，忽然觉得它的眼神既慈悲，又冷漠。起初我不明白， 后来才意识到：这种复杂感，不只来自造像，也来自观看者自己。当你孤独时， 可能会从中读出冷漠；当你渴望安慰时，又会读出慈悲。它并不是在不断变化， 而是以一种足够深的神情，让你在它面前照见自己。看似无情，实则悲心深藏； 看似疏远，实则无所不包。正因为它不急着评判你，这份宁静反而显得格外温柔。 弥勒手结说法印，通肩袈裟自然垂下。上半身衣纹呈放射状展开，下半身近乎湿 衣裹体，仍能看到犍陀罗传统的清晰遗痕。服饰边缘的花边又多了几分贵族化的 柔软与秩序。软垫上雕有带波斯意味的小花，下方是带有罗马柱形制的建筑构件， 两侧各立一只圆雕狮子。这种结构深受希腊、罗马世界影响，是吉尔吉特造像极 具代表性的特征之一。 于是，这尊弥勒就不只是“坐在那里”，而像是被一整套建筑性的秩序托举出来。 莲瓣托起一切，狮子撑住空间，弥勒坐于其上，如山，如星辰。如果想象自己被 缩小，站在它面前抬头去看，你会明白它真正厉害的地方：它能在有限的尺度里， 造出一种宏大的经验。 也正是这种宏大感，让这件弥勒最后把人带到了一个更深的问题里：既然弥勒象 征未来，那么时间真的存在吗？ 在宇宙 138 亿年的尺度面前，海龟的一百五十年、大象的九十年、人类的七十 年、猫的十五年、蝴蝶的十一个月、浮游的一天，似乎都只是转瞬。而在星空中， 我们赖以生存的地球，也不过只是那个“暗淡蓝点”。 从空间和时间的尺度看，人实在太渺小了。可偏偏，渺小的我们，却拥有极为独 特的东西。作为同样由碳构成的生命，我们却拥有抽象思维、推理能力、创造能 力，也拥有道德感、反思力与自我发问的能力。康德说：有两样东西，他愈是长 久地凝视，愈会生出惊叹与敬畏——一个是头顶的星空，一个是心中的道德律。 这句话之所以动人，恰恰因为它说出了人类最深的奇异性：我们明明终将化作尘 土，却仍然能在有限生命里，思考无限，选择善，承担责任。而站在弥勒面前时， 这种奇异性被放大了。它让人意识到：人的伟大，不在于寿命，不在于财富， 而在于即使身处短暂与无常之中，仍然能够追问意义、回应痛苦、走向觉醒。 于是，问题终于走到了最深处。 当我们看着月升日落，看着钟表滴答作响，看着自己从孩童渐渐步入中年，我们 会本能地觉得：时间在流动，它当然存在。牛顿说，时间是绝对的，像一条独立 的直线，不依赖于我们是否运动。爱因斯坦则说，时间是相对的，它与空间交织 在一起，会因为速度和引力而改变。而量子引力的一些思考又进一步提出：时间 也许并不是独立存在的实体，它只是人类用来描述变化的一种方式。而佛教的看 法，则又更进一步。佛教并不把时间看作一种固定、实有、独立存在的东西。过 去、现在、未来，都是缘起与无常中的假名安立。真正能被把握的，不是抽象的 时间本体，而是此刻这一念。 所以，当弥勒作为“未来佛”出现在我们面前时，它并不是只在谈一个遥远的未来。 它更像是在反问我们：如果未来是由当下组成的，那么彼岸，会不会本就在此岸 之中？如果时间并不是牢不可破的实体，那么你此刻这一念，又是不是比你想象 中更真实？也许，所谓“未来”，并不是迟到的答案，而是当下正在形成的方向。 也许，弥勒真正送给我们的，不是关于未来的承诺，而是一个关于“当下”的礼物。 所以，这件吉尔吉特说法弥勒之所以动人，不只是因为它稀少，也不只是因为它 风格独特。它真正珍贵的，是它把很多分散的东西凝缩到了一起：一条文明通道 的历史，一个王国的命运，几种艺术传统的碰撞，一位匠人对比例、结构与气息 的精准把握，以及一个关于慈悲、宇宙、生命与时间的深层追问。所以，站在它 面前，我们看到的，不只是一件古代雕塑。我们看到的，是人类如何在有限生命 中，始终试图触碰无限。而弥勒留给我们的最后一个问题，也许始终是：当你凝 视未来时，你能否真正看见此刻？
viewingNote.en: Some sculptures move us not merely by their beauty, but because—standing before them—one suddenly realizes: they do not simply belong to a single era; rather, they seem to emerge from the very depths where entire civilizations converged. This 8th-century figure of Maitreya Preaching the Dharma from Gilgit is precisely such a work. Gilgit is situated in what is today northern Pakistan, nestled between the Karakoram and Hindu Kush mountain ranges. Far from being a peripheral zone, it served as a vital corridor—a nexus connecting India, Central Asia, and the world of the high plateaus, and a pivotal node in the dissemination of Buddhism. Following the decline of the Kushan Empire, Gilgit gradually evolved into an independent kingdom. It reached its zenith between the 6th and 8th centuries, establishing a robust administrative system and undertaking the expansion of its palaces and monasteries. Yet, it remained perpetually situated at the crossroads of competing powers, engaging in alternating periods of alliance and conflict with polities such as Kashmir and the Tibetan Empire, until it was ultimately annexed by Kashmir in the 11th century. Why, then, is the art of Gilgit so complex? Because its very destiny was complex. Within it, one can discern the lingering legacy of Gandhara, the harmonious grace of the Gupta Empire, and the exquisite refinement of Kashmir. Precisely for this reason, contemporary scholarship often groups the sculptural works of Gilgit and the surrounding region under the broader rubric of the "Kashmiri Art Style." Gilgit once played a pivotal role in the propagation of Buddhism; however, with the collapse of the kingdom and the vicissitudes of time and environment, many of its artistic treasures have long since been lost. Fortunately, thanks to historical exchanges with Xizang, a small number of these works found their way into that region, where they were preserved through centuries of continuous veneration. This 8th-century figure of Maitreya Preaching the Dharma is one such survivor. Sculptures of this specific subject matter are exceedingly rare in existence; thus, its value lies not merely in its antiquity or its aesthetic beauty, but in the fact that it stands—in and of itself—as tangible evidence that a lost civilization can still be seen. What is it about this particular work that compels a viewer to pause at first glance? First and foremost: its proportions. For a Buddhist sculpture to truly succeed—to possess that sense of inherent rightness—its proportions are absolutely critical. Yet, proportions are never merely an abstract concept; they are invariably defined by, and relative to, the viewer's vantage point. Upon encountering this figure of Maitreya, my immediate impression was this: it does not appear to have been created to be viewed from a lofty distance; rather, it seems designed to be placed at a level where a passerby need only lift their gaze slightly to meet its eyes. The head of this Maitreya emerges from a foundation that is nearly spherical in form. The head is oval, the snail-shell curls of the hair are full and lush, and the face itself is compact and refined; yet, the facial features have been deliberately accentuated. Deeply incised brows, slender eyes, a broad and substantial nose, and a tightly composed mouth collectively project an intense spiritual power. The most captivating aspect of this face lies in its inherent paradox: while the overall impression is one of roundness, softness, and fullness, the incised lines defining the brows, eyes, and mouth are remarkably sharp and distinct—imbuing that softness with a persistent trace of keenness. Viewed from a distance, the aura it radiates is neither mere compassion nor mere majesty, but rather a temperament characterized by strength tempered with gentleness. On one occasion, as I gazed at it from a profile angle, I was struck by the sudden sensation that its expression was simultaneously compassionate and aloof. At first, I was perplexed; only later did I realize that this sense of complexity stems not solely from the sculpture itself, but also from the viewer. When you feel lonely, you may perceive aloofness in its gaze; yet, when you yearn for solace, you may perceive compassion. It is not that the image itself is in constant flux; rather, through an expression of profound depth, it serves as a mirror in which you may glimpse your own reflection. It appears dispassionate, yet conceals a deep reservoir of compassion; it appears distant, yet is all-encompassing. Precisely because it does not hasten to pass judgment upon you, its stillness feels all the more tender. Maitreya’s hands form the *Dharmachakra Mudra* (the gesture of expounding the Law), while his *kasaya*—a monastic robe draped over both shoulders—falls in natural folds. The drapery across the upper torso radiates outward in a fan-like pattern, whereas the fabric clinging to the lower body resembles wet garments adhering to the form—a stylistic choice that clearly bears the enduring imprint of the Gandhara tradition. The decorative borders adorning the edges of the robe lend an additional touch of aristocratic elegance, characterized by both softness and rhythmic order. The soft cushion upon which the figure rests is carved with delicate floral motifs suggestive of Persian influence; beneath it lies an architectural plinth featuring elements reminiscent of Roman columns, flanked on either side by a fully three-dimensional sculptural rendering of a lion. This structural configuration, deeply influenced by the Greco-Roman world, stands as one of the most distinctive and representative characteristics of the sculptures from the Gilgit region. Thus, this figure of Maitreya is not merely "sitting there"; rather, it appears to be uplifted and borne aloft by an entire architectural order. Lotus petals cradle all things; lions brace the space; and upon them sits Maitreya—steadfast as a mountain, radiant as a star. If you were to imagine yourself miniaturized—standing before it and gazing upward—you would grasp its true brilliance: within a finite scale, it succeeds in conjuring an experience of the sublime. It is precisely this sense of the sublime that ultimately leads this figure of Maitreya to guide the viewer toward a deeper inquiry: If Maitreya symbolizes the future, does time truly exist? Against the cosmic scale of 13.8 billion years, the turtle’s 150 years, the elephant’s 90, the human’s 70, the cat’s 15, the butterfly’s 11 months, and the plankton’s single day all seem to pass in the blink of an eye. And amidst the starry firmament, the very Earth upon which we depend for our existence appears as nothing more than that "pale blue dot." Viewed through the lens of spatial and temporal scales, humanity is indeed infinitesimally small. Yet, paradoxically, we—these tiny beings—possess something truly unique. Though composed of the same carbon elements as all other life forms, we are endowed with abstract thought, powers of reasoning, and creative capacity; we possess a moral compass, the ability to engage in self-reflection, and the capacity to question our own existence. As Kant observed: there are two things that, the more he contemplated them, filled him with ever-increasing awe and reverence—the starry heavens above him, and the moral law within him. The poignancy of this statement lies precisely in its articulation of humanity’s most profound paradox: though we are destined, ultimately, to return to dust, we nonetheless possess the capacity—within the confines of a finite life—to contemplate the infinite, to choose the good, and to shoulder responsibility. Standing before this figure of Maitreya, this paradox is magnified. It compels us to realize that human greatness resides not in longevity, nor in material wealth, but in the capacity—even amidst the fleeting and the impermanent—to seek out meaning, to respond to suffering, and to strive toward enlightenment. And so, the inquiry finally reaches its deepest core. As we watch the moon rise and the sun set, as we listen to the rhythmic ticking of a clock, and as we observe ourselves gradually transition from childhood into middle age, we instinctively feel that time is flowing—and that, surely, it must exist. Newton posited that time is absolute—like an independent, linear trajectory—existing irrespective of our own motion. Einstein, conversely, argued that time is relative; it is inextricably interwoven with space and subject to alteration by both velocity and gravity. Reflections within the realm of quantum gravity take this concept a step further, suggesting that time may not be an independently existing entity at all, but merely a construct humans employ to describe change. The Buddhist perspective advances this notion even further still. Buddhism does not regard time as a fixed, substantial, or independently existing entity. The past, present, and future are all merely nominal designations—conceptual constructs—arising from the interplay of dependent origination and impermanence. What can truly be grasped is not the abstract essence of time itself, but rather this very thought-moment occurring *right now*. Thus, when Maitreya appears before us as the "Future Buddha," he is not merely speaking of a distant tomorrow. Rather, he seems to be posing a rhetorical question: If the future is composed of a succession of present moments, might the "Other Shore"—the realm of enlightenment—already reside within *this* shore? If time is not an immutable, unbreakable entity, then is this very thought-moment you are experiencing right now not far more real than you had imagined? Perhaps the so-called "future" is not a belated answer awaiting its arrival, but rather a trajectory actively taking shape in the present moment. Perhaps the true gift Maitreya bestows upon us is not a promise regarding the future, but a gift concerning the "present." Therefore, this particular sculpture—the "Gilgit Maitreya Expounding the Dharma"—moves us not merely because of its rarity, nor solely because of its unique stylistic flair. Its true preciousness lies in its ability to distill a multitude of disparate elements into a single form: the history of a civilizational conduit, the destiny of a kingdom, the convergence of diverse artistic traditions, a craftsman’s precise mastery of proportion, structure, and spiritual resonance, and a profound inquiry into the nature of compassion, the cosmos, life, and time itself. Thus, standing before it, we behold not merely an ancient sculpture; we witness the enduring human endeavor—within the confines of a finite existence—to reach out and touch the infinite. And the final question Maitreya leaves with us, perhaps forever echoing, is this: When you gaze into the future, can you truly *see* the present moment?

note.zh: 著录： J. Siudmak, "The Development of the Classical Buddha Image from Kashmir, and Some Observations on Kashmirian Influence on the Sculpture of West Xizang,"
note.en: 著录：J. Siudmak, "The Development of the Classical Buddha Image from Kashmir, and Some Observations on Kashmirian Influence on the Sculpture of West Xizang,"

excerpt.en: A Kurkihar image of the Buddha with refined casting and devotional gravity.

viewingNote.zh: 在印度漫长的历史中，库尔基哈尔这个名字，或许不像菩提伽耶、那烂陀那样广 为人知。但在佛教古美术的世界里，它却因为一批风格独特、工艺精绝的造像， 被牢牢记住。 为什么是它？答案，要先从它所在的位置说起。 库尔基哈尔位于菩提伽耶与那烂陀寺之间。前者，是释迦牟尼成道之地，是整个 佛教世界的精神中心；后者，则是古代印度最重要的佛教学术重镇之一，汇聚了 无数前来求法的僧人。千百年来，信徒、学者、朝圣者往返于这两地之间。而库 尔基哈尔，恰恰处在这条道路上。它不是偶然被经过的地方，而是朝圣路线上一 个持续被激活的节点。换句话说，它离佛教世界最核心的两处圣地都很近——既 靠近“觉悟”发生的地方，也靠近“佛法”被整理、传授和传播的地方。正因如此， 库尔基哈尔从来都不只是一个地名。它更像是一处被信仰、学术与朝圣共同托举 起来的文化场域。 库尔基哈尔真正进入现代学术视野，是因为 1930 年的一次偶然发现。当时，当 地村民在挖掘荒土堆时，意外在古代红砖遗迹下发现了一批金铜造像。随着后续 考古工作的展开，最终清理出两百多件金属造像，尺寸从几厘米到一百五十多厘 米不等。这些造像风格统一却又高度成熟，部分还带有题记。这一发现，后来被 评价为继那烂陀之后，印度佛教考古史上最重要的发现之一。也正是在那一刻， 人们突然意识到：原来在菩提伽耶与那烂陀之间，还曾经存在过这样一个高度繁 盛的造像中心。 关于“Kurkihar”一词的来源，学界并非没有争议。最早发现库尔基哈尔的英国考 古学家马克汉姆·基托认为，这个名字可能来自“Koorka Vihara”，意思是“库克的 大寺院”。而印度考古学家亚历山大·康宁汉则提出另一种更具佛教意味的解释： 它可能源自“Kukkuta-pada Vihara”，与古代印度对鸡足山的称谓相关，因此可 理解为“鸡足山的大寺院”。这两种说法虽然不同，但都把问题指向同一个核心： 这里不是普通聚落，而是一处与寺院、圣地和朝圣传统密切相关的地方。而“鸡 足山”这个线索之所以重要，是因为在佛教传统中，它并不是一座普通的山。据 经典记载，释迦牟尼入灭之前，将袈裟与锡杖交予迦叶尊者，嘱托他前往鸡足山 入定，等待未来佛弥勒降世，再将法脉象征交付。也正因此，鸡足山在佛教历史 中，始终带着一种“过去与未来交汇”的意味。 玄奘、义净等中国高僧，也都曾在西行求法途中拜访过这里，并留下文字记载。 这些文献并不只是地理说明，它们更在不断强化一个事实：库尔基哈尔所在的区 域，并不是佛教世界的边缘，而是深深嵌在佛教记忆与神圣地图中的一部分。 如果继续追问，就会来到一个更关键的问题： 为什么库尔基哈尔风格的造像，今天存世如此少，而且大多带着明显的出土痕 迹？这背后，其实是一个断裂的历史。 到了 13 世纪初，随着穆斯林势力进入印度东北部，作为印度佛教最后重镇之一 的帕拉文化圈开始崩塌。寺院被毁，僧团流散，原本繁盛的佛教造像传统也随之 中断。库尔基哈尔，自然无法独善其身。也就是说，库尔基哈尔风格并不是自然 衰落，而是在一场大规模历史动荡中被突然掩埋的。正因为如此，我们今天在博 物馆或私人收藏中见到的库尔基哈尔造像，不论石质还是铜质，绝大多数都带着 明显的出土皮壳，它们不是在连续不断的本土供奉中被自然磨润出来的，而是被 埋进土中，沉默了几个世纪之后，才重新被人看见。而少量能够以“传世”状态延 续下来的作品，则显得格外罕见。因为这意味着：它们必须在那场断裂中被及时 带走，并在别的文化环境中继续被供奉、被珍惜、被保存。 我们现在终于可以回到眼前这件作品了。 这是一尊宝冠释迦坐像。在我看来，它最迷人的地方，不是单个局部的华丽，而 是那种一眼扑面而来的整体和谐。先看上半身。宝冠为三叶冠式，冠上嵌有银丝 与宝石；耳后横出的缯带前端，垂着镶嵌宝石的耳饰；项链上又以银与红宝石点 缀。这些装饰相当繁复，但奇妙的是，它们一点都不显凌乱，反而层层呼应，秩 序井然。 再看面部。嵌银的白毫与双眼，让神性一下被点亮；嵌红铜的嘴唇，则让微笑格 外鲜活。这种银、红铜与金铜之间的综合色彩关系，不只是材质上的变化，更是 气质上的安排：银让眼神清明，红铜让唇部有生命力，而金铜则把一切包裹在一 种温暖却尊贵的光泽之中。 所以，这张脸最打动人的地方，是它一边维持着王子的身份感，一边又没有拉开 与人的距离。它高贵，却不冷；神圣，却不僵；甚至带着一点轻盈的俏皮。那种 正面直视的笃定，不是压迫，而是一种年轻生命才会有的挺拔与正气。也正因如 此，这尊释迦并不显得遥不可及。它不像高高在上、只可仰望的神，反而像一位 你愿意靠近、甚至愿意与之交谈的王子。 再往下看，真正显出大师功力的，其实不是宝石，不是镶嵌，而是比例。袈裟的 处理极为简洁，只以边缘线轻轻带出。这样的处理，并没有削弱身体，反而让体 魄更健康、更明朗。简单的衣纹线条，把肌肉的起伏和体块的饱满准确勾勒出来， 丰盈而富有弹性。 下方莲座同样精彩。上下连珠纹颗颗饱满，中央莲瓣片片充满张力，排列几乎达 到一种近乎苛刻的对称。这种对称不是机械，而是一种被精心控制后的和谐。它 与上半身复杂而有序的华丽形成呼应，让整件作品从头到尾都维持在同一个呼吸 里。而最难的，其实是宝冠与头部的比例关系。宝冠释迦这个题材之所以难做， 就难在这里。宝冠如果稍大，就会显得头重脚轻；稍小，又会显得局促、小气。 可这尊作品恰恰把那个最微妙的平衡点抓住了。它华丽，却不拖沓；繁复，却始 终轻盈。这正是整体感真正成立的原因。 再加上全身宝石几乎都落在深红色系，金属镶嵌部分多为银质，与眼、唇色调形 成呼应。银经过氧化之后，收起了原本刺眼的亮度，与沉稳的红融合在一起，整 件作品于是呈现出一种极度克制、却又藏不住的贵气。这种贵，不是炫耀性的贵， 而是一种被审美充分消化之后的高贵。 到这里，一个更深的问题自然浮现出来：当年究竟是一位怎样的匠人，才能做出 这样的作品？ 在我看来，首先，这一定是一位大师级工匠。因为从建模、铸造、打磨到镶嵌， 这件作品几乎没有任何一处露怯。每一个环节都准确，每一个部分都收得住。这 样的完成度，不是仅靠天分就能达成的，它背后一定是极其大量的实践与极其成 熟的判断。 但仅有技术，还不够。这尊作品真正稀有的，是它呈现出的整体观。也就是说， 匠人在工作时看到的，不是局部，而是从头到尾、从冠到座、从颜色到比例、从 身份到气质的一整个系统。 很多作品失败，不是因为某个局部不好，而是因为局部都好，却无法汇成整体。 有些匠人会用更多装饰去弥补这种缺陷，可那些零碎的华丽，经不起推敲。 真正的和谐，无法靠堆砌获得。它只能来自于一个内心有秩序、有尺度、能从一 看到一百的人。所以我更愿意相信：这位匠人不仅技术高超，而且内心一定足够 从容、开阔。因为作品中的和谐，不只是审美训练的结果，往往也是人格与观看 方式的显影。 如果继续往下推，这件作品背后的答案，其实已经慢慢清楚了。 库尔基哈尔所在的比哈尔邦风格，与孟加拉邦非常不同。孟加拉更具动势，更外 放；而比哈尔则更端正、更高贵，更强调一种秩序中的正气。这一点，放在这尊 宝冠释迦身上非常明显。它没有夸张的动态，也没有炫技式的张扬，所有力量都 被收束进一种庄严、端正、平衡的格局里。这种气质，不禁让人想到宫廷艺术。 因为宫廷审美最看重的，从来不是单纯的动人，而是：秩序、平衡、等级感，以 及不容失误的体面。 而这一推测，其实并非空穴来风。在竹瑾居 2023 年的展览中，曾出现过一尊带 有铭文题记的世尊观音。从图像学判断，它属于典型的 11 至 12 世纪比哈尔邦 风格，而非孟加拉体系。铭文的大意可理解为：“印度施主为西藏施主供养所需 而造。” 这条铭文很重要。它至少说明了一点：当时比哈尔地区的工坊，不仅承担本地宗 教需求，也在为跨地区、甚至高等级的供养需求服务。而能够承接这样任务的， 绝不会是普通工坊。结合这件世尊观音，以及其他比哈尔邦作品的整体水准，再 回头看这尊宝冠释迦的美学高度，我们有理由推测：它极有可能是在严格要求之 下，由当时最顶级的工坊为高等级供奉需求而制作。至于这种高等级需求，是寺 院系统，还是更接近王室层面的供养，目前虽难下绝对定论，但从它整体的规格、 完成度与气质来看，它显然不是一件普通作品。 在那个时代，艺术家的名字往往不会被留下。但他们留下的作品，却替整个时代 说了话。这些造像，更是一段历史的见证，一种文化气质的结晶，一个时代如何 理解神圣、秩序与高贵的缩影。所以，当我们面对这尊宝冠释迦时，我们看到的 并不只是宝石、银丝、金铜与微笑。我们真正看到的，是一个地方如何因为靠近 圣地而繁盛，一个传统如何因历史断裂而沉埋，一位伟大的匠人又如何在短暂的 人生里，把那个时代最好的审美与精神，安放进一尊雕塑之中。 名字也许会失传。但真正伟大的作品，终究会替创作者活下来。
viewingNote.en: Throughout India’s long history, the name Kurkihar may not be as widely known as Bodh Gaya or Nalanda. Yet, within the realm of ancient Buddhist art, it is indelibly etched in memory thanks to a collection of sculptures distinguished by their unique style and exquisite craftsmanship. Why Kurkihar? The answer begins with its geographical location. Kurkihar lies situated between Bodh Gaya and the Nalanda Monastery. The former is the very site where Siddhartha Gautama attained enlightenment—the spiritual epicenter of the entire Buddhist world; the latter was one of ancient India’s most vital hubs of Buddhist scholarship, drawing countless monks in search of the Dharma. For millennia, devotees, scholars, and pilgrims have traveled back and forth between these two sites. Kurkihar sits precisely along this route. It was not merely a place stumbled upon by chance, but rather a continuously active node along this pilgrimage path. In other words, it lies in close proximity to the two most central holy sites of the Buddhist world—situated near both the place where "enlightenment" occurred and the place where the "Dharma" was codified, taught, and disseminated. For this very reason, Kurkihar has never been merely a place-name; it is, rather, a cultural sphere sustained collectively by faith, scholarship, and pilgrimage. Kurkihar truly entered the purview of modern scholarship following a serendipitous discovery in 1930. At that time, while excavating a mound of barren earth, local villagers unexpectedly unearthed a cache of gilt-bronze sculptures beneath the ruins of ancient red-brick structures. As subsequent archaeological work unfolded, a total of over two hundred metal sculptures were eventually recovered, ranging in height from a mere few centimeters to over 150 centimeters. These sculptures exhibited a stylistic unity yet displayed a high degree of artistic maturity; some even bore inscriptions. This discovery was subsequently hailed as one of the most significant finds in the history of Indian Buddhist archaeology since the excavations at Nalanda. It was at that very moment that scholars suddenly realized: situated between Bodh Gaya and Nalanda, there had once existed a center of sculptural production of extraordinary vibrancy. Regarding the etymology of the name "Kurkihar," scholarly opinion remains a subject of some debate. Markham Kittoe, the British archaeologist who first discovered Kurkihar, believed that the name likely derived from "Koorka Vihara," meaning "Koorka's Great Monastery." The Indian archaeologist Alexander Cunningham, however, proposed an alternative interpretation with stronger Buddhist connotations: he suggested it might originate from "Kukkuta-pada Vihara"—a name linked to the ancient Indian designation for "Chicken-Foot Mountain"—and thus could be understood as "The Great Monastery of Chicken-Foot Mountain." Although these two theories differ, they both point to the same core conclusion: this was no ordinary settlement, but rather a site intimately connected with monasticism, sacred geography, and traditions of pilgrimage. The clue regarding "Chicken-Foot Mountain" is particularly significant because, within the Buddhist tradition, it is no ordinary peak. According to scriptural accounts, just before entering *parinirvana* (final extinction), Shakyamuni Buddha entrusted his monastic robe and staff to the Venerable Mahakasyapa, instructing him to retreat to Chicken-Foot Mountain to enter deep meditation and await the advent of Maitreya—the Future Buddha—to whom he would then pass on these symbols of the Dharma lineage. Precisely for this reason, Chicken-Foot Mountain has always carried a profound significance within Buddhist history—a sense of the "past and future converging." Renowned Chinese monks such as Xuanzang and Yijing also visited this site during their westward journeys in search of the Dharma, leaving behind written records of their visits. These documents serve as more than mere geographical descriptions; they continually reinforce a fundamental truth: the region where Kurkihar is situated was not merely a peripheral zone of the Buddhist world, but a place deeply embedded within the collective memory and sacred geography of Buddhism. If we probe further, we arrive at an even more critical question: Why are there so few surviving examples of Kurkihar-style Buddhist statuary in existence today, and why do the majority of them bear the distinct traces of having been unearthed from the ground? Behind this phenomenon lies a history marked by abrupt rupture. By the early 13th century, with the expansion of Muslim power into northeastern India, the Pala cultural sphere—one of the last great strongholds of Buddhism in India—began to collapse. Monasteries were destroyed, monastic communities were dispersed, and the once-flourishing tradition of Buddhist iconography was abruptly severed. Kurkihar, naturally, could not escape this fate. In other words, the decline of the Kurkihar artistic style was not a process of natural decay; rather, it was a tradition suddenly buried beneath the earth amidst a period of massive historical upheaval. Precisely for this reason, the Kurkihar sculptures we encounter today—whether in museums or private collections, and regardless of whether they are made of stone or bronze—almost invariably bear the distinct patina of excavation. They were not naturally polished smooth through centuries of uninterrupted local veneration; rather, having been buried in the earth and lying in silent slumber for centuries, they were only rediscovered and brought back into human sight relatively recently. Consequently, the rare few works that have managed to survive in a "transmitted" state—that is, having remained above ground throughout their history—appear exceptionally rare. For such survival implies that these pieces must have been whisked away in the nick of time during that period of cultural rupture, subsequently continuing to be venerated, cherished, and preserved within entirely different cultural environments. We can now, at last, turn our attention to the specific piece before us. This is a seated figure of Shakyamuni, adorned with a jeweled crown. To my mind, its most captivating quality lies not in the individual splendor of its isolated details, but rather in the overwhelming sense of holistic harmony that strikes the viewer at first glance. Let us begin with the upper body. The jeweled crown takes the form of a tripartite tiara, inlaid with silver wire and gemstones; from the ends of the ribbons—which extend horizontally behind the ears—hang pendant ornaments likewise inlaid with gems; the necklace, too, is embellished with accents of silver and ruby. These adornments are remarkably intricate; yet, miraculously, they never appear cluttered or chaotic. Instead, they engage in a layered interplay of visual echoes, arranged with impeccable order and precision. Next, observe the face. The *urna* (the tuft of hair between the eyebrows) and the eyes, both inlaid with silver, instantly ignite a sense of divine presence; the lips, inlaid with red copper, lend the smile a vivid, lifelike quality. This complex interplay of color—arising from the interplay of silver, red copper, and gilded bronze—represents more than a mere variation in material composition; it constitutes a deliberate aesthetic arrangement. The silver imparts a sense of clarity and lucidity to the gaze; the red copper infuses the lips with vitality; while the gilded bronze envelops the entire visage in a glow that is at once warm and regal. Thus, the most moving aspect of this face is its ability to simultaneously uphold the dignity of a prince while never creating a sense of aloofness or distance from the human observer. It is noble, yet never cold; sacred, yet never rigid; it even possesses a subtle touch of lighthearted playfulness. The unwavering confidence of its direct, frontal gaze conveys not a sense of oppression, but rather that upright bearing and moral rectitude characteristic only of a youthful spirit. And it is precisely for this reason that this figure of Shakyamuni never appears distant or beyond our reach. It does not resemble a deity aloof and distant—one to be merely gazed up at from afar—but rather a prince whom you would willingly approach, and with whom you might even wish to converse. Looking further down, what truly reveals the hand of a master is neither the gemstones nor the settings, but rather the proportions. The treatment of the monastic robe is remarkably minimalist, merely suggested by a faint outline along its edges. Far from diminishing the physique, this approach actually enhances the body’s vigor and clarity. Simple lines delineating the drapery precisely articulate the undulation of the muscles and the fullness of the anatomical forms, rendering them both substantial and supple. The lotus pedestal below is equally magnificent. The rows of pearl-like beads—both above and below—are plump and full, while the central lotus petals are charged with tension, arranged in a symmetry so precise as to border on the exacting. Yet, this symmetry is not mechanical; rather, it is a harmony born of meticulous control. It echoes the complex yet orderly opulence of the upper body, ensuring that the entire piece maintains a unified rhythm and breath from head to toe. The greatest challenge of all, however, lies in the proportional relationship between the jeweled crown and the head itself. This is precisely where the inherent difficulty of the "Crowned Shakyamuni" motif lies: if the crown is even slightly too large, the figure appears top-heavy and unbalanced; if slightly too small, it looks cramped and petty. Yet, this particular piece captures that most subtle point of equilibrium with absolute precision. It is lavish, yet never ponderous; intricate, yet consistently light and airy. This is the true foundation of its profound sense of overall unity. Furthermore, the gemstones adorning the entire figure fall predominantly within the deep red spectrum, while the metal settings—mostly silver—harmonize with the tones of the eyes and lips. Having undergone oxidation, the silver has shed its original dazzling glare, blending seamlessly with the deep, steady reds to imbue the entire work with an air of nobility—one that is profoundly restrained, yet utterly unmistakable. This is not a nobility of ostentation, but rather a high-born elegance that has been fully internalized and refined through the lens of aesthetic mastery. At this juncture, a deeper question naturally arises: What manner of craftsman, in an era long past, could possibly have created such a work? In my view, first and foremost, this must have been the work of a master artisan. For from the initial modeling and casting to the final polishing and gem-setting, there is not a single aspect of this piece that betrays any lack of skill. Every stage of the process is executed with precision; every component is perfectly resolved and balanced. A level of finish such as this cannot be achieved through natural talent alone; it must be the product of an immense amount of practice and an exceptionally mature judgment. Yet, technical skill alone is not enough. What makes this particular piece truly rare is the holistic vision it embodies. That is to say, what the artisan perceived while working was not merely a collection of isolated parts, but rather an entire system—from start to finish, from crown to pedestal, from color palette to proportion, and from iconography to overall demeanor. Many works fail not because a specific detail is flawed, but because, despite every individual part being well-executed, they fail to coalesce into a coherent whole. Some artisans attempt to mask this deficiency with excessive ornamentation; however, such fragmented ostentation rarely withstands close scrutiny. True harmony cannot be achieved through mere accumulation. It can only emerge from an individual whose inner world possesses order and measure—someone capable of perceiving the whole while focusing on the part. Thus, I am inclined to believe that this artisan was not only technically masterful but also possessed an inner spirit of profound composure and expansiveness. For the harmony evident in a work of art is not merely the result of aesthetic training; it is often a direct reflection of the artist's character and their unique way of seeing the world. If we pursue this line of reasoning further, the true story behind this artwork begins to slowly come into focus. The artistic style of Bihar—the region where Kurkihar is located—differs significantly from that of Bengal. The Bengal style tends to be more dynamic and outwardly expressive, whereas the Bihar style is characterized by greater rectitude and nobility, placing a stronger emphasis on a sense of moral uprightness within a framework of order. This distinction is strikingly evident in this crowned figure of Shakyamuni. It eschews exaggerated movement or ostentatious displays of technical virtuosity; instead, all its energy is channeled into a composition defined by solemnity, rectitude, and balance. This particular aesthetic quality inevitably brings to mind the traditions of court art. For the aesthetic sensibilities of the royal court have never prioritized mere emotional appeal; rather, they value order, balance, a sense of hierarchy, and an impeccable decorum that admits no error. Moreover, this hypothesis is by no means unfounded. During the 2023 exhibition held at Zhujinju, a figure of Avalokiteśvara bearing an inscribed dedication was on display. Based on iconographic analysis, that piece was identified as a quintessential example of the 11th–12th century Bihar style—distinct from the artistic traditions of Bengal. The inscription’s general purport may be interpreted as: "Commissioned by an Indian patron to fulfill the devotional needs of a Tibetan patron." This inscription is of great significance. It demonstrates, at the very least, one crucial point: during that era, workshops in the Bihar region served not only local religious demands but also catered to cross-regional—and indeed, high-status—devotional requirements. The workshops capable of undertaking such commissions were, without question, far from ordinary. When considering this figure of the World-Honored Avalokiteśvara—alongside the overall caliber of other artworks from Bihar—and then turning back to examine the aesthetic sublimity of this Crowned Śākyamuni, we have ample grounds to surmise: it was almost certainly created under exacting standards by the era’s most elite workshops, specifically to fulfill high-level devotional needs. As to whether these high-level requirements stemmed from monastic institutions or from devotional practices closer to the royal sphere, it remains difficult to draw an absolute conclusion at this juncture; nevertheless, judging by its overall scale, technical perfection, and inherent aura, it is patently not a work of ordinary provenance. In that era, the names of artists were rarely preserved for posterity. Yet, the works they left behind speak volumes on behalf of their entire age. These sculptures serve as witnesses to history, as the crystallization of a distinct cultural ethos, and as a microcosm of how a specific era perceived the sacred, the ordered, and the noble. Thus, when we stand before this Crowned Śākyamuni, what we behold is far more than mere gemstones, silver filigree, gilded bronze, and a smile. What we truly perceive is the story of a region that flourished through its proximity to sacred sites; of a tradition that was eventually buried beneath the ruptures of history; and of a great craftsman who, within the span of a fleeting lifetime, succeeded in encapsulating the finest aesthetic sensibilities and spiritual essence of his age within a single sculpted form. Names may fade into oblivion. But truly great works of art will, in the end, live on in place of their creators.

excerpt.en: A Swat bronze of Maitreya with a lucid silhouette and inward poise.

viewingNote.zh: 斯瓦特谷地，位于今天巴基斯坦西北部，兴都库什山脉南麓。它表面上看是一处 山地谷地，实际上却处在古代印度次大陆、中亚与高原世界之间的交通节点上。 从地理位置来说，它既不是单纯的边缘，也不是封闭的腹地，而是一条真正意义 上的文化通道。 法显在《佛国记》中较早记录了这一地区，玄奘则在《大唐西域记》中将其记作 乌仗那国，并描绘出 7 世纪这里佛法兴盛、寺院众多的景象。这说明，至少到玄 奘时代，斯瓦特已经不是一个普通的区域地名，而是一处在佛教世界中被明确感 知、被反复朝向的地方。 那么，斯瓦特为什么能在佛教史上占据如此重要的位置？ 原因并不神秘，恰恰是几个非常现实的条件叠加后的结果：它有交通优势，有寺 院网络，有持续的僧侣往来，也有足够重要的宗教声望。早期它本属更广义的犍 陀罗文化圈；但随着犍陀罗核心区域逐渐衰落，佛教中心并没有消失，而是向更 稳定、寺院基础更扎实、交通仍然活跃的区域转移。斯瓦特，正是在这个过程中， 从“传播通道”慢慢变成了“地方中心”。换句话说，斯瓦特之所以重要，不只是因 为它曾经属于犍陀罗，而是因为它在犍陀罗之后，发展出了自己的佛教生命力。 说到这里，问题自然就来了：既然斯瓦特原本属于犍陀罗文化圈，那它为什么后 来会长成自己的样子？答案就在“延续”与“转化”之间。 斯瓦特的造像并不是凭空出现的。早期它当然继承了犍陀罗传统，但它又没有停 留在犍陀罗。随着区域中心的转移、本地工坊的成熟、赞助系统的稳定，以及宗 教环境的持续活跃，斯瓦特逐渐从“继承者”变成了“再创造者”。它在继承其雕塑 语言的基础上，开始发展出更地方化、更凝练，也更带有山地圣地气质的视觉体 系。 这也是为什么我们今天看到有些斯瓦特造像时，总会觉得它既熟悉又不同：熟悉 的是，仍能看见犍陀罗遗风；不同的是，它已经不再追求那种强叙事性的古典写 实，而是转向一种更静定、更内聚、更带有精神压强的形式。 此外，在后世佛教传统，尤其是藏传佛教文献中，乌仗那国常被视为与密教传统 关系极深的神圣地理。关于斯瓦特是否可以直接等同于密教的“发源地”，或者是 否能把某些宗教传记中的出生地叙述直接当作历史事实，这在学术上仍需保持谨 慎。但可以肯定的是：斯瓦特在后世佛教记忆中，早已不只是地理空间，更是一 种被神圣化了的文化空间。也正因为如此，这里出现弥勒造像，就不仅仅只是图 像学上的一尊菩萨，它往往还承载着人们对未来、对来世、对救度何时降临的深 层想象。 弥勒，是佛教里出现得很早的一种信仰对象。作为“未来佛”，他意味着尚未到来； 作为现世仍居菩萨位的存在，他又意味着一种仍在途中、仍与众生相连的状态。 这双重身份非常关键。因为它使弥勒既属于未来，又没有离开现在。他既是“还 未成佛者”，也是“已经被等待者”。也正因此，弥勒形象很早就在佛教图像中出 现。从犍陀罗与马图拉时期开始，弥勒就是最早被广泛图像化的菩萨之一。之后， 随着佛教思想向尼泊尔、中国、南亚和东亚各地传播，弥勒的图像也不断被重新 塑造。 说到这里，我们终于可以真正走近这件作品了。 先看头部。三叶宝冠、盘起的高髻、自然垂落的两缕发辫，都属于菩萨装的基本 系统。冠叶上的塔饰，则进一步提示出它与弥勒身份之间的关联。它脸型圆润， 接近鹅蛋，轮廓饱满，骨骼感被弱化，整体内收而沉静。另一方面，我觉得它又 是锋利的。长眉弯目，双眼嵌银，眼神清澈却带穿透力；鼻梁高挺，嘴唇饱满微 抿，口鼻线条收得极紧。 于是，这张脸就形成了一种很有张力的矛盾：柔和却不软弱、优雅却不温吞、不 怒，却天然带着威仪。这恰恰是斯瓦特成熟期造像常见的精神状态：不靠夸张的 表情制造神性，而是通过内收的脸型与利落的线条，让神性在克制中显现出来。 如果说脸部已经显出斯瓦特自己的成熟面貌，那么身体与衣纹，则也在进一步中， 将精神深化。弥勒右手施说法印，左手轻扶膝盖，肩头持物，游戏坐于莲座之上。 头部微抬，躯干直立，整个姿势既松弛，又稳定。这不是一种随意的松弛，而是 一种经过高度控制之后的“自在”。再看身体本身。匠人并没有执着于局部肌肉的 琐碎雕琢，而是更强调整体体积、块面关系与前后左右的空间转折。也就是说， 身体不是靠表面纹理成立的，而是靠内部结构成立的。 因此，这尊弥勒的壮健并不是通过炫耀性的肌肉来表现，而是通过整体块面的稳 定与重量感自然显出来。这就是为什么它看起来结实、有力，却不笨重。衣褶也 是如此。下身长裙的衣纹被处理得层层起伏，像水波一样翻卷，松弛且轻柔。 一件佛教雕塑，真正见高下的，往往也不只有脸，同样还有下方托举的结构部分。 弥勒的莲台，大叶舒展，边缘平滑，张力饱满。这种处理，带有鲜明的斯瓦特地 域性特征。它并不复杂，而是强调着一种展开后的稳定感。再往下，四方须弥座 呈现出一种近似山体的几何压缩感。通过粗壮、硬朗、山形起伏般的轮廓，使整 件作品在庄严秩序之中，生出了一种接近山岩与自然地貌的力量感。 于是，上方菩萨的自在、下方台座的硬朗，形成了一个非常漂亮的对照：上是松 弛中的精神高举，下是沉稳中的地气支撑。
viewingNote.en: The Swat Valley, situated in present-day northwestern Pakistan, lies at the southern foothills of the Hindu Kush mountain range. While ostensibly a mere mountain valley, it actually occupies a pivotal nexus of communication linking the ancient Indian subcontinent, Central Asia, and the world of the high plateaus. Geographically speaking, it is neither a mere periphery nor an isolated hinterland; rather, it serves as a cultural corridor in the truest sense of the term. Faxian provided one of the earliest written accounts of this region in his *Record of Buddhist Kingdoms* (Foguo Ji), while Xuanzang, in his *Records of the Western Regions of the Great Tang* (Da Tang Xiyu Ji), identified it as the Kingdom of Udyana and vividly depicted a 7th-century landscape where the Buddhist Dharma flourished and monasteries abounded. This demonstrates that, at least by the era of Xuanzang, Swat was no longer merely an ordinary regional place-name; it had evolved into a site explicitly recognized within the Buddhist world—a destination toward which pilgrims repeatedly gravitated. Why, then, was Swat able to occupy such a prominent position in the history of Buddhism? The reasons are not mysterious; they are, in fact, the result of the convergence of several highly pragmatic conditions: it possessed strategic advantages in transportation, an established network of monasteries, a steady flow of traveling monks, and a religious prestige of considerable magnitude. In its early stages, it belonged to the broader cultural sphere of Gandhara; however, as the core region of Gandhara gradually declined, the center of Buddhist activity did not vanish entirely. Instead, it shifted toward regions that offered greater stability, a more solid monastic infrastructure, and continued vitality in terms of transportation. It was precisely through this process that Swat slowly transformed from a mere "channel of transmission" into a "regional center" in its own right. In other words, Swat’s significance stems not merely from its historical affiliation with Gandhara, but—more importantly—from its ability to cultivate its own distinct Buddhist vitality in the aftermath of the Gandhara era. At this juncture, a natural question arises: given that Swat originally belonged to the Gandhara cultural sphere, how did it subsequently evolve to assume its own unique character? The answer lies in the interplay between "continuity" and "transformation." The Buddhist iconography of Swat did not emerge in a vacuum. In its early stages, it undoubtedly inherited the traditions of Gandhara; yet, it did not remain static within the Gandhara paradigm. Driven by the shifting of regional centers, the maturation of local workshops, the stabilization of patronage systems, and the sustained vibrancy of the religious environment, Swat gradually evolved from being a mere "inheritor" into a "re-creator." Building upon its inherited sculptural vocabulary, it began to evolve a visual system that was more localized, more distilled, and imbued with an even stronger aura of a sacred mountain sanctuary. This explains why, when we encounter certain Swat sculptures today, we often find them simultaneously familiar and distinct: familiar, in that traces of the Gandhara legacy remain visible; distinct, in that they no longer pursue the highly narrative, classical realism of the past, but have instead shifted toward a form that is more serene, more inwardly focused, and charged with a greater spiritual intensity. Furthermore, within later Buddhist traditions—particularly in Tibetan Buddhist literature—the Kingdom of Uddiyana is frequently regarded as a sacred geographical realm deeply intertwined with the traditions of Esoteric Buddhism. Whether Swat can be directly equated with the "birthplace" of Esoteric Buddhism—or whether the accounts of birthplaces found in certain religious biographies should be accepted as literal historical fact—remains a subject requiring scholarly caution. Yet, one point is certain: in the collective memory of later Buddhist traditions, Swat has long ceased to be merely a geographical location; it has evolved into a sacralized cultural space. Precisely for this reason, the appearance of a Maitreya image in this region signifies something far beyond a mere iconographical depiction of a Bodhisattva; it often serves as a vessel for profound human imaginings regarding the future, the afterlife, and the eventual advent of salvation. Maitreya is an object of faith that emerged very early within the Buddhist tradition. As the "Future Buddha," he signifies that which has not yet arrived; yet, as a being who currently resides in the station of a Bodhisattva within this present world, he signifies a state of being "still on the journey"—one that remains intimately connected with all sentient beings. This dual identity is of pivotal importance, for it positions Maitreya as a figure belonging simultaneously to the future and to the present moment. He is at once "one who has not yet attained Buddhahood" and "one who is already awaited." It is precisely for this reason that the image of Maitreya appeared in Buddhist iconography at such an early stage. Dating back to the Gandhara and Mathura periods, Maitreya was among the very first Bodhisattvas to be widely depicted in visual form. Subsequently, as Buddhist thought spread across Nepal, China, South Asia, and East Asia, the iconography of Maitreya continued to be reimagined and reshaped. Having reached this point, we can finally turn our attention directly to the artwork itself. Let us begin by examining the head. The three-lobed crown, the high coiled chignon, and the two braids falling naturally—all constitute the fundamental elements of traditional Bodhisattva attire. The miniature stupa ornament adorning the crown’s central leaf further underscores the figure’s specific identity as Maitreya. The face is rounded—approaching an oval or "goose-egg" shape—with full contours and softened bone structure; the overall impression is one of inward composure and tranquility. Yet, conversely, I perceive a certain sharpness within it. The long, arched brows frame crescent-shaped eyes inlaid with silver, conveying a gaze that is at once limpid and penetrating; the bridge of the nose is high and prominent, while the full lips are slightly pursed, rendering the lines of the mouth and nose remarkably taut and precise. Thus, this face embodies a tension-filled paradox: it is gentle yet never weak, elegant yet never languid; it displays no outward anger, yet naturally radiates an inherent majesty. This precisely captures the spiritual tenor characteristic of sculptures from the Swat Valley during its mature period—a style that eschews exaggerated facial expressions to manufacture divinity, opting instead to let that divinity emerge through restraint, manifested in the face’s inward-focused contours and crisp, incisive lines. If the face already reveals the mature aesthetic of the Swat Valley tradition, the treatment of the body and drapery serves to further deepen and enrich that spiritual expression. Maitreya’s right hand performs the *Dharmachakra Mudra* (the gesture of expounding the Dharma), while his left hand rests lightly upon his knee; bearing attributes upon his shoulders, he sits in a posture of "royal ease" atop a lotus pedestal. With his head tilted slightly upward and his torso held erect, the entire pose conveys a sense of both relaxation and stability. This is not a casual relaxation, but rather a state of "effortless ease" (*isvara*) achieved through a process of highly disciplined control. Turning to the body itself: the artisan has not fixated on the minute, fussy carving of individual muscles, but has instead emphasized overall volume, the interplay of planes, and the spatial transitions across the figure’s front, back, and sides. In essence, the body’s form is not established merely by surface textures, but is grounded in its underlying internal structure. Consequently, the physical vigor of this Maitreya figure is not expressed through a ostentatious display of musculature, but rather emerges naturally from the stability of its overall planes and its palpable sense of weight. This explains why the figure appears robust and powerful, yet never heavy or ponderous. The treatment of the drapery follows this same principle. The folds of the long skirt covering the lower body are rendered in undulating layers—rippling and curling like flowing water—conveying a sense of both looseness and ethereal lightness. In the realm of Buddhist sculpture, the true measure of artistic mastery often lies not solely in the rendering of the face, but equally in the structural elements—such as the pedestal—that support and anchor the figure from below. Maitreya’s lotus pedestal features broad, unfurled leaves with smooth edges and a palpable sense of fullness and tension. This stylistic treatment bears the distinctive regional characteristics of the Swat Valley. Far from being overly complex, it emphasizes a sense of stability achieved through expansion. Further down, the square Sumeru-style base conveys a sense of geometric compression, akin to the mass of a mountain. Through its robust, rugged contours—reminiscent of undulating mountain ridges—the entire sculpture, while maintaining a solemn order, radiates a powerful energy evocative of natural rock formations and the rugged landscape itself. Thus, the effortless grace of the Bodhisattva above stands in beautiful counterpoint to the rugged solidity of the pedestal below: the upper realm embodies a spiritual elevation born of relaxation, while the lower realm provides a grounded, earthy support rooted in steadfast stability.

excerpt.en: A gilt-bronze image of Indra animated by princely ease and tensile form.

viewingNote.zh: 一说到“天界主神”，很多人第一反应，往往是威严、庄重、不可接近。但眼前这 尊帝释天，却不是这样。它当然有神性，也有秩序感，可真正打动人的，反而不 是压迫，而是一种轻盈。它坐在那里，既不紧张，也不夸张，却让人一眼就感觉 到：这不是人间的身体，而是一种已经从沉重中抽离出来的存在。 那么，问题就来了：为什么帝释天，会被塑造成这样？ 帝释天并不是佛教凭空创造出来的神祇。祂原本来自更早的古印度神话传统，后 来被佛教吸收进自己的宇宙体系之中。在佛经里，祂经常出现在佛陀生命中的关 键时刻——佛陀诞生时、成道时、涅槃时，祂都会现身礼敬、护持佛法、守护僧 团。也就是说，帝释天并不是佛，但祂是站在佛法一侧的天界主神。祂有权柄， 有威仪，也有秩序的象征意义。祂的居所，在佛教宇宙观里非常明确：忉利天。 在佛教、印度教、耆那教等传统中，须弥山都被视为宇宙的中心。它不是现实中 的某一座山，而是一种宇宙结构的象征。在这座宇宙之山的顶端，是忉利天，也 就是帝释天统领三十三天神的地方；而四面则有四大天王，分守四方，护持人间 秩序。所以，帝释天的身份，不只是“神”，祂更是一种宇宙秩序的管理者。祂所 在的位置，决定了祂并不属于混乱、欲望和争夺最浓烈的底层世界，但祂也还没 有进入完全抽象、纯精神化的境界。这就决定了帝释天的造像，不能只做得威猛， 也不能做得过于虚无。祂必须保留一种“可亲近的超越感”。 而在现实世界中，冈仁波齐之所以会被多种宗教共同尊崇，正是因为它常常被看 作人间最接近宇宙中心的地方。对朝圣者来说，绕行圣山，不只是行走，更像是 在用身体接近宇宙的轴心。换句话说，须弥山虽然不可见，但人总在不断寻找它 在人间的投影。而帝释天，也正是这一宇宙结构中最具秩序意味的神祇之一。 在佛教世界观里，三界分为欲界、色界、无色界。忉利天属于欲界天。但“欲界” 并不等于粗鄙，也不等于混乱。它只是意味着：这里仍然保有形体、感官、宫殿、 音乐、花园、风与光。也就是说，帝释天虽然超出凡人世界，却仍然带着一种“有 形的天界生活”。这很重要。因为这意味着，帝释天不是一种抽象的概念，而是 一种仍然有气息、有姿态、有威仪、也有风度的生命形态。也正因如此，匠人在 塑造帝释天时，不会把祂做成护法神那种强压式的厚重；也不会把祂做成完全脱 离身体的飘忽。祂更适合呈现为一种已经脱离人间沉重，但仍保有生命温度的存 在。 这正是眼前这尊造像最迷人的地方。它的轻盈，并不是软弱，而是一种经过净化 后的力量感。这尊帝释天出自马拉王朝早期。和后期某些更强调体块厚度、量感 的作品不同，它并不靠“重”去证明自己的力量。相反，它选择的是另一条路：通 过紧实的身体、舒展的线条、灵动的细节，去表现一种由内而外散发出来的充盈。 它坐在莲台上，姿态放松，一手结说法印，一手握金刚杵撑住莲台。金刚杵在佛 教里象征不可摧毁的佛法，也象征神力、智慧与权威。所以这件作品一开始就建 立起了一个很有意思的结构：一边，是说法印的平和；另一边，是金刚杵的坚定。 一边像是在教化，一边像是在守护。于是，帝释天的身份就完整了：它不是单纯 的武力，也不是单纯的慈和，而是一种带着秩序感的守护力量。 如果再仔细看它的身体，会发现一个更细微的妙处。它并不是“摆出”一个优雅姿 势，而像是顺着身体自身的呼吸，自然地坐在那里。结印的手臂轻倚大腿，肩背 挺拔却不僵硬，整个人看起来没有一丝用力过猛的地方。正是这种“不用力”，最 能显出它的从容。真正高等级的神性，从来不是靠紧绷撑起来的。而是即便放松， 仍然站得住。 这件作品最值得反复看的一点，就是它对“体量”的处理。匠人并没有用夸张的大 块面去堆积神性，而是把力量藏进了肌肉的紧实、线条的收放和空间的留白里。 它的身体不是松散的，而是饱满的；不是瘦削的，而是有弹性的。但这种饱满并 不会把观者压住，反而会让目光在它身体周围自由流动。这就意味着，匠人真正 塑造的，并不是“重力中的身体”，而是一种仿佛带着空气感的身体。这种空气感 非常重要。让它看上去不像被固定在金属里，而像只是暂时停驻在这个空间之中。 那是一种近乎“仙气”的存在方式——不是虚无，而是轻；不是飘散，而是透。让 神性更像一种在空间中浮起的气息。 帝释天头戴高耸华丽的宝冠，微微侧首，耳边的带饰与耳环仿佛正在风中轻轻扬 起。这让雕塑虽然静止，却带出一种流动感。这点非常高明。因为真正的“空灵”， 从来不是没有东西，而是东西虽在，却始终带着一种将要飘起的趋势。它的脸庞 圆润柔和，带有马拉王朝时期常见的甜美气息。这种甜美并不削弱神性，恰恰相 反，它把天界主神从高高在上的距离中轻轻拉回到人能感知的范围里。于是，祂 既尊贵，又亲切；既有神的身份，又有人能靠近的温度。 再看身体。上半身呈倒三角形，肩膀宽阔，腰部自然内收，随着头部轻轻偏转， 整个躯干也生出一个柔和的弧度。这条弧线很关键。它使这件作品不再停留在正 面观看的端庄，而是生出一种有节奏的动态。腰间立体的衣结、胸前的禅修带、 腿上的飘带，全都不是直直垂下，而是带着柔软的弯曲。这些细节一点一点把风 的感觉做了出来。所以，这尊帝释天最动人的地方，也许不是某一个局部，而是 整件作品都像被一阵看不见的风穿过。那不是现实中的风，而是一种属于天界的 轻清之气。 我常常喜欢用“意味”来形容一尊雕塑。因为真正打动人的，从来不只是外表。雕 塑的意味，是形态、比例、姿态、材质、空间和气质共同生成的一种精神氛围。 它看不见，却能被感到。这尊帝释天最核心的意味，就是：空灵。 不压迫，不逼迫，不震慑。它更像一阵风，一抹光，一种停驻在空间里的轻。 这种轻，不只是形式上的，它还带着一种更深的象征意味——那就是对沉重的超 越。而当我们站在它面前时，这种空灵会一点点感染我们。你会觉得呼吸变轻了， 心也慢了。它以一种不动声色的轻，把我们们带进了宁静里。 这，也正是这件帝释天最难得的地方。它不是一件只是“塑造得很美”的金属作品， 而是借由形体，把一种本来看不见的状态——一种属于天界的自在，做成了可以 被身体直接感受到的存在。
viewingNote.en: When the phrase "Lord of the Celestial Realm" is mentioned, many people’s immediate reaction is often one of majesty, solemnity, and inaccessibility. Yet, the figure of Indra (Di Shitian) standing before us here is quite different. While he certainly possesses divinity and embodies a sense of order, what truly moves the observer is not a sense of oppression, but rather a quality of lightness. Seated there—neither tense nor ostentatious—he conveys, at a single glance, the distinct impression that this is no mortal body, but an existence that has already transcended the weight of the material world. This raises a compelling question: Why was Indra sculpted in this particular manner? Indra is not a deity conjured out of thin air by Buddhism; rather, he originates from the much older mythological traditions of ancient India, having subsequently been assimilated into the Buddhist cosmological framework. In the Buddhist scriptures, he frequently appears at pivotal moments in the Buddha’s life: at the moment of the Buddha’s birth, his enlightenment, and his entry into Nirvana, Indra manifests to offer reverence, uphold the Dharma, and safeguard the monastic community. In essence, while Indra is not a Buddha himself, he stands as a celestial sovereign aligned with the cause of the Dharma. He possesses authority, majesty, and serves as a symbolic embodiment of cosmic order. Within the Buddhist cosmology, his abode is clearly defined: the Trayastriṃśa Heaven. In traditions such as Buddhism, Hinduism, and Jainism, Mount Sumeru is universally regarded as the center of the universe. It is not a specific geographical mountain found in the physical world, but rather a symbol of cosmic structure. At the very apex of this cosmic mountain lies the Trayastriṃśa Heaven—the realm where Indra reigns supreme over the thirty-three celestial deities—while the four cardinal directions are guarded by the Four Heavenly Kings, who stand watch to uphold order within the human realm. Thus, Indra’s identity transcends that of a mere "deity"; he functions, more precisely, as the steward of cosmic order. His designated position dictates that he belongs neither to the lower realms—where chaos, desire, and strife rage most intensely—nor has he yet ascended to a state of pure abstraction and absolute spirituality. Consequently, the iconography of Indra must strike a delicate balance: his statues cannot be rendered solely as fierce and formidable, nor can they be depicted as overly ethereal or void of substance. He must embody a "transcendence that remains approachable." In the physical world, the reason Mount Kailash is revered across such a diverse array of religious traditions is precisely because it is frequently perceived as the place on Earth that lies closest to the very center of the universe. For pilgrims, circumambulating a sacred mountain is not merely an act of walking; rather, it resembles an endeavor to physically draw near to the very axis of the cosmos. In other words, although Mount Sumeru itself remains invisible, humanity ceaselessly seeks its terrestrial manifestation—its projection onto the human realm. And within this cosmic structure, Indra stands as one of the deities most profoundly emblematic of order. In the Buddhist worldview, the Three Realms are categorized into the Realm of Desire, the Realm of Form, and the Realm of Formlessness. The Trayastriṃśa Heaven—Indra’s abode—belongs to the Realm of Desire. Yet, the "Realm of Desire" is by no means synonymous with coarseness or chaos; it simply signifies a sphere where physical bodies, sensory faculties, palaces, music, gardens, wind, and light still persist. In essence, although Indra transcends the mundane human world, he nonetheless embodies a form of "tangible celestial existence." This distinction is crucial, for it implies that Indra is not a mere abstract concept, but rather a living entity possessing breath, posture, majesty, and grace. Precisely for this reason, when artisans sculpt Indra, they refrain from rendering him with the imposing, heavy-handed intensity typical of Guardian Deities; nor do they depict him as a wispy, ethereal presence completely detached from the physical form. Instead, he is best portrayed as an entity that has shed the burdens of the human world while still retaining the warmth and vitality of life. This is precisely the most captivating quality of the statue before us. Its lightness is not a sign of frailty, but rather a sense of power that has undergone a process of purification. This particular depiction of Indra dates back to the early period of the Malla Dynasty. Unlike certain later works—which tend to emphasize sheer mass, volume, and physical weight—this piece does not rely on "heaviness" to assert its strength. On the contrary, it pursues a different path: utilizing a taut, compact physique, fluid lines, and intricate details to convey a sense of fullness and vitality that radiates outward from within. Seated upon a lotus pedestal, he assumes a relaxed posture; one hand forms the *Dharmachakra Mudra* (the gesture of expounding the Dharma), while the other grasps a *Vajra* (thunderbolt scepter) to steady himself against the pedestal. In Buddhist iconography, the *Vajra* symbolizes the indestructible nature of the Dharma itself, as well as divine power, wisdom, and authority. Thus, from its very inception, this artwork establishes a fascinating dynamic: on one side lies the serenity of the *Dharmachakra Mudra*; on the other, the steadfastness of the *Vajra*. One side appears to be instructing and enlightening, while the other stands guard in protection. Thus, the identity of Indra is fully realized: he represents neither mere brute force nor simple benevolence, but rather a protective power imbued with a profound sense of order. A closer examination of his physique reveals an even more subtle nuance. He does not merely "strike" an elegant pose; rather, he appears to be seated naturally, as if simply following the rhythm of his own breath. His arms, forming a ritual *mudra*, rest lightly against his thighs; his shoulders and back are erect yet never rigid, conveying an overall impression utterly devoid of any strained effort. It is precisely this absence of strain—this "effortlessness"—that most vividly manifests his composure. True divinity of the highest order is never propped up by tension; rather, it remains steadfast and poised even in a state of complete relaxation. The most compelling aspect of this masterpiece—one that invites repeated contemplation—is its masterful treatment of "volume." The artisan did not attempt to construct divinity through the accumulation of exaggerated, massive forms; instead, he concealed that power within the tautness of the musculature, the interplay of expanding and contracting lines, and the strategic use of negative space. His physique is not flaccid, but full-bodied; not gaunt, but resilient and supple. Yet, this fullness does not overwhelm the viewer; on the contrary, it allows the gaze to flow freely around his form. This implies that what the artisan truly sculpted was not a "body subject to gravity," but rather a body imbued with an ethereal, almost airy quality. This sense of "airiness" is crucial; it ensures that the figure does not appear to be rigidly anchored within the metal medium, but rather as if merely pausing momentarily within this space. It represents a mode of existence bordering on the "celestial"—not a state of void, but of lightness; not of dissipation, but of translucence. It allows the divine essence to manifest less as a solid object, and more as an ethereal presence hovering within the spatial expanse. Indra wears a towering, magnificent jeweled crown; his head is tilted ever so slightly to the side, while the ribbons and earrings adorning his ears appear to flutter gently in an unseen breeze. This imbues the sculpture—though physically static—with a profound sense of fluidity. It is a stroke of sheer genius, for true "ethereality" is never defined by an absence of substance; rather, it is the quality wherein substance remains present, yet perpetually carries the latent potential to take flight. His countenance is rounded and gentle, radiating that sweet, serene aura characteristic of the Malla Dynasty period. This sweetness does not diminish the divine nature; quite the contrary, it gently draws the celestial sovereign—typically aloof and distant—back into a realm perceptible to human senses. Thus, He appears simultaneously noble and approachable—possessing the stature of a deity, yet radiating a warmth that invites human proximity. Next, consider the physique. The upper torso forms an inverted triangle, characterized by broad shoulders and a waist that tapers naturally inward; as the head tilts ever so slightly, the entire torso responds with a gentle, flowing curve. This specific curve is pivotal; it elevates the piece beyond mere frontal stateliness, infusing it instead with a rhythmic sense of motion. The three-dimensional knots at the waist, the meditation sash across the chest, and the fluttering ribbons adorning the legs—none hang in rigid vertical lines; rather, each is rendered with a soft, graceful curvature. These details, bit by bit, evoke the sensation of a breeze. Consequently, the most captivating aspect of this particular Indra figure may not lie in any single isolated detail, but rather in the impression that the entire sculpture has been swept through by an invisible wind—not a wind of the physical world, but rather a light, ethereal breath belonging to the celestial realm. I often find myself using the term "resonance" to describe a sculpture, for what truly moves the human spirit is never merely outward appearance. The resonance of a sculpture is a spiritual atmosphere born from the interplay of form, proportion, posture, material, space, and temperament. It is invisible, yet palpable. The core resonance of this Indra figure can be distilled into a single word: *ethereality*. It neither oppresses, nor imposes, nor overawes. It resembles, instead, a breeze, a glimmer of light, or a sense of lightness suspended within space. This lightness is not merely formal; it carries a deeper symbolic significance—namely, the transcendence of all that is heavy and burdensome. As we stand before it, this ethereality gradually permeates our own being. We feel our breathing grow lighter, our heartbeats slow. With a quiet, understated lightness, it draws us into a state of profound serenity. This, indeed, is the most exceptional quality of this Indra sculpture. It is not merely a metal artifact that has been "beautifully sculpted"; rather, through the medium of physical form, it transforms an inherently invisible state—a celestial sense of unencumbered freedom—into a tangible presence that can be directly felt and experienced by the human body.

label.zh: John Adam Stainton（1921-1991）舊藏， 贈予Aberdeen and Hindus六世伯爵及伯爵夫人為禮物，之後由其後人繼承。
label.en: Formerly in the collection of John Adam Stainton (1921–1991), gifted to the 6th Earl and Countess of Aberdeen, later inherited by their descendants.

excerpt.en: A 13th-century thangka structured through paired presence, symmetry, and chromatic depth.

viewingNote.zh: 有些唐卡如同雕塑，第一眼抓住人的，不是题材，而是气息。这幅画就是如此。 大片沉着而鲜亮的红色，浓密却不滞重；繁复的装饰、层层叠叠的人物、密而不 乱的结构，又让画面像一座被折叠起来的精神建筑，你会很自然地被它吸引。 9 世纪中叶以后，吐蕃王朝崩解，中央权力瓦解，卫藏地区原有的大规模寺院支 持系统也随之衰退。但佛法并没有因此从高原彻底消失。相反，它开始在边缘地 带重新积蓄力量。这时，西藏西部的阿里地区，成了一个关键支点。吐蕃后裔在 这里建立地方政权，尤其是古格王朝，对佛教表现出极强的护持姿态。在这样的 政治背景下，西藏西部不再只是高原边缘，而逐渐变成了后弘期最重要的文化转 运地之一。也正是在这一时期，据传统记载，仁钦桑布往返克什米尔求法，并在 寺院营建与图像制作中大力引入了克什米尔工艺体系。因此，西藏西部早期艺术 的底色，首先是克什米尔式的。这是这片土地最早长出来的一层审美骨架。 但历史没有停在这里。 11 世纪以后，随着印度高僧、翻译系统、卷轴绘画和经典文本不断进入高原腹 地，东印度帕拉系统的图像语言也开始向卫藏和藏西扩散。到了 13 世纪，尼泊 尔艺术又以更鲜明的装饰性、更细腻的线条趣味和更成熟的卷草、摩羯、缯带等 程式，继续进入西藏的视觉世界。于是，一个非常关键的局面出现了：西藏西部 不再只是接受某一种外来风格，而开始把不同来源的视觉传统重新编排，长出自 己的面貌。这幅上师对坐唐卡，正是在这样的背景中诞生的。它不是单一传统的 复制品，而是一幅只有在多重文化交汇之后，才可能出现的作品。 在藏传佛教绘画里，上师题材从来不是边缘性的。因为上师不是普通人物，而是 法脉的承接者、教法的传递者，也是佛与众生之间最具体的桥梁。佛、菩萨当然 代表终极的觉悟；但对修行者来说，真正使佛法落到现实中的，往往不是抽象的 教义，而是具体的人——那位教他、引他、带他走进法门的人。所以，上师肖像 并不仅只是“纪念画像”。它更像是一种被宗教化了的在场：人在上师圆寂之后， 仍然试图借由图像，让传承继续可见，让师徒之间的关系继续被保留下来。也正 因为如此，上师唐卡的重点，从来不只是像不像。它还要同时完成两件事：一是 让人物具有真实存在过的可信感，二是让这位具体的人，被安放进更大的法脉秩 序之中。这幅画，做的正是这件事。 如果只把这幅画当作两个人面对面坐着的肖像画来看，它就太简单了。真正高明 的地方，在于它通过构图，把“对谈”变成了“传承的显形”。画面最上层，以棋格 式分段方式安排了多排神灵、喇嘛、供养人等形象。佛、菩萨、祖师、成就者和 供养者共处于同一视觉秩序中。这不是热闹，也不是单纯为了丰富画面，而是在 告诉我们：这两位上师的对坐，并不是私人的交谈，而是发生在一条完整法脉之 中的精神事件。 再往下看，半圆形背光上方的岩洞式空间里，又安置了多位不同身份的人物：祖 师、护法、本尊、成就者。这种处理方式非常值得注意。它不是简单地把人物往 上堆，而是在画面内部制造出一种“由人间而法界”的层层递进。于是，中央两位 上师所在的空间，就不再是一个普通场景，而更像是一处被传承、护法、本尊共 同包围起来的法性空间。也就是说，这幅画真正要画的，不只是“谁在对谈”， 而是：这场对谈，发生在什么样的宇宙秩序之中。 如果继续追问，就会来到很关键的部分：这幅画为什么会显得这样复杂、这样丰 沛，却又始终予人聚气和整体感而没有散掉？ 答案在于，它把几股不同的艺术力量，压进了同一个系统里。 先看人物本身。两位上师的面容、眉眼、鼻梁与嘴部关系，线条收得很稳，神情 温和却不松散。那种略带内敛的优雅、细劲的笔触、微微带着古典意味的神韵， 明显和东印度帕拉绘画传统有关。也就是说，画中人物的“神”，首先来自一种成 熟的帕拉式人物语言。但仅有帕拉，不会长成今天这样。再看六拏具上方的摩羯、 旋转的卷草、衣饰边缘的装饰节奏、花纹的细密与流动，你会发现这里又有很强 的尼泊尔趣味。尤其是卷草尾端的翻转、装饰性线条的灵动感，都说明这幅画已 经吸收了尼泊尔绘画的视觉语汇。这种吸收并不是表层点缀，而是渗透进了整幅 画的呼吸里。 而真正让它成为“西藏西部绘画”的，则是它的整体组织方式。无论是多层宇宙式 的布局，还是中央人物的突出方式，还是上中下之间严密而清晰的秩序，都带着 西藏西部绘画特有的结构意识。它不像单纯的印度绘画那样更多依赖人物之间的 自然展开，也不像后来某些卫藏唐卡那样更强调教派标准化图式，它更像是在一 个尚未完全定型、却极其有创造力的时刻，把不同来源的语言压成一个新的整体。 所以，这幅画因为它“融合了帕拉和尼泊尔元素”，让我们看见：西藏西部并不是 被动接受，而是在主动重组。 说到这里，另一个问题就出现了：画中的两位上师，究竟是谁？有学者曾倾向将 这幅作品放入噶举系统来理解。这是一个有分量的判断。但如果只根据目前能看 到的图像特征来看，这个结论仍然不是完全没有讨论空间。尤其是右侧人物的俗 家形象，非常值得注意。因为在藏传佛教图像中，俗家上师并不只是一个服装差 别，它往往还牵连到具体传承身份的判断。若结合若干已知图像材料与人物特征 来看，把右侧人物理解为仲敦巴系统中的祖师形象，并不是毫无可能。因此，这 幅画被理解为噶当系统的上师对坐，也有其逻辑。但仍需更多同类作品和题记材 料互相印证。 很多人看唐卡，最容易先被细节吸引。这幅画当然也经得起细看——上师衣饰上 的花卉纹样像金线刺绣般繁复，繁而不乱。线条穿插有节奏，色彩浓重却不打架。 红、蓝、金、绿被组织得非常严密，像一张已经被调好音的乐谱。所以它不是色 彩越多越热闹，而是越看越能感到一种内在的统率力。五彩双层仰覆式莲台，下 方四狮两象的配置，也非常关键。这类组合有明显的帕拉图像来源，但被移植进 西藏西部之后，不再只是装饰部件，而成了稳定整个构图重心的基座。同样值得 注意的，还有最下层那十六位舞动的天女。她们并不是附属热闹，而是在宇宙秩 序中承担方位意义。四位一组，共分四方，这使整幅画从上到下，不再只是人物 堆叠，而变成了一个方向明确、中心稳定的世界。 同样，这幅画背面的内容，也很关键。背面塔内写有多组咒语与经文：莲花生心 咒、普巴金刚、文殊、观音六字真言、金刚手菩萨咒，以及“ye dharma hetu...” 等佛教偈语，还有《波罗提木叉经》的摘录。这说明了什么？首先，它说明这幅 画绝不只是“画完就结束”的观看对象。它是一件被被纳入修法和供养体系的法物。 其次，这样的咒语组合，也提示出它并非只服务于单一、狭窄的图像功能，而是 处在一个更复合的修法背景中。也就是说，这幅画既是肖像，也是传承图；既能 被观看，也能被加持；既属于绘画，也属于仪轨现场。 所以，背面的文字并不是附属说明。它们是在告诉我们：这幅画并不只活在正面， 它从里到外，都是被实践激活的。同样，画面的整体保存状态而言，在历经了近 700 年时光里，每一位护持者对其都极为珍视和爱护。 当把这些层层展开后，再站到这一副画前时：上师不是在说话，而是在传递。不 是在叙述，而是在延续。它让一场本来无声的对坐，长成了一幅关于法脉、历史、 智慧与记忆的图卷。我们真正看到的，是一个时代如何通过绘画，把自己的信念、 秩序与精神结构保存下来。而那些名字未必可考的画工，也正是在一笔一线之间， 替整个时代，留下了它最深的神情。
viewingNote.en: Some thangkas resemble sculptures; what captures the viewer’s attention at first glance is not the subject matter itself, but rather the palpable aura it exudes. This painting is precisely such a work. Expansive fields of color—simultaneously subdued and vivid red—appear dense yet never heavy or stagnant. The intricate ornamentation, the layers upon layers of figures, and the composition—dense yet never chaotic—render the image akin to a spiritual edifice, folded and condensed; one is drawn to it quite naturally. Following the mid-9th century, the Tibetan Empire disintegrated and central authority collapsed; consequently, the extensive monastic support systems that had previously flourished in the Ü-Tsang region also fell into decline. Yet, the Dharma did not vanish entirely from the plateau as a result. On the contrary, it began to gather strength anew in the peripheral regions. During this period, the Ngari region in western Xizang emerged as a pivotal nexus. Here, descendants of the Tibetan Empire established local polities—most notably the Guge Kingdom—demonstrating an unwavering commitment to the patronage and protection of Buddhism. Against this political backdrop, western Xizang ceased to be merely a fringe territory of the plateau, gradually evolving into one of the most critical cultural conduits of the "Later Propagation" period. It was also during this era—according to traditional accounts—that Rinchen Zangpo undertook multiple journeys to Kashmir in search of the Dharma, subsequently introducing the Kashmiri artistic and architectural traditions into the construction of monasteries and the creation of religious imagery. Thus, the foundational aesthetic of early western Tibetan art was, first and foremost, Kashmiri in character—forming the very first aesthetic framework to take root in this land. However, the course of history did not halt there. After the 11th century—as eminent Indian masters, translation systems, scroll paintings, and canonical texts flowed continuously into the heartland of the plateau—the visual idiom of the East Indian Pala school began to diffuse across the Ü-Tsang and western Tibetan regions. By the 13th century, Nepalese art further penetrated Xizang’s visual landscape, distinguished by its heightened decorative flair, its refined linear sensibilities, and its sophisticated application of stylistic conventions—such as scrolling foliage, *makara* motifs, and fluttering ribbons. Consequently, a pivotal moment emerged: western Xizang ceased to be a mere passive recipient of a single foreign style; instead, it began to synthesize and reconfigure visual traditions drawn from diverse sources, thereby forging a distinct artistic identity of its own. It is against this very backdrop that this thangka—depicting two spiritual masters seated in dialogue—came into being. It is not merely a replica of a single tradition; rather, it is a work that could only emerge from the convergence of multiple cultures. In Tibetan Buddhist painting, the subject of the Guru is never peripheral. This is because the Guru is no ordinary figure; he is the inheritor of the Dharma lineage, the conveyor of the teachings, and the most tangible bridge between the Buddha and sentient beings. While Buddhas and Bodhisattvas naturally represent ultimate enlightenment, for the practitioner, what truly grounds the Dharma in reality is often not abstract doctrine, but a concrete human being—the very person who teaches, guides, and leads them into the spiritual path. Thus, a portrait of a Guru is not merely a "commemorative image." It functions more as a sacralized form of presence: even after the Guru has passed into *parinirvana*, the disciple seeks—through the medium of imagery—to ensure that the lineage remains visible and that the bond between master and student is preserved. Precisely for this reason, the primary focus of a Guru *thangka* is never simply a matter of physical likeness. It must simultaneously accomplish two objectives: first, to imbue the figure with a credible sense of having truly existed; and second, to situate this specific individual within the broader cosmic order of the Dharma lineage. This painting achieves precisely that. To view this work merely as a portrait depicting two individuals seated face-to-face would be to oversimplify it. Its true brilliance lies in how, through its composition, it transforms a "dialogue" into the "manifestation of a lineage." Across the uppermost section of the painting, rows of deities, lamas, and patrons are arranged in a grid-like, segmented pattern. Buddhas, Bodhisattvas, patriarchs, realized masters, and patrons all coexist within a single visual hierarchy. This arrangement is neither mere visual bustle nor a simple attempt to enrich the composition; rather, it conveys a profound message: the seated dialogue between these two Gurus is not a private conversation, but a spiritual event unfolding within the context of an unbroken Dharma lineage. Moving downward, within the cave-like space situated above the semi-circular aureole, a host of figures representing various spiritual roles are further enshrined: patriarchs, Dharma protectors, meditational deities (*Yidams*), and realized masters. This compositional approach is particularly noteworthy. It does not merely stack figures vertically; instead, it creates—within the visual fabric of the painting itself—a layered progression ascending "from the human realm toward the Dharma Realm." Consequently, the space occupied by the two central Gurus ceases to be a mere ordinary setting; rather, it resembles a realm of *Dharmata*—the true nature of reality—collectively enveloped by lineages, Dharma protectors, and meditational deities. In other words, the true subject of this painting is not simply "who is conversing," but rather: within what cosmic order does this conversation take place? If one probes deeper, one arrives at a pivotal point: Why does this painting appear so complex and so rich in detail, yet consistently convey a sense of concentrated energy and holistic unity, never fragmenting into disarray? The answer lies in the fact that it has compressed several distinct artistic forces into a single cohesive system. First, consider the figures themselves. The faces of the two Gurus—their brows, eyes, nose bridges, and the interplay of their mouths—are delineated with remarkably steady lines; their expressions are gentle yet never slack. That quality of slightly understated elegance, those fine and vigorous brushstrokes, and that subtle aura imbued with a classical sensibility clearly link this work to the East Indian Pala painting tradition. In short, the "spirit" (*shen*) of the figures in this painting derives, first and foremost, from a mature visual vocabulary rooted in the Pala style. Yet, the Pala influence alone would not have resulted in the work we see today. Now, turn your gaze to the *Makaras* (mythical sea creatures) surmounting the *Six Ornaments* (*Shadanga*), the swirling foliate scrolls, the rhythmic ornamentation along the hems of the garments, and the intricate, fluid patterns; here, you will discover a distinct Nepalese aesthetic. In particular, the curling tips of the foliate scrolls and the dynamic vitality of the decorative lines demonstrate that this painting has fully assimilated the visual vocabulary of Nepalese art. This assimilation is not merely a superficial embellishment; rather, it has permeated the very "breath" of the entire painting. However, what truly defines this work as a specimen of "Western Tibetan painting" is its overall organizational structure. Whether in its multi-layered, cosmic layout, the manner in which the central figures are given prominence, or the rigorous yet lucid hierarchical order spanning the upper, middle, and lower registers—every element bears the distinctive structural consciousness characteristic of Western Tibetan art. Unlike purely Indian painting, which tends to rely more on the organic interplay and natural arrangement of figures, and unlike certain later *Thangkas* from the Ü-Tsang region, which emphasize standardized sectarian iconography, this painting appears to have emerged during a moment that was not yet fully codified—yet was profoundly creative—a moment in which artistic languages ​​from disparate sources were fused into a singular, new entity. Thus, precisely because this painting "fuses elements from both the Pala and Nepalese traditions," it reveals to us that Western Xizang was not merely a passive recipient of external influences, but was actively engaged in their reconfiguration. At this juncture, another question arises: Who, exactly, are the two masters depicted in this painting? Some scholars have tended to interpret this work within the context of the Kagyu lineage. This is a weighty assessment; however, if we base our judgment solely on the visual characteristics currently observable, this conclusion remains open to debate. The secular appearance of the figure on the right, in particular, warrants close attention. In the iconography of Tibetan Buddhism, the depiction of a master in secular attire is not merely a matter of sartorial distinction; it often carries implications regarding the identification of a specific lineage identity. When examining this piece in conjunction with other known iconographic materials and the specific physical attributes of the figures, interpreting the figure on the right as a patriarch within the Dromtönpa lineage does not appear to be an implausible hypothesis. Consequently, interpreting this painting as a depiction of two masters from the Kadampa lineage seated in dialogue also possesses its own logical validity. Nevertheless, further corroboration through the examination of additional comparable artworks and textual inscriptions remains necessary. When viewing Thangkas, many people are instinctively drawn first to the intricate details. This painting, too, certainly rewards close scrutiny: the floral motifs adorning the masters' robes are as intricate as gold-thread embroidery—complex, yet never chaotic. The interplay of lines possesses a rhythmic flow, while the rich, saturated colors harmonize rather than clash. The hues of red, blue, gold, and green are organized with meticulous precision, akin to a musical score that has been perfectly tuned. Thus, the painting does not derive its vibrancy merely from a profusion of color; rather, the more one gazes upon it, the more one senses a profound, underlying sense of compositional unity. The multi-hued, double-tiered lotus pedestals—featuring an arrangement of four lions and two elephants at the base—also play a pivotal role. While this specific combination clearly traces its iconographic origins to the Pala tradition, its transplantation into the context of Western Xizang transformed it from a mere decorative element into a foundational plinth that anchors and stabilizes the entire composition. Equally noteworthy are the sixteen dancing celestial maidens depicted along the lowest register. Far from serving merely as decorative embellishments to enliven the scene, they fulfill a specific spatial function within the cosmic order. Arranged in four groups of four, corresponding to the four cardinal directions, they transform the entire painting—from top to bottom—from a mere vertical stacking of figures into a fully realized world characterized by clear spatial orientation and a stable, centered core. Likewise, the content inscribed on the reverse side of this painting holds equally critical significance. Inscribed within the tower on the reverse side are various sets of mantras and scriptures: the Heart Mantra of Padmasambhava, mantras of Vajrakilaya, Manjushri, the Six-Syllable Mantra of Avalokiteshvara, and the mantra of Vajrapani, alongside Buddhist verses such as "Ye dharma hetu..." and excerpts from the *Pratimoksha Sutra*. What does this signify? First, it demonstrates that this painting is by no means merely an object of visual contemplation—something to be cast aside once the act of painting is complete. Rather, it is a ritual object, fully integrated into a system of spiritual practice and devotional offering. Second, this specific combination of mantras suggests that the painting does not serve a singular, narrow iconographic function; instead, it exists within a more complex and multifaceted ritual context. In other words, this painting functions simultaneously as a portrait and a lineage chart; it is an object to be viewed, yet also a vessel for spiritual blessings; it belongs to the realm of fine art, yet is equally an integral part of the ritual space in which it is utilized. Thus, the inscriptions on the reverse side are not merely supplementary annotations. They serve to remind us that this painting does not exist solely on its surface; from its innermost core to its outermost layer, it is a work activated through spiritual practice. Furthermore, judging by the painting’s overall state of preservation—having endured nearly seven centuries of time—it is evident that every guardian throughout its history has cherished and safeguarded it with the utmost care. When we peel back these layers of meaning and stand once more before this painting, we perceive that the Guru depicted within is not merely speaking, but transmitting; not merely narrating, but perpetuating. The work transforms what would otherwise be a silent, seated encounter into a sweeping visual scroll—a tapestry woven from threads of spiritual lineage, history, wisdom, and memory. What we truly witness here is how an entire era, through the medium of painting, succeeded in preserving its core beliefs, social order, and spiritual architecture. And those painters—whose names may now be lost to history—were, through every brushstroke and every line, capturing and preserving for their entire age its most profound and enduring spirit.

excerpt.en: A quietly forceful Central Xizang Buddha of restrained elegance.

viewingNote.zh: 初看之下，这尊来自西藏中部的释迦造像，似乎并没有脱离早期高原佛像的一般 范式：发髻高耸，眉眼细长，体态宽阔，鎏金尚存，神情安稳。但真正与它长时 间相对之后，会发现它最动人的地方，并不在于某一个局部的精巧，而在于一种 整体性的气息——一种难以被立即说清，却又持续向外弥散的感受。它像雾，也 像风；像某种无边的“在场”，却又不以具体形状限定自身。若一定要为这种感受 命名，我更愿意称之为：虚空感。 这里所说的“虚空”，并不是虚无，更不是空洞。它指向的是一种没有边界、没有 执著、却能容纳一切的存在状态。而问题也正是从这里开始：一尊金铜造像，为 什么会让人感到“虚空”？要回答这个问题，不能只谈抽象的宗教观念，也不能只 停留在审美直觉上。更有效的方式，是回到它的诞生：这尊像是如何被造出来的？ 它为何长成这个样子？它经历了怎样的流转与供养？又为何在今天仍能维持如 此强的精神张力？ 这尊释迦最先引人注意的，是它明显带有一种不属于高原本土初始阶段的柔性气 息：高高的发髻、细长而略微上挑的眉眼、嘴角那抹似笑非笑的神情，以及衣缘 轻轻外扬的走势，都呈现出一种流动性的节奏。这样的处理方式，并不是单纯的 地方趣味，而与 11 世纪前后藏地的帕拉艺术系统有密切关系。帕拉风格本属东 印度佛教晚期的重要视觉传统。随着后弘期佛教重建，印度高僧入藏、经典流通、 图像范本传播，以及东印度政治格局变化带来的人才与艺术资源流动，这种风格 逐渐进入高原腹地。尤其是在阿底峡入藏之后，东印度佛教的教法、图像和审美 趣味都更深地卷入了西藏的宗教生活。因此，这尊造像身上的帕拉特征，不应被 理解为某种“外来装饰”，而是 11 世纪高原佛教重组过程中，一个重要视觉语言 的进入与本地化结果。也就是说，这尊释迦并不是“长得像帕拉”，而是诞生于一 个帕拉样式已经开始参与塑造西藏佛像面貌的时代。它是一段跨地域艺术交流在 高原落地之后的形成。 在为这件作品配置底座时，我请来了一位长期合作的成都金工匠人。作为长期与 金属打交道的手艺人，他对不同合金的密度、薄厚与应力反应极为敏感。就在测 量过程中，他很快意识到一个惊讶之处：这尊像胎体极薄，某些部位甚至仅有一 点几毫米，却仍然保持着完整的一体空心结构。 这意味着什么？意味着它依赖的是高度成熟的失蜡铸造能力：从蜡型塑造、泥芯 稳定、金属配比、浇铸温度，到出模后的打磨与鎏金，任何一个环节稍有偏差， 胎体都可能塌陷或报废。对于 11 世纪的工艺条件而言，这种薄胎而完整的大体 量一体铸造，不只是技术问题，更是组织能力、经验累积与工坊成熟度的体现。 它的宽阔不依赖沉重的宽阔、它的存在感也不是依靠厚度堆砌出来的存在感。相 反，它是在极轻的胎体之上，仍然维持了结构的稳定、比例的完整与气韵的充盈。 在 11 世纪的高原语境中，金铜并非普通材料。铜本可用于武器、器具等重要领 域，金更是高度贵重的物质。因此，一尊体量不小、通体鎏金、又由高等级工坊 完成的佛像，本身已经在暗示其原始供养身份非同一般。它很可能不属于普通私 人供奉，而更接近于王室、大寺院或高等级施主系统中的中心性供养对象。 这种判断并不仅是由“大体量和整体气息”这种感性印象推出的，而是由以下几个 相互支持的线索共同构成：首先，大体量而薄胎的一体铸造，本身对应高工艺等 级；全鎏金说明施主具备较强资源；造型完成度高，并无明显铸补，说明并非普 通批量性作品；帕拉样式被吸收得如此自然，说明工坊对新风格已非常熟练，而 这往往发生在持续赞助的中心区域。换句话说，作品本身已构成证据。它的特别， 不必靠传说来证明。 如果说第一重意义来自铸造时刻，那么第二重意义，则来自之后漫长的使用与观 看。今天我们看到的这尊释迦，并不是刚铸成时的样子。它表面的金层已经退去 部分炫目的锋芒，原始供奉状态形成的皮壳使它显得更沉静，也更有时间的厚度。 这种变化极为重要。因为佛教金铜造像从来不只是“完成即结束”的艺术品，它们 在诞生之后，还要经历长久的供养、抚触、注视、灯火熏染与空间相处。因此， 一尊古代造像真正的精神面貌，往往不只由初始造型决定，也由后天的供养史持 续塑成。也正因为如此，今天它之所以能显得如此安稳，除了工艺以外，还因为 它被一代又一代人的目光和心念慢慢“养”过。所以，我们看到的不是单一时刻的 作品，而是一件在时间中被反复确认、反复安放、反复供奉过的存在。它的“神 韵”，也并不仅是瞬间的，而是积累出来的。 说到这里，最核心的问题才真正浮现：为什么它传达出的，不是单纯的庄严，不 是单纯的慈悲，而偏偏是一种“虚空”感？原因就在于，它让观者面对的，不是某 一个被明确限定的情绪，而是一种去边界化的存在经验。 首先，它没有压迫感。这尊像并不试图用力量征服你，也不靠强烈的姿态来占领 你的视线。它站在那里，像一个已经与自身达成和解的人——不动，不争，不急 于证明什么。其次，高髻、眉眼、衣缘、袖口、肩背的走势，全部都在一个“由 形入空”的方向上行走：线条是清楚的、延展的；体量是充盈的，却不笨重。于 是，观看者的目光不会被某个局部钉住，而会顺着它的气息，在它的周围缓缓流 动。它略带微笑的神情也似乎在讲述着：你本自具足、你很就是美好存在。 在这种面对中，“我是谁、我够不够好、我怎样才算正确”这些现代性问题，会短 暂失去抓力。念头开始松动，心也不再那么急于抓住一个定义自身的位置。这， 正是“虚空”的心理结构。不是把人变成空壳，而是让人暂时脱离那些过度坚硬的 自我执着。所以，这尊像之所以让人感到自由，是因为它在让人经验到：当执着 暂时松开之后，存在本身原来可以如此广阔。 2025 年为了展览画册的拍摄，我们将部分展品带去川西，希望让造像重新回到 更接近高原文化语境的环境中呈现。这尊释迦，也是其中之一。 在一次拍摄途中，我们走进一顶黑帐篷。帐篷里住着一个七岁的小女孩和她的奶 奶。起初，小女孩对我们高度警惕，不断强调“不许偷我的小牛”。这种近乎本能 的防备，并不难理解——她对世界的认识，正在从具体的生存经验中一点点长出 来。而就在这样的气氛里，她忽然看见了这尊释迦，说了一句极简单的话：“祂 在笑耶。” 这句话其实很重要。因为它几乎绕开了所有风格、历史、供养、工艺和学术概念， 直接触到了造像最根本的力量——它仍然能够被一个从未接受过驯化的孩子，立 即识别为一种温和、可接近、可回应的存在。 后来，这个孩子把佛像抱在怀里，神情一下子松开了。那一刻，她不再是那个满 怀戒备的小大人，而重新变回了一个孩子。一件历经千年的古代佛像，在一个当 代高原儿童面前，依然能立刻建立起一种情绪联结。这一瞬间，也让我重新理解 了“虚空”并非抽象概念。它不是远离现实，而恰恰能够进入现实最具体、最脆弱 的时刻。它不隔绝生活，反而给生活以容纳的空间。 所以，站在它面前，我们面对的并不仅只是一尊佛像。我们面对的是：一种如何 在形体之中，让空间重新打开；如何在金属之中，让重量悄然退去；又如何在时 间之中，让焦虑逐渐松动的方式。 如果一定要用一句话概括，那么这尊像真正显现的，也许就是：万物皆在，而内 心不必再紧握不放。 这，也许就是它最深的光。
viewingNote.en: At first glance, this statue of Shakyamuni—originating from central Xizang—appears not to deviate from the general paradigm of early Buddhist imagery from the plateau: a towering topknot, slender eyes and brows, a broad physique, traces of surviving gilding, and an expression of profound serenity. Yet, after spending a significant amount of time in its presence, one discovers that its most captivating quality lies not in the exquisite refinement of any single detail, but rather in an overarching atmosphere—a sensation that defies immediate articulation yet continuously radiates outward. It is like mist, and like the wind; it resembles a boundless "presence," yet refuses to define itself through any concrete form.
