If one were compelled to give this sensation a name, I would prefer to call it: a sense of "Void." The "Void" referred to here is neither nihilism nor mere emptiness. Rather, it points to a state of existence characterized by boundlessness and an absence of attachment—a state capable of embracing all things. And it is precisely here that the central question arises: Why does a gilded bronze statue evoke such a sense of "Void"? To answer this, one cannot rely solely on abstract religious concepts, nor remain confined to purely aesthetic intuition. A more effective approach is to return to the moment of its inception: How was this statue created? Why did it take on this specific form? What history of circulation and devotional veneration has it undergone? And why does it continue to possess such potent spiritual resonance today? The first feature of this Shakyamuni figure to capture one's attention is the distinct aura of softness it exudes—a quality seemingly alien to the indigenous artistic traditions of the plateau's early period. Its towering topknot, its slender and slightly upturned eyes and brows, the enigmatic, half-smiling expression playing upon its lips, and the gentle, outward-sweeping contours of its robes—all manifest a fluid, rhythmic cadence. This stylistic treatment is not merely a reflection of local aesthetic preferences; rather, it bears a profound connection to the Pala artistic system that flourished in Xizang around the 11th century. The Pala style constitutes a pivotal visual tradition of the late Buddhist era in Eastern India. With the "Later Propagation" of Buddhism in Xizang—marked by the arrival of eminent Indian masters, the circulation of scriptures, the dissemination of iconographic models, and the influx of artistic talent and resources driven by shifting political landscapes in Eastern India—this distinctive style gradually penetrated the heartland of the Tibetan Plateau. Particularly following Atisha’s arrival in Xizang, the teachings, iconography, and aesthetic sensibilities of East Indian Buddhism became deeply interwoven with the religious life of Xizang. Consequently, the Pala-style characteristics evident in this sculpture should not be interpreted merely as "foreign ornamentation," but rather as the result of the introduction—and subsequent localization—of a vital visual language during the restructuring of Buddhism on the Tibetan Plateau in the 11th century. In other words, this figure of Shakyamuni does not merely "resemble" the Pala style; rather, it was born in an era when the Pala aesthetic had already begun to play a formative role in shaping the visual identity of Tibetan Buddhist imagery. It represents the tangible manifestation of a cross-regional artistic exchange that had taken root on the Plateau. When commissioning a base for this piece, I enlisted the expertise of a metalworker from Chengdu with whom I have collaborated for many years. As a craftsman who has spent a lifetime working with metal, he possesses an acute sensitivity to the density, thickness, and structural stress responses of various alloys. During the measurement process, he quickly made a surprising discovery: the sculpture’s metal body was remarkably thin—in some areas measuring barely more than a millimeter—yet it had somehow maintained a perfectly intact, single-piece hollow structure. What does this signify? It points to a reliance on a highly sophisticated mastery of the lost-wax casting technique. From the sculpting of the wax model, the stabilization of the clay core, and the precise alloying of metals, to the casting temperature and the final polishing and gilding after demolding—any slight deviation at any stage could have resulted in the collapse or complete ruin of the sculpture’s body. Given the technological constraints of the 11th century, executing such a large-scale, single-piece casting—characterized by its thin walls yet structural integrity—was not merely a technical feat; it was a testament to exceptional organizational capability, accumulated experience, and the advanced maturity of the workshop responsible for its creation. Its imposing breadth does not rely on sheer physical weight; nor is its commanding presence achieved through a mere accumulation of material thickness. On the contrary, it manages to maintain structural stability, proportional harmony, and a palpable sense of spiritual vitality atop an incredibly lightweight frame. Within the context of the 11th-century Tibetan Plateau, gilded bronze was by no means a commonplace material. Copper itself was a valuable resource, utilized in critical applications such as weaponry and tools, while gold was a substance of the utmost rarity and prestige. Thus, a Buddhist sculpture of such substantial size—fully gilded and executed by a workshop of the highest caliber—inherently signals that its original patron was a figure of extraordinary status. It is highly probable that this piece does not belong to the realm of ordinary private devotion, but rather approximates a central object of worship within the systems of royalty, major monasteries, or high-ranking patrons. This judgment is not derived solely from subjective impressions—such as its "imposing scale and overall aura"—but is instead substantiated by several mutually reinforcing clues: First, its substantial size combined with a thin-walled, single-cast construction signifies a high level of craftsmanship. The complete gilding indicates that the patron possessed significant resources. The high degree of refinement in its modeling—devoid of any obvious casting repairs—demonstrates that it is not a mass-produced, ordinary piece. Furthermore, the assimilation of the Pala style is so seamless that it suggests the workshop had already attained a high level of mastery over this new aesthetic—a proficiency typically found in central regions where artistic production was sustained by continuous patronage. In other words, the artwork itself serves as its own evidence; its exceptional nature requires no legends to validate it. If the first layer of significance stems from the moment of its casting, then the second layer emerges from the long subsequent history of its use and contemplation. The figure of Shakyamuni we behold today is not identical to the image that emerged fresh from the mold. The gold plating on its surface has shed some of its initial dazzling brilliance; the patina formed through its history of worship lends it a greater sense of serenity and imbues it with the palpable weight of time. This transformation is of profound importance. For Buddhist gilt-bronze sculptures are never merely artworks whose existence concludes the moment they are "finished"; rather, following their creation, they undergo a prolonged process of veneration, physical contact, intense scrutiny, exposure to the smoke of ritual lamps, and immersion within their spatial environment. Consequently, the true spiritual character of an ancient sculpture is rarely determined solely by its initial form; it is continuously shaped by the subsequent history of its worship. It is precisely for this reason that this piece appears so profoundly tranquil today—a quality attributable not only to its craftsmanship but also to the fact that it has been slowly "nurtured" by the gaze and devotion of generations of worshippers. Thus, what we perceive is not merely a work frozen in a single moment, but an entity that has been repeatedly affirmed, situated, and venerated throughout the passage of time. Its "spiritual resonance," too, is not a fleeting phenomenon, but a quality that has been accumulated over time. Having reached this point, the most fundamental question finally comes into sharp focus: Why is it that what this sculpture conveys is neither mere solemnity, nor mere compassion, but—specifically—a profound sense of "emptiness"? The reason lies in the fact that what it presents to the viewer is not a single, clearly defined emotion, but rather an existential experience devoid of boundaries. First and foremost, it exerts no sense of oppression. This statue makes no attempt to conquer you with sheer force, nor does it seek to seize your gaze through dramatic gestures. It simply stands there—like a person who has made peace with themselves—motionless, non-contending, and in no hurry to prove anything. Secondly, every element—the high topknot, the contours of the brows and eyes, the hems of the robes, the cuffs of the sleeves, and the lines of the shoulders and back—all move in a direction best described as "transitioning from form into emptiness." The lines are distinct yet expansive; the volume is substantial yet never ponderous. Consequently, the viewer’s gaze is never pinned down by a single detail; instead, it follows the statue’s subtle aura, flowing gently around it. Its expression—bearing a faint smile—also seems to whisper a message: *You are already complete within yourself; you are, in your very essence, a beautiful existence.* In this encounter, those quintessential questions of modernity—"Who am I?", "Am I good enough?", "How must I be to be 'right'?"—momentarily lose their grip. Thoughts begin to loosen their hold, and the mind is no longer so desperate to latch onto a fixed position to define itself. This, precisely, is the psychological structure of "emptiness." It does not reduce a person to a hollow shell; rather, it allows one to temporarily detach from those overly rigid forms of self-attachment. Thus, the reason this statue evokes such a sense of freedom is that it allows the viewer to experience this profound truth: once attachment is momentarily released, the nature of existence itself reveals itself to be vast beyond measure. In 2025, for the photography sessions intended for our exhibition catalog, we transported a selection of exhibits to Western Sichuan. Our hope was to present these statues within an environment that more closely mirrors their original cultural context—the high plateau. This statue of Shakyamuni was among them. During one of our photography excursions, we stepped into a traditional black yak-hair tent. Inside lived a seven-year-old girl and her grandmother. At first, the little girl regarded us with intense wariness, repeatedly insisting, "You are not allowed to steal my little calf!" Such an almost instinctive defensiveness was not difficult to understand; her understanding of the world was, at that very moment, slowly taking shape—growing, piece by piece, directly out of her concrete experiences of survival. And amidst this very atmosphere, she suddenly caught sight of this figure of Shakyamuni and uttered a remarkably simple phrase: "He is smiling." This observation was, in fact, profoundly significant. For it bypassed—almost entirely—all notions of style, history, patronage, craftsmanship, and academic theory, reaching directly to the statue’s most fundamental power: the fact that it could still be instantly recognized—by a child whose spirit had never been tamed—as a gentle, approachable, and responsive presence. Later, the child cradled the Buddha statue in her arms, and her expression instantly softened. In that moment, she was no longer that guarded "little adult," but had transformed back into a child once more. An ancient Buddha statue, having endured for a thousand years, was still able to forge an immediate emotional bond with a contemporary child from the highlands. This fleeting moment also led me to a renewed understanding: that "emptiness" is not merely an abstract concept. It does not signify a detachment from reality; rather, it possesses the capacity to enter into reality’s most concrete and vulnerable moments. It does not isolate us from life; on the contrary, it grants life the space to breathe. Thus, standing before it, we confront something far greater than a mere Buddha statue. We confront a *way*—a way to allow space to unfold anew within physical form; a way to let weight quietly recede within the solidity of metal; and a way to let anxiety gradually dissolve within the passage of time. If one were compelled to summarize it in a single sentence, perhaps what this statue truly reveals is this: that all things exist, yet the heart need no longer cling to them so tightly. This, perhaps, is its deepest light.

title.zh: Buddhist Art of the Xizang Realm, P.110, fig.12.
title.en: Buddhist Art of the Xizang Realm, p. 110, fig. 12.

excerpt.en: A compact Kathmandu image whose stillness emerges through precision and proportion.

viewingNote.zh: 站在一件雕塑面前，你是否有过这样的经验：原本意识纷杂、情绪嘈杂，甚至只 是匆匆一瞥，整个人却忽然安静了下来。不是震撼，不是惊艳，也不是某种强烈 的宗教压迫感，而是一种更轻、更深、更不动声色的力量——它不推着你进入某 种意义，反而让你暂时从意义中退开，让大脑放空，让情绪沉降。 我第一次面对这尊释迦造像时，产生的正是这样的经验。那是一个普通的午后。 天气闷热，琐事堆积，人的精神状态并不清明。可就在走进展厅、与这尊像四目 相对的一刻，一种极其温和却又十分稳定的力量悄然发生。它没有把我拉向激动， 也没有把我推向崇高，而是让原本分散的意识突然失去了继续喧哗的理由。那一 刻，头脑几乎是空的，情绪也随之平下去。像是上一秒还在心里翻涌的烦躁，被 一种更大的秩序轻轻接住了。 后来我一直在想：这种安静，究竟是怎么发生的？它显然不只是“审美愉悦”， 也不完全是“文化感动”。如果要更准确地说，它更像是一种被雕塑触发的心理 复位：在混乱的内在节奏中，突然出现了一个更完整、更通透、更稳定的形象， 于是人的精神被短暂地重新校准了。 从风格上看，这是一尊 12 世纪尼泊尔、并融合帕拉美学因素的释迦牟尼造像。 如果只看图像学题材，它并不特殊；但真正使它成立的，不是题材本身，而是它 对“释迦”这一形象所做出的独特处理。 首先是面部。高耸的发髻、细长而微微上扬的眉眼、挺直的鼻梁、嘴角那一抹极 轻的笑意，都带有一种优雅与流动感。但这种流动不是表面的装饰，而是一种被 控制得极好的内在节奏。眉眼虽然细长，却不显轻薄；嘴角虽有笑意，却并不落 入世俗表情。整个面部情绪带着一种早期造像常见的优美感——安静、节制、微 妙，既不拒人于千里之外，也不急着向观者靠近。所以，这张脸最迷人的地方， 是情绪的完整性。它没有多余的表演，也没有局部失衡的冲突。你一眼望过去， 会觉得这张脸内部是通的。而正是这种“通”，使它具有安定人的能力。 从形体上看，这尊释迦的上身宽阔而不臃肿，手臂、肩膀、胸腔之间的转折非常 清楚。这种清楚，不是依赖夸张肌肉，而是依赖对骨架、体块和肌肉走向的准确 理解。也就是说，匠人没有把身体概念化地“拼”出来，而是真正理解了身体在 空间中如何成立。 再看下身。双腿结跏趺坐，腿部修长而舒展，因弯曲与承重而出现的肌肉鼓胀感 并未被抹平，反而被有意识地保留下来。这使造像虽然静坐，却没有失去生命性。 你能感觉到这不是一个僵硬摆出的姿势，而是一个真实的身体在安住之后，所呈 现出的稳定状态。从构成上说，头、肩、腰、腿之间形成了一个非常舒朗、开阔 的三角式结构。这种结构一方面保证了造像应有的庄严与稳定，另一方面又使它 不至于板滞。也正因为如此，它既显神性，又不失人的温度。 如果要用一个更准确的词来概括，我会说：这尊像体现的是一种被理想化了的真 实人体。它不是现实中的某一个人，却又始终以真实身体为基础。所以它会让人 想到青春、想到干净、想到最明亮的生命阶段。那不是脆弱的年轻，而是一种尚 未被消耗、尚未被内耗、尚未被现实撕裂的完整状态。 通常，很多人会误以为，雕塑越复杂越难，越简约越容易。其实恰恰相反。真正 难的，往往不是堆砌细节，而是在极少的语言里，仍然让形体成立。这尊释迦的 处理，就是一个典型例子。衣饰轻薄、贴体，边缘线控制得很克制，没有多余的 波折，也没有依赖繁复褶皱去制造“精致感”。这种做法很危险，因为一旦形体 理解不够，衣纹一减，结构就会立刻松掉。但这件作品恰恰相反。正因为衣纹简， 身体的结构才被更清楚地释放出来。肩胛骨的起伏、背部的转折、臀部与腰部之 间的关系，都没有被装饰遮蔽。即便在整体风格偏概括的前提下，骨架、肌肉、 衣物三者之间的层次仍然清楚可辨。也就是说，这尊像真正的“炫技”，并不在 热闹处，而恰恰藏在那些看似平静、看似不费力的地方。它越简，越说明匠人底 层功力扎实；它越安静，越说明每一处都已经被推敲到了刚刚好的位置。 如果进一步追问，我们会发现：这尊释迦之所以能让人产生安静感，并不仅仅因 为它“做得好”，而是因为它塑造了一种极其完整的理想人格形象。佛教雕塑中 的“理想美”，从来不只是形式上的漂亮。它也不是单纯为了表达宗教威严。更 深的层面在于，它把一种关于“人可以成为什么样”的理想，放进了可见的形体 里。当人站在它面前时，真正被唤起的，也许不是某种宗教概念，而是一种更私 人、却更本质的记忆：原来人也曾经有过这样一种状态——干净、真诚、明亮、 不自我撕扯。也正因如此，这尊像带来的感受，不只是“我看见了一尊佛”，而 更像是：“我突然想起了，自己曾经也有过接近这种状态的时候。”
viewingNote.en:
Have you ever stood before a sculpture and had this experience: your mind—previously cluttered with thoughts and noisy with emotions—suddenly fell silent, even if only after a fleeting glance? It was not a sense of shock, nor of dazzling beauty, nor even the intense, imposing weight of religious awe; rather, it was a power at once lighter, deeper, and more understated. It did not compel you toward a specific meaning; instead, it allowed you to temporarily step back from meaning itself—letting the mind go blank and allowing emotions to settle. This was precisely the experience I had when I first stood before this statue of Shakyamuni. It was an ordinary afternoon. The weather was muggy, trivial tasks had piled up, and my state of mind was far from clear. Yet, the very moment I stepped into the exhibition hall and met the gaze of this statue, an incredibly gentle yet profoundly steady force quietly took hold. It did not pull me toward excitement, nor did it push me toward the sublime; instead, it caused my previously scattered consciousness to suddenly lose any reason to continue its clamor. In that moment, my mind was almost entirely empty, and my emotions subsided in tandem. It was as if the agitation that had been churning within me just moments before had been gently caught and cradled by a greater order. Later, I found myself constantly pondering: How, exactly, did this stillness come about? It was clearly not merely "aesthetic pleasure," nor was it solely "cultural resonance." To describe it more precisely, it felt more like a psychological "reset" triggered by the sculpture: amidst the chaotic rhythms of my inner world, a more complete, lucid, and stable image suddenly emerged, and in its presence, my spirit was briefly recalibrated. Stylistically, this is a 12th-century Nepalese depiction of Shakyamuni, infused with aesthetic elements from the Pala tradition. If judged solely by its iconographic subject matter, it is not particularly unique; yet, what truly gives it its power is not the subject itself, but rather the unique manner in which it interprets and renders the figure of "Shakyamuni." First, consider the face. The towering topknot, the slender and ever-so-slightly arched eyebrows and eyes, the straight bridge of the nose, and the faint trace of a smile at the corners of the mouth—all convey a sense of elegance and fluidity. Yet, this fluidity is not mere surface ornamentation; rather, it is an inner rhythm—one that has been controlled and refined with exquisite mastery. Although the eyes and brows are slender and elongated, they do not appear frivolous; and while a smile plays upon the corners of the mouth, it never descends into mere worldly expression. The overall emotional tenor of the face carries a sense of gracefulness characteristic of early sculptural art—quiet, restrained, and subtle—neither holding the viewer at a distant remove nor rushing to draw them close. Thus, the most captivating aspect of this face lies in the integrity of its emotional state. It is devoid of superfluous theatricality, as well as any localized conflicts or imbalances. At a single glance, one senses a profound internal coherence within the face. And it is precisely this "coherence" that endows it with the power to instill a sense of calm in the beholder. In terms of physique, the upper body of this Shakyamuni figure is broad yet never bloated; the transitions between the arms, shoulders, and torso are articulated with striking clarity. This clarity is achieved not through the exaggeration of musculature, but through a precise understanding of skeletal structure, anatomical mass, and the natural flow of muscles. In other words, the artisan did not merely conceptualize and "assemble" the body; rather, they possessed a genuine understanding of how the human form establishes its presence within three-dimensional space. Turning to the lower body: the legs are crossed in the full lotus position—long, slender, and gracefully extended. The subtle swelling of the muscles—naturally occurring as a result of flexion and weight-bearing—has not been smoothed away; on the contrary, it has been consciously preserved. This ensures that, despite its seated stillness, the sculpture never forfeits its sense of vitality. One senses that this is not a rigid, posed attitude, but rather the stable equilibrium attained by a living body that has settled into a state of profound repose. Structurally, the head, shoulders, torso, and legs form a triangular composition that feels remarkably open and expansive. On one hand, this structure ensures the figure possesses the requisite dignity and stability; on the other, it prevents the form from appearing stiff or static. It is precisely for this reason that the sculpture simultaneously radiates divinity while retaining the warmth of the human spirit. If I were to summarize its essence with a single, precise term, I would say this: the statue embodies an idealized vision of the human form. It does not depict any specific individual from reality, yet it remains firmly grounded in the anatomical truth of the human body. Consequently, it evokes thoughts of youth, of purity, and of that most radiant phase of human existence. This is not a fragile youthfulness, but rather a state of absolute wholeness—one that has yet to be depleted, consumed by internal strife, or torn asunder by the harsh realities of the world. Many people commonly—and mistakenly—assume that the more complex and intricate a sculpture is, the more difficult it must be to create, while the simpler it is, the easier it must be. In reality, the exact opposite is true. The true challenge often lies not in the accumulation of details, but rather in establishing a compelling form using the most minimal of visual language. The treatment of this statue of Shakyamuni serves as a quintessential example of this principle. The drapery is rendered as light and clinging to the body; its contours are controlled with great restraint, devoid of superfluous undulations or any reliance on intricate folds to manufacture a sense of "refinement." This approach is inherently risky; for if one’s understanding of anatomy is insufficient, the reduction of drapery lines will cause the underlying structural integrity to collapse instantly. Yet, this piece achieves precisely the opposite effect. It is precisely *because* the drapery is so sparse that the underlying bodily structure is revealed with even greater clarity. The undulation of the scapulae, the turning of the back, and the dynamic relationship between the hips and the waist—none of these are obscured by ornamentation. Even within a stylistic framework that favors broad generalization, the distinct layers—comprising the skeletal structure, the musculature, and the drapery—remain clearly discernible. In other words, the true display of technical virtuosity in this statue lies not in its ostentatious features, but rather in those places that appear outwardly calm and effortless. The simpler it appears, the more it testifies to the craftsman’s solid foundational mastery; the quieter it feels, the more it demonstrates that every single detail has been meticulously refined and positioned to absolute perfection.

If we probe deeper, we discover that the sense of tranquility evoked by this statue of Shakyamuni stems not merely from the fact that it is "well-made," but because it embodies and projects an exceptionally complete image of an ideal human persona. The "ideal beauty" found in Buddhist sculpture has never been a matter of mere aesthetic prettiness. Nor is it solely intended to convey religious majesty. On a deeper level, it takes an ideal—an aspiration regarding "what a human being can become"—and renders it visible within a tangible form. When one stands before this statue, what is truly awakened may not be a specific religious concept, but rather a more personal—yet profoundly essential—memory: the realization that we, too, have once known such a state of being—one that is pure, sincere, luminous, and free from internal conflict. It is precisely for this reason that the sensation evoked by this statue transcends the simple observation of "I see a Buddha"; instead, it feels more like: "I have suddenly been reminded of a time when I, too, came close to attaining such a state."

excerpt.en: A standing Vajrapani of upright energy and sharply focused form.

viewingNote.zh: 每次去巴黎，吉美国立亚洲艺术博物馆总是我们一定会去的地方。明明已经去过 很多次，可每当走到那些喜欢的早期雕塑面前，我们还是会不由自主地停下来。 那一刻，最明显的感受并不是“惊艳”，而是——时间慢了。博物馆仿佛忽然安静 下来，人与雕塑之间只剩一种无声的对视。思绪被吸住，心也慢慢沉下来。这其 实是一个很值得追问的问题：为什么一尊雕塑，能够改变人的感知节奏？ 第一次见到这尊 9—10 世纪尼泊尔金刚手菩萨立像时，感受就和在吉美相似。 我连续看了好几天。它并不靠夸张的体量取胜，也不以装饰的繁复压住你，但每 次看，都会觉得它丰富得像一个真实的人，不单调，不重复，始终带着一种极强 的耐看性。那么，这种耐看性从哪里来？ 不同地域的造像，往往会悄悄携带它所诞生之地的文化记忆。尼泊尔早期艺术尤 其如此。李查维王朝大约活跃于 4 至 9 世纪，是尼泊尔早期最重要的历史王朝 之一。学界普遍认为，李查维时期的艺术与北印度笈多传统关系密切；而在李查 维之后的过渡阶段，尼泊尔并没有丢掉这种古典底色，反而在长期发展中把它不 断提炼、内化，形成了自己稳定而优雅的早期造像面貌。这尊金刚手身上，最先 能感受到的，正是这种古典传统留下的纯净气息。 菩萨头戴三叶冠，冠饰并不张扬，却十分明确地建立起身份的尊贵感。耳后的冠 结处点缀着细小花朵，头顶发髻高束，如一朵圆润收拢的莲蓬。两侧垂落的发丝 前方，佩戴着圆形与花瓣形耳环，这些细节都带有明显的时代印记。但真正让人 停下来的，还是脸。 这张脸并不靠强烈表情来吸引你。它的眉弓细长，鼻梁高挺，双眼安静，嘴角极 轻地向上收着。那不是一种热烈的笑，而是一种几乎像呼吸一样自然的微笑。也 正因为如此，它不会把你往某个具体情绪里推，却能一点点把你心里的浮躁安抚 下去。观者面对它时，会感到自己心里那些零碎的东西，正在被一个更整合的形 象慢慢接住。 如果说最初抓住我的，是它整体的气息，那么真正让我一直回头去看的，是它的 眼神。大多数时候，我们所熟悉的菩萨形象，目光往往带着很明确的温柔与慈悲。 那是一种指向性的凝视：它看着你，回应你的祈愿，接住你的情绪。但这尊金刚 手不太一样。当我试图捕捉它的目光时，首先感受到的是，它的眼神并没落在任 何一个具体的地方，但眼中又充满着内容。那并不是空洞的神情，而是一种既写 实、又超出的深度。像一扇半掩的门，门后似乎通往更辽阔的意识空间。 也就是说，它的目光并不急着落在某一个具体对象上。它既不专门为某一个祈求 者停驻，也不热情地朝向现实世界。它更像越过了肉身与日常，落向某个遥远、 无形、却更长久的地方。所以，这尊金刚手最迷人的地方就在这里：它的眼神并 不提供即时答案，它也不急着抚慰。它更像是在凝望一个永恒的问题。而那个问 题本身，就已经足够把人带入沉思。这也正是它耐看性的第一重来源：不是因为 它给得很多，而是因为它保留得很多。它没有把意义一次说尽，所以观者才会不 断回去看，不断试图进入那份静默。 如果说眼神抓住的是意识，那么身体抓住的，就是感官。 这尊金刚手最初让我开始思索的，其实是它那带有明显动势的三折姿。因为乍看 之下，这种幅度较大的躯干扭转，很容易让人觉得它会失去稳定。可真正细看时， 却会发现：它不但没有不稳，反而呈现出极强的内在平衡。这也同样是匠人发挥 很精彩的地方。造像整体的衣纹非常简练，几乎剔除了多余的装饰性褶皱，只用 最基本的线条勾勒身体的起伏。在体块处理上，也没有走向厚重和堆砌，而是采 用概括与留白并用的方式，让形体更干净、更经得起反复观看。所以，身体的成 立，不是靠表面的细节，而是靠结构本身。 如果以宝冠顶部为中点，顺着身体划出一条中轴线，就会清楚看见匠人的塑造逻 辑。右侧上半身，从腋下到腰部，再到髋部，形成一个向内扭转的连续弧线，像 风中枝干，带有明显的运动趋势。但右腿却几乎保持垂直，如立柱般稳稳落地。 而左侧则相反：上半身相对平静，下半身却以更符合人体自然舒适度的姿态轻轻 放松下来。于是，左右两侧形成了一种极其微妙的互补关系：一边在动，一边在 守；一边像流，一边像立。 这也正是三折姿真正美学价值所在——它不是简单地“扭一下”，而是在动与静之 间建立一种更高级的平衡。所以，这尊像之所以耐看，不只是因为它姿态优美， 而是因为匠人做到了更难的一步：在看似矛盾的结构里，创造出和谐。 真正好的雕塑，从来不是被动地模仿现实。它一定包含了一次筛选、提炼和再创 造。这尊金刚手也是如此。匠人显然不是机械地照着自然人体去复制动作，而是 在长期观察身体、理解平衡、掌握节奏之后，重新组织了一个更理想的形体。所 以，它看起来当然符合真实人体的基本规律，但你又能明显感觉到：这不是日常 中任何一个人会自然站出的样子。它比现实更有秩序，比现实更有音乐感，也比 现实更接近一种“被提高过的生命状态”。正因如此，这尊金刚手给人的观感，不 是单纯的写实，也不是纯粹的抽象神化。它更像是一种经过精神过滤后的身体。 它仍然有肉身的重量、骨架的逻辑、动作的节奏，但这一切都已经被组织成一种 更清楚、更高级、更能容纳神性的形式。所以说，这尊像不是自然人体的复制， 而是对“什么样的身体才足以承载神性”这一问题的一次回答。 说到这里，就会来到一个更深的问题。这样一件既优雅、又稳定，既具古典纯净 感、又带有明显内在张力的作品，为什么会出现在 9—10 世纪的尼泊尔？这恰 恰和它所处的时代有关。 李查维王朝结束之后，尼泊尔进入一个权力转换与社会重组的过渡阶段。这种时 期往往不是文化停滞的时候，反而常常是艺术语言重新聚拢、重新确认自身方向 的时候。因为时代越有缝隙，人越会本能地寻求某种更稳的形象。而在佛教艺术 中，这种稳定并不是僵死的秩序，而是一种把人引向更大、更宽、更不被现实局 限的方向感。从这个意义上说，这尊金刚手的诞生，也许并不只是宗教供养行为 的自然结果。它更像是一种回应：在时代并不完全安稳的时候，匠人与供养人共 同借由一尊像，把自己所信的秩序、希望与精神方向，做成了一个能被看见、能 被安放、也能被反复确认的形象。所以，这件作品里那种美好、坚定和宽广，也 许并不是后人强加上去的解释，而本来就是它诞生时被寄托进去的东西。 这时，最初的问题其实也就有了答案。为什么一尊雕塑，能够让人感知发生深刻 变化？为什么它会让时间变慢，让情绪松开，让人短暂地从现实纷扰中抽离出 来？因为它所提供的，不只是一个“好看”的形象，而是一个足够完整、足够稳定、 足够有方向感的存在结构。它的脸，不把意义说尽；它的眼神，不急着回应现实； 它的身体，在动与静之间保持平衡；它的整体气息，又让人感觉一切都已经在更 大的秩序里被安放妥当。于是，观者面对它时，内心那些原本散乱的东西，也开 始慢慢被重新组织。这就是为什么疗愈会发生——不是因为它替你解决了什么， 而是因为它以自身的完整，短暂地帮你恢复了一种完整。所以，这尊金刚手之所 以耐看，是因为它在有限的空间里，做出了近乎无限的回味。 回到最初。 每次在吉美博物馆站在那些喜欢的早期雕塑前，我总会有一种奇怪的感受：好像 并不是我在看它们，而是我借由它们，慢慢靠近了自己某个更安静的部分。这尊 金刚手，也是如此。 它的眼神、它的姿态、它那种动中有稳的节奏，都像在把人往某个方向轻轻引过 去。那个方向，也许正是：平和、自由、超越、宽广。 所以，它更像是一则关于“抵达”的故事。 那么，你呢？ 当你面对这样一尊像时， 是否也已经在不知不觉之间， 开始靠近那份安定与辽阔了？
viewingNote.en: Whenever we visit Paris, the Guimet Museum of Asian Arts is a destination we never fail to include in our itinerary. Although we have visited countless times, whenever we find ourselves standing before the early sculptures we so admire, we invariably find ourselves pausing. In that moment, the most palpable sensation is not one of being "dazzled," but rather—that time itself has slowed down. The museum seems to fall suddenly silent, leaving nothing but a wordless exchange of gazes between the viewer and the sculpture. One’s thoughts become captivated, and the heart slowly settles into a state of calm. This raises a question well worth exploring: How is it that a single sculpture can alter the very rhythm of human perception? When I first encountered this standing figure of Vajrapani—a Nepalese Bodhisattva dating to the 9th or 10th century—my experience was remarkably similar to those moments at the Guimet. I returned to observe it, day after day, for nearly a week. It does not seek to impress through sheer, exaggerated scale, nor does it overwhelm the viewer with an excess of intricate ornamentation; yet, with every viewing, it feels as rich and complex as a living human being—never monotonous, never repetitive, and always possessing a profound, enduring visual appeal. From where, then, does this enduring appeal stem? Sculptural traditions from different regions often quietly carry within them the cultural memories of the lands in which they were born. This is particularly true of early Nepalese art. The Licchavi Dynasty, active roughly between the 4th and 9th centuries, stands as one of the most significant historical dynasties of early Nepal. Scholarly consensus holds that the art of the Licchavi period was deeply intertwined with the Gupta artistic traditions of Northern India. During the transitional period that followed the Licchavi era, Nepal did not cast aside this classical foundation; rather, through a process of sustained evolution, it continuously refined and internalized these elements, thereby forging a distinct, stable, and elegant aesthetic for its early sculptural forms. What one senses most immediately in this figure of Vajrapani is precisely this—the pure, resonant aura left behind by that classical tradition. The Bodhisattva wears a tripartite crown; while its ornamentation is understated rather than ostentatious, it nonetheless serves to unequivocally establish a sense of noble dignity. Tiny floral motifs adorn the knots of the crown where they rest behind the ears, while the hair is gathered into a high topknot that resembles a smooth, tightly furled lotus pod. Suspended just in front of the strands of hair cascading down the sides of the head are earrings—some circular, others shaped like petals—details that bear the unmistakable stylistic hallmarks of the era. Yet, what truly compels one to pause and linger is, ultimately, the face. This face does not rely on intense expressions to captivate you. Its brow ridges are slender and elongated, its nose bridge high and prominent; its eyes are serene, and the corners of its mouth curl upward ever so slightly. It is not a fervent smile, but rather a smile as natural—almost—as breathing itself. Precisely for this reason, it does not attempt to steer you toward any specific emotion; instead, it gradually soothes the restlessness stirring within your heart. When a viewer stands before it, they sense that the fragmented pieces of their inner world are slowly being gathered and embraced by a more unified, cohesive presence. If what initially seized my attention was its overall aura, then what truly compels me to return to it again and again is the gaze within its eyes. In most instances, the Bodhisattva figures we are familiar with possess a gaze imbued with a distinct sense of tenderness and compassion. It is a directed gaze—one that looks *at* you, responds to your supplications, and absorbs your emotions. This particular Vajrapani, however, is different. When I attempt to meet its gaze, my first impression is that its eyes do not rest upon any specific point; yet, they are simultaneously brimming with substance. It is not an empty stare, but rather a depth that feels both grounded in reality and transcendent of it—like a door left slightly ajar, seemingly opening onto a boundless realm of consciousness. In other words, its gaze is in no hurry to settle upon any particular object. It neither pauses exclusively for a specific supplicant nor turns outward with fervor toward the material world. Instead, it seems to look past the physical form and the mundane details of daily life, fixing its gaze upon something distant, intangible, yet enduring. Thus lies the most captivating quality of this Vajrapani: its gaze offers no immediate answers, nor does it rush to offer solace. Rather, it appears to be contemplating an eternal question. And that very question—in and of itself—is enough to draw the observer into a state of deep contemplation. This, too, is the primary source of its enduring appeal: not because it gives so much away, but because it holds so much in reserve. It does not exhaust its meaning in a single revelation; consequently, the viewer is compelled to return again and again, ceaselessly striving to enter into the profound silence it embodies. If the gaze captures the consciousness, then the body captures the senses. What first prompted me to reflect upon this Vajrapani was, in fact, its *tribhanga*—the triple-bend posture—which conveys such a palpable sense of dynamic movement. At first glance, this figure’s dramatic torso twist might easily lead one to believe it is on the verge of losing its stability. Yet, upon closer inspection, one discovers the opposite: far from being unstable, it radiates a profound sense of inner equilibrium. This represents a particularly brilliant aspect of the craftsman’s artistry. The drapery covering the figure is remarkably minimalist; nearly all superfluous decorative folds have been eliminated, leaving only the most fundamental lines to delineate the contours of the body. In handling the sculptural masses, the artist avoided heaviness or excessive accumulation, opting instead for a technique that combines generalization with strategic "negative space." This approach renders the form cleaner and more enduring—capable of withstanding repeated, prolonged scrutiny. Consequently, the body’s presence is established not through superficial details, but through the strength of its underlying structure. If one were to designate the apex of the jeweled crown as the central point and trace an imaginary axis down through the body, the craftsman’s sculptural logic would become immediately apparent. On the right side, the upper body—from the armpit to the waist, and down to the hip—forms a continuous, inward-twisting arc; much like a branch swaying in the wind, it conveys a distinct sense of dynamic motion. The right leg, however, remains nearly vertical, planted firmly on the ground like a steadfast pillar. The left side presents a striking contrast: the upper body remains relatively still, while the lower body relaxes gently into a posture that aligns more closely with the natural comfort and ease of the human form. Thus, the left and right sides enter into an exquisitely subtle complementary relationship: one side is in motion, while the other holds steady; one flows, while the other stands firm. This constitutes the true aesthetic value of the *Tribhanga* (triple-bend) posture: it is not merely a simple "twist," but rather the establishment of a higher-order equilibrium between dynamism and stillness. The reason this sculpture remains so endlessly captivating lies not solely in the grace of its pose, but in the craftsman’s achievement of a far more difficult feat: creating harmony within a structure that appears, on the surface, to be inherently contradictory. Truly great sculpture is never a passive imitation of reality; it invariably involves a process of selection, refinement, and re-creation. This figure of Vajrapani is no exception. The craftsman clearly did not mechanically replicate the movements of the natural human body; instead—having spent years observing the body, comprehending the principles of balance, and mastering the rhythms of form—he reconstructed and synthesized a more idealized physical presence. Thus, while it certainly appears to adhere to the fundamental laws of the human physique, one can also distinctly sense that this is not a posture any ordinary person would naturally assume in daily life. It possesses a greater sense of order than reality, a more musical quality, and comes closer to embodying a "heightened state of being." Precisely for this reason, the visual impression conveyed by this figure of Vajrapani is neither mere realism nor pure, abstract deification. Rather, it resembles a body that has undergone a process of spiritual filtration. It retains the weight of the flesh, the structural logic of the skeleton, and the rhythm of movement; yet, all these elements have been organized into a form that is clearer, more elevated, and more capable of containing the divine. Thus, this statue is not a mere replication of the natural human form, but rather an answer to the question: "What kind of body is truly worthy of bearing the divine?" This brings us to an even deeper inquiry: Why would such a work—at once elegant and stable, possessing both a sense of classical purity and a palpable inner tension—emerge in Nepal during the 9th or 10th century? The answer lies precisely in the era in which it was created. Following the collapse of the Licchavi Dynasty, Nepal entered a transitional phase marked by shifts in power and social restructuring. Such periods are rarely times of cultural stagnation; on the contrary, they often serve as moments when artistic languages ​​coalesce anew and reaffirm their own trajectories. For the more fractured and uncertain an era becomes, the more instinctively people seek out forms that embody stability. In the context of Buddhist art, however, this stability does not signify a rigid, lifeless order; rather, it represents a sense of direction—one that guides the individual toward something vaster, broader, and unconstrained by the limitations of mundane reality. In this sense, the creation of this Vajrapani figure may not have been merely the natural outcome of a religious act of offering. Instead, it appears to be a form of response: in a time of social instability, the artisan and the patron collaborated—through the medium of this statue—to transform their shared beliefs in order, hope, and spiritual direction into a tangible image—one that could be seen, given a place of honor, and repeatedly reaffirmed. Thus, the qualities of beauty, steadfastness, and expansiveness that we perceive in this work today may not be interpretations retroactively imposed by later generations, but rather the very sentiments and aspirations that were imbued into it at the moment of its creation. And with that, the initial question finds its answer. Why is it that a single sculpture can trigger such a profound shift in one’s perception? Why does it seem to slow down time, soothe the emotions, and allow one to briefly withdraw from the clamor of reality? Because what it offers is not merely a "pleasing" image, but an existential structure—one that is sufficiently complete, stable, and imbued with a sense of direction. Its face does not exhaust its meaning; its gaze is in no rush to respond to the demands of reality; its body maintains a perfect equilibrium between motion and stillness; and its overall aura conveys the sense that everything has already been harmoniously settled within a greater cosmic order. Consequently, when a viewer stands before it, the scattered fragments within their own inner world begin, slowly but surely, to reorganize themselves. This is why healing occurs—not because the sculpture solves any specific problem for you, but because, through its own inherent wholeness, it momentarily helps you reclaim a sense of wholeness within yourself. Thus, the enduring appeal of this particular Vajrapani figure lies in its ability to evoke a sense of near-infinite resonance within a finite space. Let us return to the beginning. Whenever I stand before the early sculptures I admire at the Guimet Museum, I am invariably struck by a peculiar sensation: it feels as though I am not merely observing them, but rather, through them, I am slowly drawing closer to a quieter, deeper part of my own self. This Vajrapani figure evokes that very same feeling. Its gaze, its posture, and the rhythm of its movement—poised between dynamism and stillness—all seem to gently guide the viewer in a specific direction. That direction, perhaps, is precisely this: peace, freedom, transcendence, and expansiveness. In this sense, it serves less as a mere object of art and more as a narrative—a story of "arrival." And what of you? When you stand before a statue such as this, have you, too—perhaps without even realizing it— begun to draw closer to that very same sense of serenity and boundlessness?

excerpt.en: A Shalu-style Vajrasattva combining Nepalese refinement with Xizang gravity.

viewingNote.zh: 一听到“金刚”二字，很多人脑海里浮现出的，往往是锋利、强硬、忿怒，甚至 带着威慑力的形象。但眼前这尊金刚萨埵，恰恰相反。它端坐不迫，神情清朗， 既不张扬，也不逼人。如果一定要概括它最打动人的地方，我会说：它像是一位 “忿怒中的温柔者”。 这听上去似乎矛盾。但也正是这个矛盾，构成了金刚萨埵真正的核心。要理解这 件作品，首先要了解：金刚萨埵到底是谁？ 在藏传佛教中，金刚萨埵是极重要的本尊之一，最核心的功能与“净除罪障、清 净业力”有关。金刚萨埵在密教传统中，普遍被作为净化罪垢与烦恼的重要修法 本尊。而在更高一层的密教体系中，金刚总持被理解为究竟法身、也就是金刚乘 佛教中的本初佛；金刚萨埵则常被视为与金刚总持密切相关、可被修行者直接依 止和实践的化身显现形式。金刚总持被视为金刚乘的本初佛，而金刚萨埵则被理 解为其“内在形式”之一。 如果把这个关系说得更清楚一点，金刚总持像是“究竟真理”本身，而金刚萨埵， 则是这种真理进入修行过程后，变得可以被观修、可以被依靠、也可以被内在经 验触及的样子。换言之：金刚总持是太阳本身；金刚萨埵则是穿过云层，能够温 暖人心的光 。 也正因为如此，金刚萨埵的重要性，并不在于“强”，而在于“清”。祂所面对 的，不是外部敌人，而是人心内部的遮蔽。祂不是来替人逃离烦恼，而是帮助人 有勇气直面烦恼、净化烦恼、穿透烦恼。所以，“金刚”在这里，并不等于发怒， 而更接近一种不坏、不动、不被污染的真实之心。从这个角度回头看，这尊造像 为什么温柔，也就不难理解了。因为它真正要完成的，不是威慑，而是转化。不 是压服，而是照亮。 如果再把它放回西藏造像史中看，它所呈现出的，又是另一条很值得注意的脉络。 从造型语言看，这件作品明显已经进入了受尼泊尔艺术深刻影响的藏地审美系统。 13 至 14 世纪间，尼泊尔纽瓦尔艺术语言广泛进入西藏，并在很长一段时间里深 刻塑造了藏地绘画与雕塑的面貌；夏鲁寺及其相关艺术遗存，正是这一系统最重 要的代表之一。Rubin Museum 在关于夏鲁寺壁画的研究指出，14 世纪夏鲁寺的 重要项目，就是由尼泊尔与西藏工匠共同完成的；而 Himalayan Art 也指出，纽 瓦尔绘画风格进入西藏后，长期成为极具影响力的一路视觉传统。因此，可以理 解为：它属于那一路受尼泊尔艺术深刻浸润，并在藏地被重新组织之后形成的审 美体系。而这件作品最值得看的，恰恰是它如何把这种外来艺术语言，变成了一 种极有内在力量的形体。 首先，是整体体块的组织。这尊金刚萨埵给人的第一印象，不是轻，不是巧，而 是结实。从肩膀到膝部，形体的转折带着明显的方整感；肩部开阔，胸廓厚实， 肢体的体量不是圆软地过渡，而更像由一块块稳定的面构成。这种“方”的语言 很重要。因为它直接建立起一种支撑感：像地基稳，像骨架硬，像内在已经立住。 但奇妙的是，这种结实并没有把造像拖向笨重。恰恰相反，它又带着一股很轻的 神气。头部微微偏转，手中金刚杵与铃的位置关系，使静坐中生出细微的流动； 面庞圆润，额头饱满，眼形细长而略垂，刻线温和，不锋利，却极清秀。于是， 方整的身体和清朗的脸，就形成了一种非常高明的张力：形体是稳的，神情却是 轻的。 再往下看，五叶宝冠、宽大的耳垂、圆中带方的脸型、额髻与眼形的关系，都让 这件作品显出一种很典型的藏地、尼泊尔融合趣味。这里最重要的，不是装饰有 多少，而是“繁”与“简”的比例被控制得极好。臂钏、璎珞、裙边连珠纹，处 处都精致；但这些细节没有把整体切碎，反而都服务于那种庄严、明朗、稳定的 总气氛。底座同样如此。莲瓣舒展，轮廓清晰，下承连珠纹，节奏感很强。它不 是简单地“托住”造像，而更像是在帮助整尊像呼吸。于是你会感觉，这件作品 虽然结实，却不凝滞；虽然厚重，却不沉闷。动人的，还有那两条从脑后轻轻扬 起的飘带。如果没有它们，这尊像会是一件强壮、非常稳定的作品；可正因为有 了这两道轻薄的线，整个作品才突然有了“风”。风一出现，神圣场域也就随之 出现。它像在提醒你：这不只是一个被固定在金属里的身体，而是一位正从空性 之中显现出来的本尊。这也正是我为什么会说，它是“忿怒中的温柔者”。它的 “金刚”，体现在方整、稳定、不动摇；它的“温柔”，则体现在神情、飘带、 微笑以及那种不压迫人的清朗。它让人意识到：真正的净化，从来不是靠外力的 教训完成，而是靠你愿不愿意把心里那些遮蔽带到光里来。 所以，这尊金刚萨埵真正要面对的，从来不是某个外在对象，而是人的内心。那 些创伤、执念、反复循环的情绪、迟迟无法解开的结，若始终不被照见，就会形 成障碍；而一旦愿意转身面对，愿意承认，愿意让它暴露在光中，那份勇气本身， 就会开始转化。 因此，金刚萨埵的意义，放在今天，它依然成立。因为“障碍”并没有消失，它 只是换成了更现代的形式：情绪惯性、自我否定、反复内耗、无休止的羞耻与恐 惧。金刚萨埵存在提醒我们：清净不是后来才获得的东西，而是原本就在，只是 被遮蔽了。也正因如此，这件作品最后真正打动人的，并不是工艺本身，而是它 通过工艺所显现出来的那种精神结构——坚实，却不僵硬；有力，却不粗暴；能 够直面黑暗，却始终带着光。 它带有的精神性似乎也在讲述着：即使你曾迷失、曾混乱、曾偏离方向，也并不 意味着你失去了本性中的清净。你只是需要，有勇气把那些雾，一层层拨开。真 正坚不可摧的，不是盔甲，而是一个人愿意直面自己时，内心生出的那道光。
viewingNote.en: Upon hearing the word "Vajra," the image that often springs to mind for many is one of sharpness, hardness, wrath, or even sheer intimidation. Yet, the figure of Vajrasattva standing before us presents a stark contrast. He sits in unhurried composure, his expression serene and clear—neither ostentatious nor overbearing. If I were compelled to summarize his most captivating quality, I would describe him as a "Gentle One amidst Wrath." This may sound like a contradiction. Yet, it is precisely this contradiction that constitutes the true core of Vajrasattva. To understand this work of art, one must first ask: Who, exactly, is Vajrasattva? In Tibetan Buddhism, Vajrasattva is one of the most significant deities; his primary function is centered on "purifying karmic obstacles and cleansing negative karma." Within the esoteric traditions, Vajrasattva is widely revered as a principal deity for meditative practice aimed at purifying moral impurities and afflictions. Furthermore, within the higher echelons of the esoteric system, Vajradhara is understood as the Ultimate Dharmakaya—the Primordial Buddha of Vajrayana Buddhism. Vajrasattva, conversely, is often regarded as a manifestation closely linked to Vajradhara—an incarnate form upon which practitioners can directly rely and through which they can engage in spiritual practice. While Vajradhara is viewed as the Primordial Buddha of the Vajrayana path, Vajrasattva is understood as one of his "inner forms." To clarify this relationship further: Vajradhara is akin to "Ultimate Truth" itself; Vajrasattva, however, represents that very Truth as it enters the process of spiritual practice—taking a form that can be visualized, relied upon, and touched through inner experience. In other words: Vajradhara is the Sun itself; Vajrasattva is the light that breaks through the clouds to warm the human heart. It is for this very reason that the significance of Vajrasattva lies not in "strength," but in "purity." The adversaries He confronts are not external enemies, but rather the obscurations residing within the human heart. He does not come to help people escape their afflictions, but rather to empower them with the courage to face those afflictions directly, to purify them, and to see through them. Thus, the term "Vajra" here does not equate to mere anger; rather, it more closely signifies a true heart—one that is indestructible, unshakable, and untainted. Viewed from this perspective, it is not difficult to understand why this particular sculpture appears so gentle. For its true objective is not intimidation, but transformation; not subjugation, but illumination. If we place this piece back within the broader history of Tibetan sculpture, it reveals yet another noteworthy artistic lineage. In terms of stylistic language, this work clearly belongs to a Tibetan aesthetic system that had come under the profound influence of Nepalese art. Between the 13th and 14th centuries, the artistic idiom of Nepal’s Newar people spread widely throughout Xizang, profoundly shaping the character of Tibetan painting and sculpture for a considerable period; Shalu Monastery—along with its associated artistic heritage—stands as one of the most significant exemplars of this very system. Research conducted by the Rubin Museum regarding the murals at Shalu Monastery notes that the major artistic projects undertaken there in the 14th century were executed jointly by Nepalese and Tibetan artisans; similarly, the *Himalayan Art* resource points out that, following its arrival in Xizang, the Newar painting style evolved into a highly influential visual tradition that endured for a long time. Consequently, this piece can be understood as belonging to an aesthetic system that was deeply imbued with Nepalese artistic sensibilities, yet subsequently reinterpreted and reconfigured within the Tibetan cultural context. What makes this particular work most compelling is precisely the manner in which it transforms this imported artistic idiom into a physical form imbued with immense inner power. First and foremost is the organization of its overall mass. The initial impression conveyed by this figure of Vajrasattva is neither lightness nor delicacy, but rather solidity. From the shoulders down to the knees, the transitions of the form possess a distinct sense of angularity and structure; the shoulders are broad, the chest robust, and the limbs do not transition with a soft, rounded fluidity, but appear instead to be composed of a series of stable, planar surfaces. This "angular" visual language is of great significance, for it directly establishes a sense of structural support—evoking the stability of a solid foundation, the rigidity of a sturdy skeleton, and the steadfastness of an inner core that stands firm. Yet, remarkably, this solidity does not drag the sculpture down into a state of ponderous heaviness. On the contrary, it retains an air of distinct lightness and spiritual grace. With the head tilted ever so slightly—and the specific spatial relationship between the *vajra* and bell held in the hands—a subtle sense of flow emerges from the seated posture. The face is rounded, the forehead full; the eyes are slender and slightly downcast, delineated by lines that are gentle—never sharp—yet possess a distinct and exquisite elegance. Thus, the square-set body and the serene, luminous face combine to create a tension of remarkable sophistication: the physical form is grounded and stable, while the expression remains light and ethereal. Looking further down, the five-lobed crown, the distended earlobes, the face shape—which is at once rounded and angular—and the interplay between the cranial knot and the eyes all imbue this piece with a quintessential aesthetic blending Tibetan and Nepalese artistic traditions. What matters most here is not the sheer quantity of ornamentation, but rather the masterful balance struck between "complexity" and "simplicity." The armbands, the *hāra* necklaces, and the beaded borders of the skirt are exquisite in every detail; yet, these details do not fragment the whole; instead, they serve to reinforce the overarching atmosphere of solemnity, clarity, and stability. The base functions in precisely the same way. The lotus petals unfurl gracefully with crisp contours, resting upon a band of continuous beading that creates a strong sense of rhythmic vitality. It does not merely "support" the statue; rather, it seems to help the entire figure breathe. Consequently, one senses that while this work is robust, it is never static; and while substantial, it is never ponderous. Equally captivating are the two ribbons that flutter gently upward from behind the head. Without them, the statue would remain a powerful, immovably stable work of art; yet, precisely because of these two slender, ethereal lines, the entire composition is suddenly imbued with the presence of "wind." And with the arrival of the wind comes the manifestation of a sacred field. It serves as a reminder: this is not merely a body fixed within metal, but a deity emerging into manifestation from the very heart of Emptiness. This is precisely why I describe it as "the Gentle One amidst the Wrathful." Its "Vajra" nature is embodied in its square-set form, its stability, and its unwavering steadfastness; its "gentleness," conversely, is revealed in its expression, the fluttering ribbons, its smile, and that luminous clarity which never feels oppressive. It compels us to realize that true purification is never achieved through the external imposition of moral lessons, but rather depends entirely upon our own willingness to bring the veils and obscurations within our hearts out into the light. Thus, what this figure of Vajrasattva truly seeks to confront is never merely some external object, but rather the human heart itself. Those traumas, obsessions, cyclical emotions, and knots that stubbornly refuse to unravel—if left unexamined, they inevitably crystallize into obstacles. Yet, the moment one is willing to turn and face them—willing to acknowledge them, willing to let them be exposed to the light—that very act of courage initiates a process of transformation. Consequently, the significance of Vajrasattva remains just as valid today as it ever was. For "obstacles" have not vanished; they have merely assumed more modern guises: emotional inertia, self-negation, ceaseless internal strife, and an unending cycle of shame and fear. Vajrasattva exists to remind us that purity is not something acquired belatedly, but rather an intrinsic quality that has always resided within us—merely obscured from view. It is precisely for this reason that what ultimately moves the viewer in this artwork is not the craftsmanship itself, but rather the spiritual architecture revealed *through* that craftsmanship—solid, yet never rigid; powerful, yet never brutal; capable of staring darkness in the face, yet always radiating light. The spirituality embodied in this piece seems to whisper a message: even if you have been lost, confused, or have strayed from your path, this does not signify that you have forfeited the innate purity of your true nature. You simply require the courage to part the mists, layer by layer. For what is truly indestructible is not a suit of armor, but rather that inner light which ignites within a person the moment they find the courage to face themselves.

excerpt.en: A Bihar image of Avalokiteshvara shaped by Pala elegance and devotional calm.

viewingNote.zh: 如果要在印度晚期佛教艺术中寻找一个真正的高峰，帕拉王朝几乎无法绕开。 8 世纪中叶，瞿波罗王建立帕拉王朝，其版图大致覆盖今天印度东部的比哈尔邦、 西孟加拉邦以及孟加拉国一带。此后四百余年间，历代君王持续护持佛教，使这 一时期成为印度佛教发展的最后一个黄金时代。菩提迦耶的大菩提寺、那烂陀的 大僧院，都在这一阶段继续扩展和兴盛；而飞行寺、超戒寺等重要寺院的建立， 也使大乘佛教与密教在此并行发展、相互激荡。来自亚洲各地的朝圣僧侣、留学 僧和佛教信徒汇集于此，使帕拉王朝不仅成为佛法传播的中心，也成为佛教造像 艺术最成熟、最精密的生产地之一。也正是在这样的背景下，眼前这尊 11 世纪 的嵌银嵌红铜世尊观音，才显得格外重要。它并不仅仅是一件造像精品，更像是 帕拉王朝成熟期审美、工艺与宗教观念共同凝结出的一个典范性答案。 观世音菩萨在佛教传统中具有三十三种应化身，世尊观世音即其一。此尊造像最 令人惊叹的地方，在于它并不是单凭某一个局部取胜，而是从整体气韵到细部刻 画，都呈现出极高完成度。合金青铜在长久流传中逐渐形成了细润而温厚的表面 质感，使整个造像在金属固有的坚实之外，又生出一种几乎近于肌肤的柔和光泽。 正是这种经过时间沉淀后的温润，使观音本应超越世俗的圣洁感，被转化成了一 种可感、可亲、可被凝视的在场。 若从头部开始细看，这尊观音的身份与时代特征都极为明确。菩萨头戴小三叶冠， 冠缘嵌银，并以连珠纹精细装饰，层次分明而富于秩序。发髻高耸、发丝盘绕， 整体偏扁平，盘束的发辫一股一股清晰可辨，如麻绳般紧密缠绕，显出极强的塑 造控制力。发髻正前方化现一尊阿弥陀佛，这一细节不仅点明了观音的身份，也 明确了其与莲花部的归属关系。耳侧的扇形冠结横向展开，缯带向上翻扬，带有 鲜明的装饰性，却并未破坏整体的庄严，反而在稳定中增添了向上的生气。 再看面部，这尊观音几乎集中体现了成熟帕拉造像最迷人的开脸方式。额间长方 形白毫、嵌银双目、嵌红铜双唇，与近黄铜色的面部肌肤形成了极其精妙的色彩 对比。这种材质上的综合色，并非仅为了华丽，而是在逼真、神圣与装饰之间取 得一种平衡。高挑如弓的双眉末端与鼻梁自然相连，鼻部高挺，嘴唇丰厚而微微 向中间收束，面庞既有南亚人种特征的写实依据，又经过理想化提炼，形成一种 既真实又超越现实的神性容貌。换句话说，这张脸并不只是在“塑造观音”，而是 在回答一个更深的问题：一尊菩萨应当如何既保有人的温度，又显出超越人的光 辉？ 身体部分的处理，同样是帕拉造像成熟时期的重要标志。观音双肩宽阔，胸部厚 实，双臂圆润，整个躯体挺拔而不僵硬。匠人并没有依赖夸张的装饰来掩盖形体， 而是通过打磨与收面，直接塑出胸肌、小腹与四肢起伏的轮廓，使人体结构清晰 而自然。这里最值得注意的是：这种自然写实，并不是对现实肉身的被动复制， 而是一种带有古典主义意味的理想化处理。身体既健硕又节制，既有力量又无粗 粝，显示出匠人对“菩萨之身”这一概念的深刻理解——它必须比现实更完整、更 稳定，也更能承载神圣性。 菩萨胸前佩戴单层嵌银项链，下方为嵌宝石璎珞，双臂饰菱形臂钏；胸前巾帛、 圣索、腰带、裙边，则分别以嵌银、嵌红铜与阴线刻画配合完成。这里的高明之 处，在于装饰虽多，却没有一处显得多余。每一种材质都承担着不同的视觉功能： 银使轮廓清亮，红铜使局部生动，而阴线则负责唤起贴体衣料的柔软感。于是， 装饰不再只是附着于身体之上的华美，而成为形体节奏的一部分。 观音左手执莲，右手轻垂膝前施予愿印，这一组合意义非常明确：一边是莲花象 征的清净，一边是愿印所表达的允诺与救度。菩萨并非高踞于不可触及之处，而 是以一种留驻世间、主动垂顾的姿态面对众生。两侧花枝蜿蜒向上，于肩头绽放， 既强化了观音与莲部的关系，又使整尊造像在稳定之中生出一种生命正在伸展的 活力。在以花枝、缯带、手印与目光共同表达一种“向外流动的慈悲”。 下方的仰覆式莲座，同样值得反复观看。上下缘连珠纹颗粒饱满，排列严谨，尤 其底层连珠夸张而丰厚，几乎像一颗颗独立镶嵌而成，形成极强的视觉节奏。莲 瓣满饰一周，平整而舒展，上下错落有致；在莲瓣与莲台之间，又嵌有三圈红铜， 使整个底座在金属色调的对比中更富层次。莲台背面的两行古梵文，字体娟秀而 劲健，进一步说明这不是一件普通作品，而是一件带有明确宗教身份与供养背景 的高规格作品。 如果将这些局部重新收回到整体中，就会发现，这尊世尊观音之所以能成为帕拉 成熟期的典型，并不只是因为它符合若干风格特征，而是因为它把帕拉造像最本 质的几个问题，都处理得非常完整：其一，如何在写实与神圣之间取得平衡；其 二，如何在华丽装饰中维持整体秩序；其三，如何让慈悲不是抽象概念，而是通 过形体、神情与姿态真实显现出来。 也正因为如此，这尊观音的气质并不单一。它有健硕挺拔的躯体，因此显出坚定 与英勇；有高弓般的双眉、微扬的嘴角与微含的下颌，因此又带着一种沉静而深 邃的悲悯；如山高耸的发髻与向上翻动的缯带，使寂静相中又生出肃穆与威仪； 而花枝与蓓蕾的柔软，则像将无边之爱化作甘露，柔软且悄然地洒向世间。诸多 看似不同的力量，被匠人组织成一个整体，于是这尊造像最终呈现出的，便不是 单一情绪，而是一种异乎寻常的寂静力量。所谓“寂静力量”，并不是无声无息， 而是无需夸张张扬，便已具足穿透人心的分量。 所以，当我们站在这尊世尊观音面前时，看到的并不只是一件 11 世纪的青铜造 像。我们真正看到的，是印度佛教最后黄金时代如何把自己的审美、信仰、工艺 与理想人格，压缩进一尊像中；也看到，一位伟大的匠人如何用金属、宝石、线 条与比例，让“慈悲”不再只是被诵读的词语，而成为可以被目光直接感知的形体。 附注： 铜像基座后部的莲瓣上镌刻梵文铭文一则，根据卡锡纳特.塔摩特（Kashinath Tamot）的 释读，该铭文以古俱胝罗字体（Kutila script）的变体书写，应属北印度地域化城体（Nāgar ī script）的一种，为公元 11 世纪前后所流行。1 内容具体如下： “देवव_गय_ झांव_लक नकष पू_यपाहवकस ।।”； 罗马字母转写：Devavaggarya Jhāṃmveṃlika nikaṣa pūṇyapālhalokasa ||； 汉译：“庞雅波罗洛迦舍向（圣）观自在菩萨敬顶礼”。 关于铭文中提及的人名“庞雅波罗洛迦舍（pūṇyapālhalokasa）”，其身份与生活年 代暂不可考，但可推测其应为来自印度的一名僧人。尤需关注的是铭文中对观音菩萨的称谓 并未采用梵文传统中的“Avalokiteśvara”，而是借用了藏语称谓“坚惹色”（ སྤाན་ास་གस་ा , Chenrizik），并经对音转写为梵文“Jhāṃmveṃlika”后再行刻写。2 显而易见，此乃充分 考虑到藏语中对于观音菩萨梵文称谓的发音困难而采取的方法，带有强烈的面向藏族传统的 趋同性。在目前经过已经译读的波罗风格造像铭文中，这是十分罕见的案例，仅在西藏林周 县杰拉康所发现的波罗风格石刻中存在类似情况。3 我们亦可由此断定，此尊铜像应是印度 施主专为西藏供奉所需而造。 —— 摘自马美美文章 注释: 1. 此处参照 Kashinath Tamot 所提供的铭文释读结果：An Inscription of a Padmapāṇi Avalokiteśvara, 5 October 2020. Personal communication. 2. 前揭注释 1。 3. 杰拉康弥勒菩萨石像铭文同样采用藏文转写梵文的方式镌刻，根据夏吾卡先观点， 杰拉康弥勒菩萨石像应为藏族匠师于西藏本地雕制。见夏吾卡先: 《西藏林周县杰拉康帕拉 风格石雕造像与碑刻的调查研究》。成都：四川大学出版社，《藏学学刊》第 22 辑，2020 年第一期。页 81-98，图版 4-5。
viewingNote.en: If one seeks to identify a true pinnacle within the realm of late Buddhist art in India, the Pala Dynasty is a period that simply cannot be overlooked. In the mid-8th century, King Gopala established the Pala Dynasty, a realm whose territory roughly encompassed the regions corresponding to present-day Bihar and West Bengal in eastern India, as well as Bangladesh. Over the ensuing four centuries, successive monarchs consistently patronized and protected the Buddhist faith, thereby ushering in what would become the final golden age of Buddhism in India. During this era, major institutions such as the Mahabodhi Temple in Bodh Gaya and the Great Monastery at Nalanda continued to expand and flourish; furthermore, the establishment of pivotal monastic centers—such as Vikramashila and Odantapuri—facilitated the parallel development and dynamic interplay of Mahayana and Vajrayana Buddhism. Pilgrims, visiting scholars, and devotees from across Asia converged upon this region, transforming the Pala realm into not only a central hub for the dissemination of the Dharma but also one of the most sophisticated and refined centers for the production of Buddhist iconography. It is precisely against this historical backdrop that the 11th-century figure before us—a silver- and copper-inlaid depiction of *Bhagavan Avalokiteshvara*—assumes such extraordinary significance. It stands as far more than merely a masterpiece of sculptural art; rather, it serves as an exemplary embodiment—a definitive answer—crystallizing the aesthetic sensibilities, technical craftsmanship, and religious ideals characteristic of the Pala Dynasty’s mature period. Within the Buddhist tradition, the Bodhisattva Avalokiteshvara is said to manifest in thirty-three different incarnate forms; *Bhagavan Avalokiteshvara* represents one such manifestation. The most astonishing quality of this particular sculpture lies in the fact that its excellence does not derive from any single isolated detail; rather, it demonstrates an exceptional degree of artistic perfection across the entire spectrum—from its overarching spiritual aura (*qiyun*) to the meticulous rendering of its minute details. Through the passage of time and centuries of handling, the bronze alloy has acquired a surface texture that is at once smooth, lustrous, and imbued with a gentle warmth. This quality endows the sculpture with a softness—almost akin to the texture of human skin—that transcends the inherent rigidity of the metal itself. It is precisely this mellow patina—the product of time’s gentle refinement—that transforms Avalokiteshvara’s inherent sanctity (a quality typically perceived as transcending the mundane world) into a palpable, approachable, and intimately present reality—one that invites and sustains our contemplative gaze. Upon closer examination—beginning with the head—both the specific identity of this Avalokiteshvara figure and the stylistic characteristics of its historical era are revealed with striking clarity. The Bodhisattva wears a small, three-lobed crown. The rim of the crown is inlaid with silver and exquisitely adorned with a continuous pearl motif—a design characterized by distinct layers and a sense of perfect order. The hair is gathered into a towering topknot, with individual strands coiled and intertwined; though the overall profile is somewhat flattened, the bound braids remain clearly discernible—tightly wound like hemp rope—demonstrating a mastery of sculptural control. Centered directly at the front of the topknot appears a small manifestation of Amitabha Buddha; this detail not only identifies the figure as Avalokiteśvara but also explicitly establishes his affiliation with the Lotus Family. Flanking the ears, fan-shaped crown ties spread outward horizontally, with their ribbons sweeping upward—a distinct decorative flourish that, far from disrupting the figure's overall solemnity, instead infuses its stability with a sense of upward-soaring vitality. Turning to the face, this figure of Avalokiteśvara embodies nearly every captivating facial convention characteristic of the mature Pala sculptural style. A rectangular *urna* (tuft of hair) adorns the forehead, while the eyes are inlaid with silver and the lips with red copper—elements that create a remarkably subtle chromatic contrast against the brass-like hue of the facial skin. This interplay of materials and colors serves a purpose beyond mere opulence; it strikes a delicate balance between realism, divinity, and ornamentation. The high, arching eyebrows flow naturally into the bridge of the nose, which is tall and prominent; the lips are full yet taper slightly toward the center. The face draws upon realistic anthropological features characteristic of South Asia, yet undergoes an idealized refinement, resulting in a divine countenance that is simultaneously grounded in reality and transcendent of it. In other words, this face does not merely "depict Avalokiteśvara"; rather, it answers a profound question: How can a Bodhisattva retain the warmth of humanity while simultaneously radiating a splendor that transcends the human realm? The treatment of the body serves as another hallmark of the mature Pala sculptural period. Avalokiteśvara possesses broad shoulders, a robust chest, and rounded arms; the entire torso stands tall and erect, yet remains free of any stiffness. The artisan did not rely on exaggerated ornamentation to obscure the underlying form; instead, through meticulous polishing and surface modeling, they directly sculpted the contours of the pectoral muscles, the subtle undulations of the abdomen, and the curves of the limbs—rendering the human anatomy with both clarity and naturalness. Most noteworthy here is that this naturalistic realism is not a passive replication of the physical body as it exists in reality, but rather an idealized treatment imbued with a classical sensibility. The body appears at once robust and restrained—powerful yet devoid of coarseness—demonstrating the craftsman’s profound understanding of the concept of the "Bodhisattva’s Body": it must be more complete, more stable, and more capable of embodying the sacred than any physical form found in the real world. Across the Bodhisattva’s chest rests a single-strand necklace inlaid with silver, beneath which hangs a jeweled *Yingluo* (decorative garland); the upper arms are adorned with diamond-shaped armbands. The drapery across the chest, the sacred cord, the waist sash, and the hem of the skirt are rendered through a combination of silver inlay, red copper inlay, and incised line engraving. The brilliance of this execution lies in the fact that, despite the abundance of ornamentation, not a single element appears superfluous. Each material serves a distinct visual function: silver lends clarity to the contours, red copper enlivens specific details, while the incised lines evoke the tactile softness of fabric clinging to the body. Thus, the ornamentation ceases to be mere embellishment superimposed upon the form; instead, it becomes an integral part of the figure’s rhythmic composition. Guanyin holds a lotus in the left hand, while the right hand hangs gently before the knee, forming the *Varada Mudra* (Gesture of Granting Wishes). The significance of this combination is unequivocal: on one side stands the purity symbolized by the lotus; on the other, the promise of deliverance and salvation expressed by the *Mudra*. The Bodhisattva does not sit aloof in an inaccessible realm, but rather faces sentient beings with a posture of abiding in this world and actively bestowing compassionate regard. Flowing upward from both sides, floral branches blossom at the shoulders, simultaneously reinforcing Guanyin’s affiliation with the Lotus Pantheon and infusing the entire sculpture—otherwise grounded in stability—with the vibrant vitality of life in full expansion. Through the interplay of floral branches, flowing ribbons, hand gestures, and gaze, the figure collectively articulates a "compassion that flows outward." The inverted-and-everted lotus pedestal upon which the figure rests is likewise worthy of repeated contemplation. The borders along the upper and lower edges feature a "continuous pearl" motif—rendered with plump, full beads arranged in rigorous precision. The lowest tier, in particular, displays an exaggerated richness; the beads appear almost as if they were individually set gemstones, creating a powerful visual rhythm. Lotus The lotus petals, arranged in a full circle, are flat yet gracefully unfurled, staggered in a harmonious, rhythmic pattern. Between the petals and the lotus pedestal, three bands of red copper are inlaid, enriching the entire base with a heightened sense of depth through the interplay of contrasting metallic tones. On the reverse side of the pedestal, two lines of ancient Sanskrit—rendered in a script that is at once delicate and vigorous—further attest that this is no ordinary work; rather, it is a piece of exceptional caliber, imbued with a distinct religious identity and a specific context of devotional offering. When these individual details are viewed once again within the context of the whole, one realizes that this figure of the World-Honored Avalokiteśvara stands as a quintessential exemplar of the mature Pala period not merely because it conforms to a checklist of stylistic traits, but because it masterfully resolves several of the most fundamental challenges inherent to Pala iconography: First, how to strike a balance between realism and the divine; second, how to maintain overall structural order amidst lavish ornamentation; and third, how to ensure that compassion remains not merely an abstract concept, but is instead tangibly manifested through form, facial expression, and posture. It is precisely for this reason that the aura of this Avalokiteśvara figure is far from monolithic. Its robust, upright physique conveys a sense of steadfastness and valor; its high, arching brows, slightly upturned lips, and gently lowered chin imbue it with a profound, tranquil compassion; the towering, mountain-like topknot and the fluttering ribbons rising skyward lend an air of solemnity and majestic dignity to its otherwise serene countenance; while the softness of the floral branches and budding blossoms seems to transform boundless love into celestial nectar—gently and quietly sprinkling it upon the world. These myriad, seemingly disparate forces have been harmonized by the artisan into a unified whole; consequently, the final impression conveyed by this sculpture is not that of a singular emotion, but rather that of an extraordinary, tranquil power. This "tranquil power" is not to be confused with mere silence or stillness; rather, it signifies a presence that—without the need for exaggeration or ostentation—possesses the profound weight and resonance to penetrate the very depths of the human heart. Thus, when we stand before this World-Honored Avalokiteśvara, what we behold is far more than merely an 11th-century bronze sculpture. What we truly behold here is how the final golden age of Indian Buddhism distilled its aesthetics, beliefs, craftsmanship, and ideal of human character into a single sculptural form; we also witness how a master artisan, utilizing metal, gemstones, lines, and proportions, transformed "compassion" from a mere recited word into a tangible form that can be directly perceived by the eye. Note: An inscription in Sanskrit is engraved upon the lotus petals at the rear of the bronze statue's pedestal. According to the interpretation of Kashinath Tamot, this inscription is written in a variant of the ancient Kutila script—specifically, a regionalized form of the Nāgarī script prevalent in Northern India around the 11th century CE.1 The specific content reads as follows: “देवव_गय_ झांव_लक नकष पू_यपाहवकस ।。”; Romanized Transliteration: Devavaggarya Jhāṃmveṃlika nikaṣa pūṇyapālhalokasa ||; Chinese Translation: "Puṇyapālhalokasa reverently bows in homage to the (Holy) Avalokiteśvara Bodhisattva." Regarding the personal name mentioned in the inscription—"Puṇyapālhalokasa"—his identity and dates of life remain currently unverified, though it may be surmised that he was a Buddhist monk hailing from India. Of particular note is the fact that the inscription, in referring to Avalokiteśvara Bodhisattva, does not employ the traditional Sanskrit appellation "Avalokiteśvara"; instead, it adopts the Tibetan appellation "Chenrezig" (སྤ་ན་ར་ས་གཟིགས་), which was then phonetically transcribed into the Sanskrit form "Jhāṃmveṃlika" before being engraved.2 Evidently, this approach was adopted to fully accommodate the phonetic difficulties inherent in pronouncing the Sanskrit name of Avalokiteśvara within the Tibetan linguistic context, thereby demonstrating a strong tendency toward convergence with Tibetan traditions. Among the Pala-style sculptural inscriptions deciphered to date, this represents an exceptionally rare instance; a similar phenomenon is observed only in the Pala-style stone carvings discovered at Jielakang in Lhunzhub County, Tibet.3 We may therefore conclude that this bronze statue was commissioned by an Indian patron specifically for the purpose of religious offering in Tibet. — Excerpted from an article by Ma Meimei Notes: 1. This refers to the inscription transcription provided by Kashinath Tamot: *An Inscription of a Padmapāṇi Avalokiteśvara*, October 5, 2020. Personal communication. 2. *Ibid.* (see Note 1). 3. The inscription on the stone statue of Maitreya Bodhisattva at Jielakang was likewise engraved using the method of transcribing Sanskrit into the Tibetan script. According to Xiawu Kaxian, this stone statue of Maitreya Bodhisattva at Jielakang was likely sculpted by Tibetan artisans locally within Tibet. See Xiawu Kaxian, "An Investigation and Study of Pala-style Stone Sculptures and Inscriptions at Jielakang in Lhunzhub County, Tibet," *Journal of Tibetan Studies*, Issue 22 (No. 1, 2020), pp. 81–98, Plates 4–5. Chengdu: Sichuan University Press.

excerpt.en: A compact Hevajra bronze of rhythmic silhouette and controlled force.

viewingNote.zh: 一尊金刚像，为什么会让人同时感到柔和与锋利？那么，这种复杂的视觉经验究 竟从何而来？ 要回答这个问题，首先要先确认：这尊像，到底是谁。 如果从图像特征出发，这件作品的身份其实非常明确。高耸如火焰般的发髻，其 上刻有十字金刚杵；头戴五叶概念化处理的骷髅冠；一手高举金刚杵，一手捧持 颅碗，肩头倚着金刚杖；身上披挂人头蔓，整个身体仿佛正在虚空中旋舞。这些 特征，共同指向一个非常鲜明的密教本尊：喜金刚。 喜金刚是藏传佛教无上瑜伽部最重要的本尊之一，在萨迦派体系中尤其核心，《喜 金刚续》更是其根本经典。这里的“喜”，并不是日常意义上的欢喜，而是“大 乐”；“金刚”也不只是武器，而象征坚不可摧的智慧、本觉与空性。因此，“喜 金刚”的真正含义，并不是一个可怕的忿怒神，而是：通过不可摧毁的智慧，抵 达究竟的大乐。也就是说，祂的形象虽然强烈，但祂所指向的，并不是暴力，而 是一种最高意义上的觉悟状态。这一点非常重要。因为如果不明白这一层，我们 就很容易误把祂的忿怒理解成“威吓”；可事实上，密教中的忿怒，本质上是一 种破执的力量。它不是为了伤害众生，而是为了击碎众生最深的无明与执著。所 以，喜金刚之所以会显得锋利，不是因为祂愤怒，而是因为祂要对抗的，正是最 难被拔除的自我幻觉。 理解喜金刚，第二个关键问题，是祂为什么总以一种强烈的动态出现。在密教语 境中，舞蹈从来不只是动作。它是一种极高强度的象征语言。本尊的舞，不是娱 乐性的，也不是装饰性的，而是一种“觉悟之舞”。这意味着什么？意味着智慧 并不是僵死的。它不是冻结在某个固定的姿态里，而是在流转中显现，在运动中 展开。所谓“动中证空”，正是这个意思：空性不是停止一切之后才出现，而是 在变化本身中，被真正看见。 从这个角度说，喜金刚之所以舞动，不是因为祂不安定，恰恰是因为祂已经彻底 自由。祂不再被固定的身份、不变的形体或单一的存在方式所束缚，所以祂能在 变化中，直接显现智慧。这也就是为什么，当我们看着这尊像时，会同时感到“梦 幻”与“真实”。因为它并不是在演示一个动作，而是在把“无常、流动、变化” 和“觉悟”同时做成形体。 如果继续往下看，就会来到这类本尊最容易被误解的部分。颅碗、人头蔓、骷髅 冠——这些元素看起来极具冲击力，也很容易让人把它理解为某种“可怕的图 像”。但在密教里，它们从来不是为了制造惊悚效果。它们真正指向的，是一个 非常直接、也非常残酷的事实：一切执著终将败坏，一切身份终将瓦解，一切“我” 的坚固感都并不真实。所以，那些头颅并不是在吓人，而是在提醒人：你所紧抓 不放的东西——身份、欲望、骄傲、羞耻、恐惧——最终都会消散。若不正面面 对这种无常，人就始终被困在幻觉里。也正因为如此，喜金刚的忿怒并不是对外 的， 而是对“幻相”的。祂要击碎的，不是众生，而是众生错认为真实的一切遮蔽。 所以，图像中的“凶”并不是目的，而是一种极其彻底的慈悲。因为只有真正敢 于把幻觉撕开，人才有可能接近真实。 现在，我们再回到雕塑本身。 如果说图像学解决的是“祂是谁”，那么雕塑语言真正解决的，是：为什么它会 以这种方式进入我们的感官。这尊喜金刚最先给人的印象，其实非常明确：它是 结实的。无论从肩膀到膝盖的转折，还是肌肉与体块的组织方式，你都能明显感 到一种偏方形、偏块面的塑造语言。肩膀方正，躯干饱满，身体的力量不是散开 的，而是被收进了一个非常稳定的结构里。这种“方”，不是僵硬，而是一种支 撑力。它让整尊像立得住，也让那种向上的冲劲有了着力点。但奇妙的是，匠人 并没有让这种结实把作品做得沉闷。恰恰相反，它又充满了“风”的感觉。 头部轻轻偏转，金刚杵与颅碗形成方向性的拉力，脑后轻薄的飘带与身体的线条 一起，把整个造像从“稳”推向了“动”。于是，观者会产生一种很特别的错觉： 它明明单脚立于支点之上，却仿佛随时会腾空而起。这就是这件作品最高明的地 方。 它没有把力量藏起来，而是让力量沿着身体、沿着线条、沿着动作，直接向外迸 发。但这种迸发又不是失控的，因为下方的支撑与整体构图始终把它稳稳收住。 所以，这件作品真正完成的，不只是“动态”，而是动势中的平衡。这也是为什 么，它会让人既觉得有攻击性，又觉得如此美。因为攻击性来自向上的爆发，而 美，则来自匠人始终没有让这股力量散掉。 说到这里，就进入了这件作品最微妙的部分。 为什么我们面对这件喜金刚时，会觉得它既真实、又像幻影？既有形体的冲击， 又像要从形体中消失？因为这尊造像的形式语言，与它所代表的精神内容高度一 致。它的线条是流动的，姿态是变幻的，重心看似不稳，却始终维持着一种几乎 不可能的平衡。这种感觉，会让人自然联想到“幻相”本身：看似具体，实则不 住；看似坚固，实则流转。而这，恰恰与密教中“空性”的表达方式不谋而合。 空性并不是空无一物，而是说：一切都在因缘中成立，一切都处在变化中，没有 任何东西能以固定、自性的方式永恒存在。所以，真正能够表达空性的形象，往 往不会是死板的、封闭的、僵硬的。它必须带着某种不稳定的稳定，某种看似即 将消散、却又异常真实的状态。 喜金刚的这尊造像，正是如此。它让你看到：形体当然存在，但形体并不封闭； 力量当然存在，但力量并不僵死。它的一切都在“流”，而正是在这种流动中， 智慧与觉悟被真正显现出来。所以，它的“梦幻感”并不是柔弱的，而是一种极 具力量的幻。不是逃离现实，而是把现实最深的真相——无常、变化、空性—— 直接以视觉形式推到你面前。 如果进一步推，就会发现：这件作品之所以如此有冲击力，还和雕塑这种媒介本 身有关。绘画是平面的，依赖线、色与构图组织视觉秩序；而雕塑是立体的，它 不仅要成立于正面，还要成立于侧面、背面和整个空间关系之中。所以，雕塑的 语言从来不只是“像”，而是通过体量、比例、节奏和空间张力，让精神真正长 出身体。这一点在佛教造像中尤其重要。因为佛教雕塑并不是为了单纯地描述一 个形象，而是要让一种本来抽象的精神状态，变成可以被直接感受到的存在。 也正因如此，真正优秀的佛教雕塑，从来不是修饰的堆砌。它不是靠装饰取胜， 而是靠“显现”成立。也就是说，它能把本来不可见的东西——力量、宁静、空 性、智慧、慈悲——压进形体里，让你即使不懂经文，也会被其精神性直接击中。 这尊喜金刚，就是一个极好的例子。它不是通过文字解释“无常”，而是直接让 你在它的姿态中，看见流动与不住；它也不是通过说教告诉你“空性”，而是让 你在它似乎随时会腾空而起的身体里，感到一切都不固定。 所以，当我们凝视它时，我们并不只是“知道了某种宗教观念”，而是在雕塑本 身的力量里：原来一切都在变化，而正是这种变化，使智慧真正成为可能。
viewingNote.en: How is it that a single Vajra figure can simultaneously evoke feelings of both gentleness and sharpness? From where, then, does this complex visual experience truly originate? To answer this question, we must first establish: who, exactly, is this figure? Judging by its iconographic features, the identity of this work is actually quite clear. It possesses a towering, flame-like topknot surmounted by a crossed *vajra*; it wears a crown of five stylized skulls; one hand is raised high holding a *vajra*, while the other cradles a skull cup, with a *vajra* staff resting against its shoulder; draped across its body is a garland of human heads, and its entire form appears to be whirling in the void. Together, these characteristics point to a very distinct deity of Esoteric Buddhism: Hevajra. Hevajra is one of the most important deities within the Highest Yoga Tantra class of Tibetan Buddhism; he holds a particularly central position within the Sakya school, and the *Hevajra Tantra* serves as his foundational scripture. Here, the character for "joy" (*xi*) does not denote ordinary happiness in the everyday sense, but rather "Great Bliss" (*Mahāsukha*); similarly, the "Vajra" is not merely a weapon, but symbolizes indestructible wisdom, primordial awareness, and emptiness. Thus, the true significance of "Hevajra" is not that of a terrifying, wrathful deity, but rather: the attainment of ultimate Great Bliss through indestructible wisdom. In other words, although his imagery is intense, what he points toward is not violence, but a state of enlightenment in the highest sense. This point is crucial. For without grasping this nuance, one might easily misinterpret his wrath as mere "intimidation"; in reality, however, wrath in the context of Esoteric Buddhism is fundamentally a force that shatters attachment. It exists not to harm sentient beings, but to shatter their deepest ignorance and clinging. Therefore, the reason Hevajra appears "sharp" is not simply because he is angry, but because the very adversary he confronts is the most intractable of all: the illusion of a separate self. The second key question in understanding Hevajra is this: why is he invariably depicted in a state of intense dynamism? Within the context of Esoteric Buddhism, dance is never merely a physical movement; it is a symbolic language of the highest intensity. The dance of a deity is neither for entertainment nor for ornamentation; it is, rather, a "Dance of Enlightenment." What does this signify? It signifies that wisdom is not static or inert. It is not frozen in a fixed posture; rather, it manifests amidst flux and unfolds through motion. The concept known as "realizing Emptiness within movement" captures this very essence: Emptiness does not emerge only after all activity has ceased; instead, it is truly perceived within the very process of change itself. From this perspective, the reason Hevajra dances is not due to restlessness, but precisely because He has attained absolute freedom. No longer bound by a fixed identity, an immutable form, or a singular mode of existence, He is able to manifest wisdom directly amidst the flow of change. This explains why, when gazing upon this statue, we simultaneously experience a sense of both the "dreamlike" and the "real." For it is not merely demonstrating a physical action; rather, it is giving tangible form to the convergence of "impermanence, fluidity, and change" with "enlightenment." Delving deeper, we arrive at the aspect of such deities that is most prone to misinterpretation. The skull cup, the garland of severed heads, the crown of skulls—these elements possess a striking visual intensity and are easily misconstrued as mere "terrifying imagery." Yet, within the esoteric traditions, they are never intended merely to evoke horror. What they truly point to is a stark and unsparing reality: all attachments are ultimately destined to decay; all identities are bound to dissolve; and the perceived solidity of the "self" is, in truth, illusory. Thus, these skulls serve not to frighten, but to remind us: the very things to which you cling—your identity, desires, pride, shame, and fears—will ultimately dissipate. Unless one confronts this impermanence directly, one remains forever trapped within the confines of illusion. It is for this very reason that Hevajra’s wrath is not directed outward toward sentient beings, but rather inward—against "illusion" itself. What He seeks to shatter is not the sentient beings themselves, but the veils of delusion that they mistakenly perceive as reality. Therefore, the "ferocity" depicted in the imagery is not an end in itself, but rather the expression of a boundless and radical compassion. For only by daring to tear through the veil of illusion can one hope to draw near to the truth. Now, let us return to the sculpture itself. If iconography addresses the question of "Who is He?", then the language of sculpture addresses the deeper inquiry: Why does He enter our senses in this particular way? The initial impression this figure of Hevajra conveys is, in fact, remarkably clear: it is *solid*. Whether observing the transitions from shoulders to knees, or the manner in which its musculature and mass are articulated, one distinctly senses a sculptural vocabulary characterized by squareness and faceted planes. The shoulders are broad and angular, the torso full and robust; the body’s power is not dissipated, but rather channeled into a highly stable structural framework. This "squareness" does not signify rigidity, but rather a sense of structural support. It anchors the entire statue firmly in place, while simultaneously providing a fulcrum for its upward-surging momentum. Yet, remarkably, the artisan has ensured that this solidity does not render the work inert or heavy. On the contrary, it is imbued with a palpable sense of "wind"—of movement and airiness. The head is tilted ever so slightly; the *vajra* (thunderbolt scepter) and the skull-cup create a directional tension, while the slender ribbons fluttering behind the head—working in concert with the body’s contours—propel the entire figure from a state of "stability" into one of "motion." Consequently, the viewer experiences a peculiar illusion: although the figure stands firmly balanced upon a single foot, it appears poised to take flight and soar into the air at any moment. This constitutes the work’s most brilliant artistic achievement. It does not conceal its power, but instead allows that power to erupt outward—coursing along the body, tracing the lines, and driving the movement. Yet, this explosive energy remains entirely controlled; the supporting base below and the overall compositional structure serve to contain and stabilize it with unwavering firmness. Thus, what this masterpiece truly achieves is not merely "dynamism," but rather a state of equilibrium *within* that dynamic motion. This explains why the viewer perceives it as simultaneously aggressive and profoundly beautiful—for the aggression stems from its upward-surging eruption, while its beauty derives from the artisan’s refusal to allow that potent energy to ever dissipate. At this juncture, we arrive at the most subtle and nuanced aspect of this work. Why is it that, when confronting this figure of Hevajra, we perceive it as simultaneously real and phantasmal—possessing both the visceral impact of physical form and the ethereal quality of something poised to dissolve into thin air? It is because the formal language of this sculpture aligns in perfect harmony with the spiritual essence it embodies. Its lines flow like currents; its posture shifts and transforms; and its center of gravity, while appearing precarious, consistently maintains a balance that borders on the impossible. This sensation naturally evokes the very concept of "illusion" (*maya*): seemingly concrete, yet in constant flux; seemingly solid, yet ever-changing. This perspective aligns perfectly with the way "emptiness" (*shunyata*) is articulated within Esoteric Buddhism. Emptiness does not imply absolute voidness; rather, it posits that all things arise through dependent origination—that everything exists in a state of perpetual change, and that nothing possesses an immutable, inherent nature allowing it to exist eternally in a fixed form. Consequently, artistic representations that truly embody this concept of emptiness are rarely rigid, static, or hermetic. Instead, they must convey a sense of "unstable stability"—a state that appears on the verge of dissolution, yet remains profoundly real. This particular sculpture of Hevajra exemplifies precisely this quality.
