It demonstrates that while physical form undeniably exists, it is not a closed or self-contained entity; similarly, while power is certainly present, it is not a dead or inert force. Every aspect of the piece is in a state of "flow," and it is precisely within this fluidity that wisdom and enlightenment are truly revealed. Thus, the "dreamlike quality" of the work is not one of fragility or weakness; rather, it is an illusion imbued with immense power. It does not offer an escape from reality, but instead thrusts reality’s deepest truths—impermanence, change, and emptiness—directly before your eyes in visual form. Pushing this inquiry further, one discovers that the profound impact of this work is inextricably linked to the very medium of sculpture itself. Painting is a two-dimensional art form, relying on line, color, and composition to organize visual order; sculpture, conversely, is three-dimensional. Its existence is established not merely from the frontal view, but equally from the sides, the back, and within the context of its entire spatial relationship. Consequently, the language of sculpture transcends mere mimesis—the act of simply "resembling" a subject. Instead, through the interplay of volume, proportion, rhythm, and spatial tension, it allows the spiritual essence to truly take physical form. This principle holds particular significance in the realm of Buddhist iconography, for the purpose of Buddhist sculpture is not merely to depict a specific image, but to transform an inherently abstract spiritual state into a tangible presence that can be directly perceived and felt. It is for this very reason that truly exceptional Buddhist sculptures are never mere accumulations of ornamentation. Their power does not derive from decorative embellishment, but rather from the sheer act of "manifestation." In other words, it possesses the ability to imbue that which is inherently invisible—power, serenity, emptiness, wisdom, and compassion—directly into physical form, ensuring that even if you do not understand the scriptures, you are struck immediately by its spiritual essence. This Hevajra statue serves as a superb example of this. It does not explain the concept of "impermanence" through words, but rather allows you to witness—directly within its very posture—the interplay of flux and non-abiding. Nor does it convey the nature of "emptiness" through didactic preaching; instead, through a form that seems poised to take flight at any moment, it compels you to sense that nothing is fixed or static. Thus, as we gaze upon it, we do not merely "acquire a certain religious concept"; rather, immersed in the power of the sculpture itself, we come to realize that everything is, in fact, in a state of constant change—and it is precisely this ceaseless transformation that renders true wisdom possible.

excerpt.en: A Nalanda image of Padmapani defined by calm modeling and measured grace.

viewingNote.zh: 在佛教史上，那烂陀几乎是一个无法绕开的名字。如果只把它理解为一座寺院， 其实并不准确。更恰当的说法是：它是一所建立在寺院体系中的大型学术中心， 也是古代世界最重要的佛教高等学府之一。大约从公元 5 世纪笈多王朝时期开始， 那烂陀逐渐形成规模。它不仅拥有僧院、佛塔与礼拜空间，也拥有图书馆、讲学 场所和相当成熟的教学系统。在最兴盛的阶段，这里汇聚了大量僧侣、学生与学 者，学习内容也远不止佛学本身，还包括逻辑学、语法学、医学、天文学等多种 知识门类。因此，那烂陀的重要性从来不只是“宗教中心”，更是一种知识中心。 也正因为如此，它的影响远远超出了印度本土。从中国到西藏，从东南亚到更广 泛的佛教世界，那烂陀都长期被视为大乘佛教思想与学术训练的核心源头。玄奘 在 7 世纪抵达印度后，之所以长期停留于此，正是因为那烂陀在当时并不只是名 寺，而是整个印度佛教思想最密集、最系统、也最具权威的地方之一。在他的记 述中，这里不仅建筑宏大，讲学氛围浓厚，更代表着当时印度文化与思想的高水 准。可也正因为它站得太高，它的毁灭才格外令人沉默。 到了 12 世纪末，那烂陀在战争与征服中遭受毁灭性打击。寺院被焚，僧侣流散， 藏书焚毁，一个延续数百年的文明中心就此中断。从佛教史的角度看，这并不只 是一个地点的终结，而更像是一条学术与宗教中轴的断裂。随后，佛教文化的重 心开始进一步向西藏、东亚等地转移。而这种断裂，也直接作用于艺术。寺院一 旦毁灭，依附于寺院体系存在的造像也往往难以幸存。因此，今天真正能够确认 与那烂陀相关的造像，数量极少。它们大多来自窖藏、塔藏或考古发掘，只有极 少数是在当年就已被带离印度、从而幸免于破坏的作品。也正因为如此，那烂陀 造像的珍贵，并不只是因为年代早，更因为它们本身就是一个文明中心遗留下来 的少量“原始证据”。 既然那烂陀如此重要，那么一个更关键的问题就是：那烂陀造像为什么会长成这 个样子？它并不是凭空出现的。从风格来源看，那烂陀地处恒河流域，其艺术语 言与北印度古典传统关系密切。尤其从笈多时期开始，北印度佛教造像逐渐形成 了一套成熟的审美范式：面容趋于宁静，身体更具理想化比例，装饰与神圣性之 间的关系也被重新组织。到了帕拉时期，这一传统又被进一步发展：金铜造像大 量出现，工艺愈发精细，密教图像学更加丰富，佛、菩萨、本尊与护法的体系日 趋完整。那烂陀正处在这一艺术演变的核心地带，因此，它最终形成了一种极具 辨识度的风格系统。 如果概括来说，那烂陀风格最重要的特征，不在于“繁”，而在于一种在次序中 的庄严感。它当然也不乏装饰，但装饰从不喧宾夺主。它更在意的是：如何让宗 教形象既神圣，又清朗；既精致，又不滞重；既具有供养对象的庄严，又保留某 种能让人安静下来的温和气息。眼前这尊 9—10 世纪的世尊观音，正是这种成熟 风格的典型体现。 我第一次看到这件作品时，最先感受到的，并不是某一个局部，而是两个字：完 整。这一点其实非常重要。因为我们今天能见到的早期那烂陀造像，很多都因为 战乱、埋藏或后世流失，出现不同程度的残缺。尤其是头顶的华盖、上部附属构 件，往往较易缺失。而这尊像之所以难得，首先就在于它较为完整地保留了原本 的构图关系。其次，这件作品的表面状态也非常动人。它并不是新铸金属那种直 接、锐利的光泽，而是带着一种经过时间沉淀后的温润。如果它确曾经历窖藏， 后又继续传世，那么这种略带斑驳、又透出铜质本色的皮壳，就不仅是材质状态， 更是时间作用过后的结果。也正因为如此，这件作品让人也感受到一种很明确的 “时间感”。而这种时间感，恰恰是在表达着：眼前看到的，并不是一件孤立的 美术品，而是一件真正走过历史、被时代触碰过的存在。 如果继续看这件作品，我们会察觉出它的轻盈感。这点其实很有意思。因为在很 多人的想象里，古代金铜造像往往容易给人“厚重、庄严、不可接近”的印象。 但这尊观音恰恰相反。它形体并不魁伟，反而显得挺拔、舒展、从容。观音头戴 宝冠，冠顶化佛清楚明确；右手施与愿印，左手持莲；一足踏于自莲座中自然生 长而出的莲花，身后则是一轮圆形背光。元素并不算少，但整件作品并没有因此 显得拥挤，反而有一种非常清透的节奏。 这种轻盈感从哪里来？首先，来自构图关系的处理。背光并没有被做得过分繁密， 纹饰以圆弧、几何和简化线条为主，因此它不是一块厚重的背景板，而更像是一 个帮助神圣性浮现的光场。其次，观音身体本身的处理也极为节制。它姿态挺拔， 四肢比例协调，整体并不依赖厚重体量来建立庄严，而是通过流畅的轮廓和开阔 的姿态，形成一种自然向上的气息。正因为留白得当，气息才会流动；正因为不 靠堆砌取胜，庄严才会显得轻。再回到面部。它柔和、和煦，甚至带着一点会让 人自然放松下来的亲近感。这种感觉，当然来自图像学上观音作为慈悲菩萨的身 份；但更关键的，仍然是匠人在形式上的组织。
viewingNote.en: In the history of Buddhism, Nalanda is a name that is virtually impossible to overlook. To understand it merely as a monastery would, in fact, be inaccurate. A more fitting description is that it was a vast academic center established within a monastic framework—one of the most significant institutions of Buddhist higher learning in the ancient world. Beginning roughly in the 5th century CE, during the Gupta Dynasty, Nalanda gradually grew to its full scale. It featured not only monastic quarters, stupas, and spaces for worship, but also libraries, lecture halls, and a highly sophisticated educational system. At the zenith of its prosperity, the site drew together a multitude of monks, students, and scholars; the curriculum extended far beyond Buddhist theology itself to encompass a diverse array of disciplines, including logic, grammar, medicine, and astronomy. Consequently, Nalanda’s significance was never limited to that of a "religious center"; it was, above all, a center of knowledge. Precisely for this reason, its influence radiated far beyond the borders of India. From China to Tibet, and from Southeast Asia to the wider Buddhist world, Nalanda was long revered as the primary wellspring of Mahayana Buddhist thought and scholarly training. When Xuanzang arrived in India in the 7th century, he chose to remain at Nalanda for an extended period precisely because, at that time, it was not merely a renowned monastery, but one of the most concentrated, systematic, and authoritative hubs of Buddhist thought in all of India. In his chronicles, he describes a place characterized not only by its monumental architecture and intense scholarly atmosphere but also as the very embodiment of the high watermark of Indian culture and intellectual life of that era. Yet, precisely because it stood so high, its eventual destruction remains all the more profoundly poignant. By the late 12th century, Nalanda suffered a catastrophic blow amidst the turmoil of war and conquest. Its monasteries were razed by fire, its monks were scattered, and its vast library—the repository of centuries of learning—was reduced to ash; thus, a center of civilization that had flourished for hundreds of years was abruptly extinguished. From the perspective of Buddhist history, this marked not merely the demise of a specific location, but rather the severing of a central axis of academic and religious tradition. In the aftermath, the epicenter of Buddhist culture began to shift further afield—toward regions such as Tibet and East Asia. This rupture, moreover, had a direct and profound impact on the realm of art. Once the monasteries themselves were destroyed, the religious statuary and iconography inextricably linked to that monastic ecosystem rarely survived. Consequently, the number of surviving artworks that can be definitively identified today as originating from—or being directly associated with—Nalanda remains exceedingly small. Most of these artifacts originate from hoards, stupa deposits, or archaeological excavations; only a very small minority are works that were taken out of India during their own era, thereby escaping subsequent destruction. Precisely for this reason, the preciousness of Nalanda sculptures lies not merely in their antiquity, but—more importantly—in the fact that they constitute a rare body of "primary evidence" left behind by a major center of civilization. Given the immense significance of Nalanda, a more critical question arises: Why did Nalanda sculpture evolve into the specific form we see today? It did not emerge in a vacuum. In terms of stylistic origins, Nalanda is situated within the Ganges River basin, and its artistic idiom is deeply intertwined with the classical traditions of Northern India. Particularly from the Gupta period onward, Buddhist sculpture in Northern India gradually established a mature aesthetic paradigm: facial expressions became increasingly serene, bodily proportions grew more idealized, and the relationship between ornamentation and sacredness was fundamentally redefined. By the Pala period, this tradition had undergone further development: gilt-bronze sculptures appeared in abundance, craftsmanship became increasingly refined, the iconography of Esoteric Buddhism grew richer, and the pantheon—comprising Buddhas, Bodhisattvas, deities, and Dharma protectors—became ever more comprehensive. Situated at the very epicenter of this artistic evolution, Nalanda ultimately forged a stylistic system that is instantly recognizable. Broadly speaking, the most defining characteristic of the Nalanda style is not "ornate complexity," but rather a sense of solemn majesty grounded in harmonious order. While these works certainly do not lack ornamentation, such decoration never overpowers the central subject. Instead, the primary focus is on how to render religious figures in a manner that is simultaneously sacred and lucid; refined yet never ponderous; possessing the solemn dignity befitting an object of veneration, while retaining a gentle aura capable of instilling a sense of inner calm in the beholder. The sculpture before us—a 9th-to-10th-century depiction of the Venerable Avalokiteśvara—serves as a quintessential embodiment of this mature style. When I first encountered this piece, my initial impression was not focused on any specific detail, but rather on a single concept: *wholeness*. This point is, in fact, of great significance. For among the early Nalanda sculptures accessible to us today, a great many suffer from varying degrees of damage or incompleteness—the result of warfare, burial, or subsequent loss over the centuries. In particular, elements such as the ceremonial canopy (parasol) surmounting the head and other ancillary components of the upper structure are frequently found to be missing. The rarity of this statue lies, first and foremost, in the fact that it has preserved its original compositional relationships with remarkable completeness. Secondly, the surface condition of this piece is truly captivating. It does not possess the direct, sharp luster of newly cast metal; rather, it bears a mellow warmth—a patina—that has settled upon it through the passage of time. If, indeed, it was once buried in a hoard before continuing its journey through the generations, then this surface—slightly mottled yet revealing the true nature of the bronze beneath—represents not merely a material state, but the tangible result of time’s transformative touch. Precisely for this reason, the piece evokes a distinct and palpable "sense of time." This sense of time conveys a specific message: what stands before us is not merely an isolated work of art, but an entity that has truly traversed history and been touched by the eras it has witnessed. Upon closer examination, one perceives a distinct sense of lightness within this work—a quality that is, in fact, quite intriguing. For in the popular imagination, ancient gilt-bronze sculptures often tend to evoke impressions of "heaviness, solemnity, and inaccessibility." This statue of Guanyin, however, presents a striking contrast. Its form is not massive or imposing; instead, it appears upright, expansive, and poised. Guanyin wears a jeweled crown, surmounted by a clearly delineated miniature Buddha; the right hand forms the *Varada Mudra* (the gesture of wish-granting), while the left holds a lotus blossom. One foot rests upon a lotus flower that appears to have sprouted organically from the base itself, while a circular *mandorla* (halo) frames the figure from behind. Although the composition incorporates numerous elements, the piece as a whole never feels cluttered; on the contrary, it possesses a remarkably lucid and rhythmic flow. From where does this sense of lightness originate? First, it stems from the handling of the compositional relationships. The *mandorla* has not been rendered with excessive density; its ornamentation consists primarily of sweeping arcs, geometric patterns, and simplified lines. Consequently, it functions not as a heavy, solid backdrop, but rather as a luminous field that facilitates the manifestation of the divine presence. Secondly, the rendering of Guanyin’s body itself demonstrates remarkable restraint. The posture is upright and the limbs are harmoniously proportioned; the figure does not rely on sheer physical mass to establish its solemnity, but instead employs fluid contours and an expansive stance to generate a natural, upward-soaring energy. It is precisely because of this judicious use of negative space that the spiritual energy is able to flow; and it is precisely because it seeks not to impress through mere accumulation that its solemnity feels so light and effortless. Returning to the face: it is gentle and serene, imbued even with a certain approachable warmth that naturally puts one at ease. This sensation stems, of course, from the iconographic identity of Guanyin as the Bodhisattva of Compassion; yet, more crucially, it remains a testament to the craftsman's formal composition.

label.en: London Market, by the early 1990s； Christie's,New York, 15 March,2016, no.46

excerpt.en: An imperial phurba whose ritual authority is matched by sculptural precision.

viewingNote.zh: 在藏传佛教与密教体系中，金刚镢，又称降魔杵、普巴杵，并不是普通意义上的 器物。它首先是一件法器，但又不仅是法器。因为它所承担的，不只是仪轨中的 使用功能，更是一整套宗教观念的可视化结果：关于降伏、净化、转化、守护， 也关于智慧如何进入现实、进入身体、进入混乱之中。金刚镢是密教仪轨中的重 要器物，同时也是忿怒尊常见的持物，其象征意义正在于刺破无明、欲望与嗔恨 这“三毒”。 也正因为如此，真正值得追问的问题出现了：它为什么会长成这个样子？ 眼前这把 15 世纪明代永乐时期的金刚镢，恰好提供了一个极好的切口。它并不 是单一材质制成，而是分为三段：上下两端为乌木，中段为紫檀。仅仅这一点， 就已经说明，它的意义远远超出了普通仪轨器具的实用层面。因为在永乐朝的宫 廷宗教艺术中，器物的材质从来不只是工艺问题，更是等级、权力、信仰与制作 系统共同作用的结果。大都会博物馆在“Defining Yongle”展览说明中也明确强 调：永乐皇帝对藏传佛教有强烈信仰，而朝廷掌握的生产能力，又使得宫廷工坊 能够制作出高等级的雕塑、法器与礼物，并用来服务仪式、赏赐高僧、塑造帝国 佛教形象。换句话说，这把金刚镢首先不是“民间器”，而是宫廷宗教语境中的 器物。要理解这件作品，必须先回到它所处的时代。 永乐皇帝是明代早期最有权力、也最积极经营宗教与视觉政治的一位皇帝。他不 仅推动大规模海上远航，也极力经营与藏传佛教上层僧侣的关系。正因为永乐朝 宫廷工坊生产的宗教艺术，不只是为了宫廷内部使用，也经常作为礼物赠予来朝 或受封的高僧，这些作品后来反过来又深刻影响了 15 世纪后半叶的藏地艺术。 而关于金刚镢这一器类，Himalayan Art 的相关研究也提供了一个极重要的线索： 现存若干与永乐时期风格接近的金刚镢，曾被学者联系到一组永乐宫廷委制、并 赐藏于萨迦寺的普巴杵系统。这就意味着，像这样一把金刚镢，不能只当作“精 致木雕”来看。它极可能原本就属于皇权与宗教共同建构的法器体系。它既是佛 教圣器，也是宫廷政治语言的一部分。换句话说，它要表现的，不只是佛法的力 量，也包括一种帝国等级下的庄严秩序。 这件金刚镢最先抓住人的，往往不是图像，而是材质。上下两段为乌木，中段为 紫檀。这种组合本身，就已经带有非常明确的等级意味。乌木色深、质密、沉稳， 天然带着一种近乎“收住”的气息。它不会把视觉引向华丽，而是先把整体气氛 压低、压稳，让法器一开始就获得一种肃穆基调。而紫檀，则恰恰相反。它不是 轻浮的亮，而是内敛、温润、却极有分量的贵重材质。从明代宫廷材料史来看， 永乐至宣德时期随着远航与朝贡往来展开，宝石、香料与珍贵木材的输入，确实 显著扩展了宫廷高等级器用的材料来源。故宫研究者文章便明确提到，郑和下西 洋带回了香料、宝石与珍贵木材，这些材料为明代高等级器物制作提供了重要条 件。 因此，这里真正重要的，并不是“乌木与紫檀哪一种更贵”，而是：两种材质被 有意识地放进了同一件法器里。这说明匠人和委制者并不是在满足最低限度的仪 轨需要，而是在通过材质组织一种等级分明、气质分层的宗教器物语言。乌木负 责压住整体的沉着与威仪，紫檀则把中段最核心的象征部位，从“功能”提升到 “仪式中心”。所以，材质在这里不是背景。它就是第一层精神语言。 如果顺着形制往下看，你会发现，这把金刚镢并不是随意拼接出来的。它几乎是 按一种“自上而下逐层展开”的方式，构成了一个完整的象征系统。最上端，是 三面忿怒护法相。怒目、卷发、焰发、咧口，这一切都在告诉你：这里的力量并 不是抚慰性的，而是介入性的。它不是来安慰幻觉，而是来打破幻觉。因此，忿 怒并不等于恶意，恰恰相反，它是一种更高强度的慈悲：因为只有真正有能力摧 破执著，才可能谈得上守护正法。 中段最核心的部分，是上下对称的扭结与莲瓣。这一段非常关键，因为整件法器 最深的哲学意味，往往不在最锋利的刃部，而恰恰在这个“连接处”。扭结的意 义，在藏密文献中常被理解为轮回与涅槃、法界与现象界、智慧与方便之间那种 首尾相接、无始无终的关系。它不是简单的装饰，而是一种无尽延续的图像化表 达。而莲瓣置于其中，则把这股“无尽性”重新拉回到佛教最核心的价值上：无 论降伏多么猛烈，最终指向都必须是清净。也就是说，力量必须安住于智慧之中， 否则它就会坠入单纯的破坏。 这一点，其实是整把金刚镢最容易被误解、却也最重要的地方。它并不是一件鼓 励暴烈的法器，而是一件不断提醒使用者：降伏若离开清净与智慧，就不再是佛 法。再往下，便进入最具有“动作感”的部分。扭结下方，是张口的摩羯。在佛 教与印度艺术中，摩羯并不只是神兽图案，它常常承担着“吞吐”“生发”“转 化”的功能。所以，当摩羯在法器上张口吐出镢刃时，它所暗示的，并不是简单 的装饰性奇观，而是一种非常明确的宗教动作：法力从混沌中被生发出来。而三 棱镢刃的意义，则更直接。它象征对“三毒”的刺破。也就是说，金刚镢之所以 是尖刃，不是因为它要征服世界，而是因为它要穿透人的遮蔽。刃面上的蛇，则 进一步把这种“穿透”从外部暴力，转成内部转化。蛇在密教与更广泛的南亚图 像语境中，经常与原始能量、欲望、潜伏的生命力联系在一起。当蛇缠绕于镢刃 之上，它就不再只是危险象征，而更像是在说：连最原始、最不稳定的力量，也 可以被驯服、被导向、被转化。 所以，摩羯、镢刃、蛇，这三者连在一起，不是在讲一个“毁灭的故事”，而是 在讲一个“转化的故事”：混乱如何被吸纳，力量如何被引出，毒如何被刺破， 本能如何被重新编入智慧的秩序之中。整件法器的最后一段，是尖刃所插入的三 角形底座。这一部分，往往最容易被忽略，但恰恰是全器最值得停下来看的一处。 因为前面所有的忿怒、扭结、摩羯、蛇与刃，最后都必须“落地”。在密教曼荼 罗与图像学中，倒置三角常常与空性、本初母、智慧母体相关。从更广泛的形式 语言上看，三角形又天然具有稳定、收束、承载的意味。于是，当镢刃插入这个 底座时，整件法器最终完成的，其实是一个非常完整的宗教动作：不是把混乱消 灭掉，而是把它钉入秩序，把它安放进智慧，把它从失控状态转为可被承载的力 量。 如果用今天的话来说，这一点甚至非常接近心理学里“阴影整合”的逻辑。真正 成熟的修行，并不是把内心所有负面力量都假装不存在，而是让它们进入一个更 稳定的中心之中，不再四处失控。所以，金刚镢的意义，并不是“杀死蛇”，而 是“让蛇不再吞噬你”。这才是它最现代、也最深的地方。
viewingNote.en:
Within the systems of Tibetan Buddhism and Esoteric Buddhism, the *Vajrakīla*—also known as the "Demon-Subduing Dagger" or *Phurba*—is no mere object in the ordinary sense. It functions primarily as a ritual implement, yet it is far more than that. For what it embodies transcends mere utility within ritual protocols; rather, it serves as the tangible manifestation of a comprehensive set of religious concepts—ideas concerning subjugation, purification, transformation, and protection, as well as the manner in which wisdom penetrates reality, the physical body, and the very heart of chaos. The *Vajrakīla* is a pivotal instrument in Esoteric Buddhist rituals and a common attribute held by wrathful deities; its symbolic significance lies precisely in its capacity to pierce through the "Three Poisons": ignorance, desire, and aversion. It is precisely for this reason that a truly compelling question arises: Why was it fashioned in this particular form? The *Vajrakīla* currently before us—dating to the 15th-century Yongle era of the Ming Dynasty—offers an excellent vantage point from which to explore this inquiry. It is not crafted from a single material but is instead segmented into three sections: the upper and lower extremities are composed of ebony, while the central section is made of red sandalwood. This feature alone demonstrates that its significance extends far beyond the utilitarian realm of ordinary ritual implements. For within the context of court-sponsored religious art during the Yongle reign, the choice of materials was never merely a matter of craftsmanship; rather, it was the product of the interplay between hierarchy, political power, religious faith, and the imperial production apparatus. In the exhibition catalogue for "Defining Yongle," the Metropolitan Museum of Art explicitly emphasizes that the Yongle Emperor harbored a profound devotion to Tibetan Buddhism; furthermore, the immense production capabilities at the court's disposal enabled imperial workshops to fabricate high-status sculptures, ritual implements, and ceremonial gifts—objects utilized to facilitate rituals, bestow honors upon high-ranking monastics, and project the image of an imperial Buddhist state. In other words, this *Vajrakīla* is, first and foremost, not a "folk object," but an artifact situated firmly within the specific religious context of the imperial court. To truly comprehend this work, one must first return to the era in which it was created. The Yongle Emperor stands as one of the most powerful monarchs of the early Ming Dynasty—and certainly the most proactive in his strategic engagement with both religious affairs and the politics of visual representation. He not only championed large-scale maritime expeditions but also assiduously cultivated diplomatic and religious ties with the highest echelons of the Tibetan Buddhist clergy. Precisely because the religious art produced by the imperial workshops during the Yongle reign was not intended solely for internal court use—but was frequently bestowed as gifts upon visiting dignitaries or high-ranking monks receiving imperial investiture—these works subsequently exerted a profound influence on Tibetan art during the latter half of the 15th century. Regarding the specific category of ritual implements known as the *Vajra-peg* (or *Vajra-stake*), scholarly research—particularly that conducted by Himalayan Art Resources—offers a crucial clue: several extant examples of *Vajra-pegs* exhibiting stylistic affinities with the Yongle era have been linked by scholars to a specific corpus of *Phurba* (ritual dagger) implements commissioned by the Yongle court and subsequently enshrined at Sakya Monastery. This implies that an object such as this *Vajra-peg* cannot be viewed merely as an "exquisite wooden carving." In all likelihood, it originally belonged to a system of ritual implements jointly constructed by—and serving to reinforce—both imperial authority and religious power. It functions simultaneously as a sacred Buddhist vessel and as an integral component of the court's political iconography. In other words, what it seeks to convey is not merely the power of the Buddhist Dharma, but also a sense of solemn order and hierarchical structure inherent to the imperial realm. What first captures the viewer's attention in this particular *Vajra-peg* is often not its iconography, but rather its materiality. The upper and lower sections are crafted from ebony, while the central section is fashioned from red sandalwood. This specific combination, in and of itself, carries a distinct and unequivocal hierarchical significance. Ebony—characterized by its deep hue, dense grain, and inherent gravitas—naturally exudes an aura of restraint and composure. Rather than drawing the eye toward ostentation, it serves to ground and stabilize the overall atmosphere, thereby endowing the ritual implement with a solemn and dignified foundation from the very outset. Red sandalwood, conversely, offers a striking contrast. Its luminosity is not one of superficial brilliance, but rather of a restrained, lustrous warmth—a precious material possessing immense intrinsic weight and substance. From the perspective of material history within the Ming imperial court, the period spanning the Yongle and Xuande reigns—marked by the commencement of maritime expeditions and tributary exchanges—witnessed a significant expansion in the range of materials available for the creation of high-status court objects, driven by the importation of gemstones, aromatics, and rare timbers. Indeed, scholarly articles authored by researchers at the Palace Museum explicitly note that Zheng He’s voyages to the "Western Oceans" brought back a wealth of spices, gemstones, and precious woods; these materials, in turn, provided the essential prerequisites for the production of the Ming dynasty's most exalted ritual and decorative objects. Therefore, what truly matters here is not the question of "which is more expensive—ebony or red sandalwood?" but rather the fact that these two materials were *consciously* incorporated into a single ritual implement. This indicates that the craftsman and the patron were not merely fulfilling the bare minimum requirements of ritual protocol; instead, through their choice and arrangement of materials, they were constructing a religious visual language characterized by distinct hierarchies and stratified spiritual qualities. The ebony serves to anchor the implement’s overall gravitas and majesty, while the red sandalwood elevates the central section—its most symbolically significant zone—from a mere "functional component" to a "ritual epicenter." Thus, the materials here are not mere background elements; they constitute the primary layer of spiritual expression. If one follows the structural design downward, one discovers that this *Vajra Kilaya* (ritual dagger) is not merely an arbitrary assemblage of parts. Rather, it constructs a complete symbolic system that unfolds in a "top-down, layer-by-layer" progression. At the very apex reside the three wrathful countenances of the Dharma Protectors. Their glaring eyes, curling hair, flaming tresses, and bared fangs all convey a singular message: the power embodied here is not one of gentle solace, but of active intervention. It does not seek to comfort illusions, but to shatter them. Consequently, this wrath is not synonymous with malice; on the contrary, it represents a form of compassion of the highest intensity—for only those truly capable of destroying attachment can genuinely be said to safeguard the True Dharma. The core of the central section features a symmetrical arrangement of knots and lotus petals. This segment is pivotal, for the deepest philosophical significance of the entire ritual implement often resides not in its sharpest blades, but precisely within this "juncture." In the literature of Vajrayana Buddhism, the symbolism of the knot is frequently interpreted as representing the ceaseless, beginningless-and-endless relationship that binds Samsara to Nirvana, the Dharma Realm to the Phenomenal Realm, and Wisdom to Skillful Means. It is not a mere decorative motif, but a visual expression of infinite continuity. The inclusion of the lotus petals within this context serves to anchor this sense of "infinity" back to the core values ​​of Buddhism: no matter how fierce the act of subduing obstacles may be, its ultimate objective must always be purification. In other words, power must be grounded in wisdom; otherwise, it risks degenerating into mere destruction. This particular point is, in fact, the aspect of the *Vajra Kilaya* that is most frequently misunderstood—yet it remains its most profoundly significant feature. It is not a ritual implement intended to encourage violence; rather, it serves as a constant reminder to its user: if the act of subjugation is divorced from purity and wisdom, it ceases to be an expression of the Dharma. Moving further down, we arrive at the section possessing the most palpable sense of "action." Beneath the twisted knot lies the open-mouthed Makara. In the contexts of Buddhist and Indian art, the Makara is not merely a decorative motif of a mythical beast; it frequently embodies the functions of "ingestion and emission," "generation," and "transformation." Thus, when the Makara on a ritual implement opens its jaws to disgorge the blade of the *phurba* (ritual dagger), it implies not merely a decorative spectacle, but a very specific religious act: the generation of spiritual power emerging from the primordial chaos. The significance of the three-edged blade is even more direct: it symbolizes the piercing and shattering of the "Three Poisons." In other words, the *vajra phurba* possesses a sharp blade not because it seeks to conquer the external world, but because it aims to penetrate and dispel the obscurations within the human mind. The serpent coiled around the blade further transforms this act of "penetration"—shifting its nature from external violence to internal transformation. In the iconography of Esoteric Buddhism—as well as within the broader visual language of South Asia—the serpent is frequently associated with primordial energy, desire, and latent vital forces. When the serpent is depicted entwined around the blade, it ceases to function merely as a symbol of danger; instead, it seems to declare that even the most primordial and volatile forces can be tamed, channeled, and ultimately transformed.

Thus, the convergence of the Makara, the blade, and the serpent does not narrate a "story of destruction," but rather a "story of transformation": how chaos is absorbed, how power is drawn forth, how poisons are pierced, and how primal instincts are rewoven into the ordered fabric of wisdom. The final section of the entire ritual implement consists of the triangular base into which the sharp blade is inserted. This component is often the most easily overlooked, yet it is precisely the part of the entire object most deserving of close attention. For all the elements that precede it—the expressions of wrath, the twisted knots, the Makara, the serpent, and the blade—must ultimately find their "grounding." In the mandalas and iconography of Esoteric Buddhism, the inverted triangle is frequently associated with Emptiness (*Śūnyatā*), the Primordial Mother, and the Matrix of Wisdom. Viewed through the broader lens of visual language, the triangle naturally embodies connotations of stability, containment, and support. Thus, when the blade of the ritual dagger is inserted into this base, the completed ritual implement ultimately enacts a profoundly complete religious gesture: not the eradication of chaos, but rather its anchoring within order, its placement within wisdom, and its transformation from an uncontrolled state into a force capable of being borne. To use contemporary terminology, this concept bears a striking resemblance to the psychological logic of "shadow integration." Truly mature spiritual practice does not entail pretending that all one's inner negative forces do not exist; rather, it involves guiding them into a more stable center, preventing them from spiraling out of control. Therefore, the true significance of the *Vajrakila*—the ritual dagger—lies not in "killing the serpent," but in "ensuring the serpent no longer devours you." This constitutes its most modern—and indeed, its most profound—dimension.

title.zh: Michael Henss, Buddhist Ritual Art of Xizang, Stuttgart
title.en: Michael Henss, Buddhist Ritual Art of Xizang, Stuttgart

excerpt.zh: 色彩作为变幻的媒介，沉醉其中的我们似乎可以获得比“线条 - 构图”更重要的关于美与神圣的本质。
excerpt.en: A Ratnasambhava thangka distinguished by chromatic order and luminous clarity.

viewingNote.zh: 当我们游走在青藏高原时，映入眼帘的大多是纯度极高的绚烂色彩，威严的 蓝、耀眼的红与超脱的白，它们与黄色、绿色构成了藏传文化的五种本源象征色。 在这里色彩不仅如实呈现了由元素构成的大千世界；更重要的是，这背后所阐述 的是一个个有关感性力量和哲学思变的具体情境。色彩作为变幻的媒介，沉醉其 中的我们似乎可以获得比“线条 - 构图”更重要的关于美与神圣的本质。在青藏 高原这片神秘而壮丽的土地上，绘画艺术如同璀璨的花朵，在圣洁的天空下绚烂 绽放，成为连接神圣与美的独特媒介。它不仅让我们领会到了高原的深厚文化底 蕴，更让我们感受到了艺术的力量和魅力。 从历史背景来看，13 世纪是西藏佛教艺术发展的重要时期。这一时期，西 藏在经历了朗达玛灭佛的混乱局面后，迎来了后弘期佛教兴盛与政教合一的时 代，同样也是西藏艺术的辉煌——丹萨替寺造像的开始。 自 11 世纪初，印度高僧阿底峡入藏弘法之后，为西藏带来了印度帕拉艺术 的影响。与此之后，随着 12 世纪末帕拉王朝的覆灭，僧侣与匠人们的纷纷逃 离，以及因十三世纪萨迦政权的繁荣强大，而被吸引过来的尼泊尔匠人，在这样 的背景下，十三世纪的藏传佛教艺术，在不断融合与创新中呈现出了新的美学高 度。这幅带有明显帕拉美学风格的宝生佛唐卡，也正是在这样的历史背景下诞生 了，它充分的展现了当时西藏艺术风格的兼容并蓄。 宝生佛，为佛教密宗崇奉的五方佛之一，这组深奥的神祇共同构成了密宗的 开悟宇宙观。五方佛中的每一尊都通过颜色、手势和方向来识别，宝生佛则住在 南方欢喜世界。唐卡采用分段叙事以及最具特征的表现手法构图，勾勒出了一个 神圣、庄严、梦幻的佛国净土。首先印入眼帘是耀眼的红和灿烂的黄，这两种颜 色作为主色调，充斥着整块画布。唐卡最上方绘画有六位人物形象，其中五位为 佛陀形象，一位为一尊上师像。人物的下一层为一排宝塔，塔顶后方为五彩斑斓 的智慧树，象征着智慧与觉悟的无尽源泉，卡通、抽象的绘画方式简单直接，像 童话世界般纯真、美好。宝塔中间为一座佛塔的塔顶位置，从塔幢形状来看，为 元代风格，这也体现了藏族画师吸收了印度风格后融入了西藏本土的元素。 叙事中段为宝生佛和金刚幢菩萨以及金刚幢菩萨的明妃。宝生佛全身金 色，面带微 笑、眉目柔和；身戴宝冠、耳饰、臂钏、项链、珠宝璎珞，身着裙 裤，佩戴腰带、脚环，华丽、高贵。宝生佛那洋溢着青春、阳光的气质，肌肉体 块明显、硬朗，匠人仅仅使 用了几笔就 将宝生佛的身躯体 现，且 线条流畅柔 软，予人肌肤富有弹性般的视觉观感，这看似简约的线条实，则彰显着匠人深厚的绘画功底。 宝生佛以结全跏趺坐的座姿，端身正坐于莲台之上，莲瓣宽大、舒展，瓣尖 翻卷，其图像风格为典型的元代特征。宝生佛左手于腹前结禅定印，右手掌心向 外，结与愿印。据说此印可以满足众生一切愿望，在佛教信仰中，宝生佛是一位 能满足众生愿求的慈悲大佛。宝生佛身后是由神兽组成的六拏具背光，在佛前虔 诚的神兽们，面容可爱、灵动；柔软的线条、丰富的色彩以及晕染的手法，使神 兽们的形象生动且立体；上方张口龇牙的摩羯，更是把我们拉入了一个梦幻且深 邃的海洋世界。在构图上，中间主尊宝生佛的比例占据整体画面的三分之二，这 种最具特征的表现手法意为凸显主尊的次第与精神威望。此种表现手法也为西 藏早期绘画风格的表现特征。 宝生佛两侧分别站立的金刚幢菩萨及其明妃，与中世纪东北印度艺术的典型 布局如出一辙。金刚幢菩萨和其明妃同样头戴宝冠、身戴珠宝、璎珞，以曼 妙、动感的身姿站立其左右侧。金刚幢菩萨和其明妃的裙裤设计很有特点，这种 以珠宝穿成线来表现薄纱透明、下垂质地的手法，不仅展现了匠人的创造力，更 将人物形象赋予了独特的神性气质。薄纱的轻盈与珠宝的璀璨相互映衬，使得裙 裤仿佛在光线的照耀下轻轻摇曳，既展现了华贵之气，又不失朦胧之美，在仙气 飘飘的气质中，更是将整个画面进一步推向了一个超脱尘世的境界。莲座下方 左右两边分别绘画有供养菩萨和宝生佛的坐骑“马”，匠人运用同系色中不同深浅 的棕色就将马儿优雅灵巧、奔放弛聘的英姿展现。整个叙事中最为特殊的是底 部绘画有藏传佛教的吉祥八宝，八宝又称八瑞相、八吉祥，是藏传佛教中八种表 示吉庆祥瑞之物，象征吉祥、幸福、圆满。吉祥八宝在大多数十三到十四世纪的 早期西藏唐卡绘画里面较为少见，这也是充分的展现了此幅唐卡的印藏融合风 格。 虽然这幅唐卡明显遵循了印度原型，但它也展示了西藏风格的地域性，特别 是人物的开脸、身型、比例以及色彩。画中没有任何废笔之处，线条有劲，轮廓 清晰，动物的绘画也都惟妙惟肖，应该是藏族画师在学习了印度绘画风格后，融 合了藏地元素进行的创作。此幅唐卡在历经了 800 余年，只是有一些轻微的脱 彩，其整体保存状态依旧如此完好、色彩依旧如此鲜艳，可见历代寺庙与藏家对 其的重视，其珍贵程度不亚于一张宫廷品质的宋画。
viewingNote.en: This Ratnasambhava thangka is distinguished by its chromatic order and carefully paced composition. Color is not merely decorative here; it structures the image, clarifies hierarchy, and gives the painting its devotional atmosphere. The work rewards slow looking, particularly in the way line, mineral pigment, and iconographic balance are held in disciplined relation.

label.zh: Richard C. Blum and Senator Dianne Feinstein Collection 收藏
label.en: Richard C. Blum and Senator Dianne Feinstein Collection

label.zh: 喜马拉雅艺术资源网编号：1868

excerpt.en: A Nepalese stupa that compresses architectural clarity into a small ritual form.

viewingNote.zh: 佛塔，是印度梵文 Stupa（窣堵波）的音译，也称为浮屠。佛塔作为佛教建 筑的代表，不仅是佛教信仰和文化的重要象征，还承载着丰富的历史、宗教、艺 术和文化意义。佛塔最早起源于释迦牟尼圆寂后，弟子们为了纪念佛陀，而用石 块建塔以埋葬其骨灰。最初的佛塔多为简单的土堆或石堆，用以纪念。随着佛 教的兴盛，佛塔逐渐演变成了复杂的石造建筑，成为了佛教信仰的重要象征。在 印度，阿育王统治时期，佛教被列为国教，佛塔的建立达到了空前的高涨，分建 了八万四千座佛塔，这一时期的佛塔多为覆钵式塔。随着佛教的传播发展和在亚 洲各地的兴起，佛塔历经漫长的历史演变过程后，形成了多种的风格和形式。佛 塔被视为佛陀圣物的安放处，象征着佛陀的身体、言语和心灵。它们也通常建造 和出现在具有宗教意义的地点，作为供奉、祈祷和冥想的中心。 这座九到十世纪的尼泊尔理查维晚期佛塔，为红铜鎏金铸造，它像是一座缩 小的庙宇建筑，在微缩世界中讲述着宏达叙事。佛塔的顶部采用了典型的螺旋形 设计，这种形式在佛教建筑中被称为“相轮”，象征着宇宙的中心与佛陀的智慧之 光。圆形的半球形顶盖，称为“覆钵”，代表了宇宙的无边无际，以及佛教中解脱 轮回的理想。覆钵的形式为早期佛塔形制的延续，同样，整个上半部分也 为尼泊尔常见的早期佛塔形制。衔接着覆钵的覆式莲瓣宽大、舒展，瓣尖微微上 翘，此种莲瓣风格延续了印度那烂陀七到八世纪的早期风格。 佛塔的中段和底座则是多层的方形结构，这些结构代表了佛教宇宙观中的不 同世界。整个塔看上去就像一座完整的缩小版庙宇，塔的四周等分布局，对称的 四扇门楣前分别供奉着四尊佛，因为年代久远已经遗失。平衡对称的布局是宇宙 秩序的镜像，更是对和谐、平衡理念的诠释。在这种对称中，我们仿佛看到了天 地万物间的相互依存与制约。每一面都如同宇宙的一扇窗，具有空间的透视 性，透过它，我们可以窥见不同的风景，却又在根本上保持着一致与统一。塔身 的门廊上装饰有对称的八根立柱，柱体表面的切面塑造写实，装饰有连珠纹和部 分几何形图案。部分立柱从侧面看过去为镂空铸造，体现了当时尼泊尔精湛的铸 造工艺。立柱的上方分别有两只面对面站立的摩羯，摩羯长鼻、利齿，翻卷的尾 翼似能翻江倒海。在古印度神话中，摩羯原本是性恶的海中巨兽，会加害于人。 然而，在佛教的度化后，摩羯的形象像神兽般灵动、温顺且可爱。 随着视线顺延向上，出现在门楣中间的为天福之面。天福之面，源于上古时期的印度，是湿婆神用第三只眼变出的一只面目狰狞、双眼外凸、嘴巴巨大且极 为贪食的怪兽。然而，由于其忠诚，最终被封为专司门户的门神，其兽面常出现 在佛塔、寺庙等宗教建筑物的门楣中央或拱道之上，位置高于其他神祇。天福之 面的塑造具有极强的视觉冲击力和表现力。其面目狰狞、凶猛异常，表现出一种 震慑人心的力量感。它作为门神的象征，守护着佛教的神圣空间，同时也传达着 一种威严和庄重的感觉。在塔基的上层其中一面铸造有心形莲瓣，起到了标注塔 方位的作用，塔基上的此种莲瓣与十世纪印度库尔奇哈尔风格造像的莲瓣如出一 辙。塔基莲台的其他三个面，以折叠的塑造方式层层扩大，如同生命的阶 梯。整个佛塔上中下的结构设计，在圆形与方形两种形状的巧妙结合中，不仅展 现了建筑形式上的变化与丰富，更带有着无限的哲学关系。 试想，如果我们真的能够将自己缩小，当站在这座佛塔面前，那将是一种何 等的体验？那一刻，所有的喧嚣与纷扰都将远去，只留下内心的平静与敬畏。我 们会惊讶于这座微缩庙宇所展现出的对称、均衡与和谐之美，那是一种超越了物 质世界的视觉震撼。在这座雕塑构建的空间里，我们会不由自主地放慢脚步，让 心灵在这份宁静与美好中得到真正的休憩与升华。 这座别致、特殊的佛塔曾经可能供奉在一座重要的寺庙中，承载着信徒们的 信仰和虔诚，见证着无数次的宗教仪式和祈祷活动。尽管经历了千年的传世，这 座佛塔保存完好，这得是无数人代人的接力传承。相较同时期或者更早的类似佛 塔而言，这件佛塔是目前见到的唯一一件几乎完整的作品，大多数都是相轮部分 破损或遗失，不论稀缺性、重要性、艺术性角度而言，这件九到十世纪的尼泊尔 佛塔都是一件不可多得的艺术珍品。
viewingNote.en: This Nepalese stupa condenses architectural intelligence into a compact ritual form. The profile is clear, the massing stable, and the ornamental program remains legible rather than excessive, allowing the object to retain both symbolic and sculptural coherence. It demonstrates how strongly Newar metalwork could translate monumentality into intimate scale.

label.zh: 巴黎德魯奧，Cornette de Saint Cyr拍賣，2005年3月11日，編號9
label.en: Cornette de Saint Cyr, Hôtel Drouot, Paris, 11 March 2005, Lot 9.

label.zh: 紐約蘇富比，2008年9月19日，編號309

title.zh: 《L'art Néwâr de la Vallée de Kâthmându》賽努奇博物館，巴黎，1990年。

title.zh: Gilles Béguin & Gérard Toffin，《L'art Néwâr de la Vallée de Kâthmându》
title.en: Gilles Béguin and Gérard Toffin, *L'art Néwâr de la Vallée de Kâthmându*

excerpt.en: A 14th-century master portrait of intimate scale and concentrated presence.

viewingNote.zh: 我有一位朋友名叫扎堆。扎堆的家，在甘孜新都桥附近。他家后山上，有四座彼 此环绕、遥遥相对的雪山。每座雪山都有名字，去过那里的人，大概已经能猜到 是哪里。屋前左右两侧，是两面宽阔的牧山。夏天一到，草色丰盛，牛羊散落其 间，如云一般浮动。每次到他家，我总喜欢坐在门前那条长木凳上，安静地望着 对面的山，一坐就是一个下午。 我想，这种停留并不是偶然。因为对面的山，给人的感觉并不只是“好看”，而 是一种非常明确的精神暗示：厚重、沉稳、可靠，像一位沉默却始终在场的长兄。 它不说话，也不回应你，但只要它在那里，心就会慢慢安静下来。为什么山会带 来这样的感受？ 如果从人类经验的深层去看，这并不难理解。在极早的生存环境中，大山、大树、 巨石，往往意味着遮蔽、稳定与庇护。它们能挡风、避险，也能为人提供方向感。 与此相对，轻易移动和变化的事物，往往意味着不确定与危险。正因为如此，人 类对于“稳固之物”的信赖，很可能早已不仅是经验记忆，而成为一种更深层的 心理反应。我们看到山，会自然地产生安定感，并不只是因为它壮观，而是因为 它在漫长的生命史中，一直是庇护的象征。 荣格在分析心理学中，常把山理解为一种与“父性”“意志”“永恒”相关的象 征。山在那里，不因你的动摇而动摇，不因你的崩溃而崩塌。它不安慰你，却天 然带着一种可供依靠的秩序感。也正是这种秩序感，使山不只是自然景观，更像 一种精神原型。而这一次，当我坐在扎堆家门前，看着对面的山时，我忽然意识 到：这份感受，与这尊 14 世纪上师肖像，竟然如此相似。 第一次见到这尊上师肖像时，最先抓住我的，不是细节，而是整体。它有一种极 强的体量感和在场感。那种分量，不是由夸张的尺寸制造出来的，而是由形体内 部的稳定性生发出来的。它像山一样厚重，却毫不臃肿；像岩石一样安定，却并 不僵硬。 上师面庞圆中带方，五官开阔，嘴角含笑，自然而亲切。双眼经过神性化处理， 不追求世俗人物那种强烈的情绪表达，而是显出一种沉静、慈悲而稳定的观看方 式。宽大的耳形，与挺拔、厚实的躯体互相支撑，使整尊像散发出一种极其少见 的气质：既庄严，又质朴；既有威仪，又毫不隔人。换句话说，这尊像最打动人 的地方，不在于“像谁”，而在于它首先成立为一种精神结构。你站在它面前， 会立刻感到某种内心上的放松。不是被震慑，也不是被说服，而是像终于遇到一 个足够稳固的存在，于是你不再需要紧绷自己。也正因为如此，我会说，这尊像 像山。不仅是因为它“厚”，而是因为它“稳”；不是因为它“高大”，而是因 为它能让人安定下来。 如果进一步追问，就会发现，这种力量并不是抽象地生出来的，它来自匠人极高 明的组织能力。首先，是工艺。这尊上师肖像的制作非常细腻。嵌银的连珠纹颗 颗分明，嵌红铜的花纹笔笔清楚，哪怕最小的装饰部位，也没有因为不易察觉而 被草率处理。这样的工艺，不只是熟练，而是意味着一种高度集中的耐心和长期 训练后的控制力。因为越是细小之处，越能暴露匠人的心是否稳定、手是否可靠。 除了“做得细”，“做得有主次”可是肖像的亮点。远看时，整尊像的结构非常 清楚，没有任何局部争抢整体；近看时，细节又足够丰富，经得起反复观看。这 说明匠人在塑造之前，心中已有非常明确的整体构图意识。他不是一边做一边补 救，而是从一开始，就知道什么该突出，什么该退后，什么要成为视觉中心，什 么只需起到辅助作用。这是一种很高的能力。因为一件真正优秀的雕塑，从来不 是“细节加细节”的堆积，而是一个整体秩序先成立，细节再各安其位。 所以，这尊像的安定感，其实首先来自匠人内心的安定。他心里必须先有秩序， 手下才可能有秩序；他自己先不乱，像才不会乱。除了沉稳和力量，这尊上师肖 像另一个让我非常惊喜的地方，是它身上竟然有一种很少见的“绘画感”。通常 来说，雕塑是靠体积、空间、比例和转折来成立的；而绘画则更依赖线条、平面、 层次与节奏。但在这尊作品上，匠人似乎让两者悄悄汇合了。 上师衣袍的褶皱，并不是随意堆叠，而是一层一层排布得非常清楚。内层坎肩上 的线条、外层披袍的走势、再到衣褶落在蒲团上的那一圈圈节奏，都带着一种近 乎笔墨勾勒般的秩序感。尤其是前方垂落的布料，更像从唐卡里走出来的一段流 线——不是僵直接的，而是被一笔一笔组织过的有序、柔软。 这就让人意识到：匠人在处理这尊雕像时，显然并没有只把它当成立体塑造来做。 他似乎同时具备一种非常强的“线性意识”。也就是说，他不只知道体积如何立 起来，也知道线条如何在体积之上继续说话。这很难。因为雕塑一旦过于强调线， 很容易失去体积感；而一旦只强调体积，又容易变得粗重。但这尊像最妙的地方， 恰恰在于它让体积与线条彼此成全：形体是厚实的，线条却是清楚的；结构是稳 固的，节奏却是流动的。因此，它看上去会给人一种非常特殊的感受：它不是被 “塑”出来的那么简单，更像是从画中慢慢走出来，坐到了现实里。 说到这里，一个更深的问题也就出现了：为什么这尊上师肖像，最终唤起的，不 只是敬畏，而更像一种心安与亲切？我想，答案仍然在它的气质里。 它的庄严，不是高高在上的那种庄严；它的慈悲，也不是情绪化、外露式的慈悲。 祂给人的感觉，更接近一个真正成熟、可靠、能承接别人情绪的人。像一位有智 慧的兄长，经历过很多，却不急着说教；知道很多，也不急着证明。你站在祂面 前，会本能地觉得：这里可以停一下，这里可以把心放下来。而这种感受，与山 其实是同一种结构。山不会主动拥抱你，但它在那里，就已经构成庇护。这尊像 也是如此。它不需要向你走来，只要坐在那里，就已经形成一种能够承接人心的 空间。所以，所谓“神性”，在这里并不只是宗教概念。它更像是一种极高质量 的人格形象：稳固、开阔、慈和、有分量，又不失温度。也正因为如此，这尊像 才会让人感到熟悉。不是因为我们真的见过他，而是因为我们心里，一直都在寻 找这样的存在。 所以，当我看着眼前的上师肖像，觉得它像山一样稳时，这并不是巧合。那是自 然原型与艺术形象在内心深处的一次重逢。而真正优秀的艺术，也正是在这里发 生。它不是把世界解释给你听，而是让你在一瞬间，再次感觉到自己与世界原本 是相通的。 这是一种比语言更早的安慰，也是一种比思想更深的庇护。
viewingNote.en: I have a friend named Zhadui. Zhadui’s home is located near Xinduqiao in Ganzi. On the mountain behind his house stand four snow-capped peaks—encircling one another yet facing each other across the distance. Each of these snow mountains bears a name; those who have visited the region can likely already guess exactly where this is. To the left and right of the house stretch two vast grazing pastures. When summer arrives, the grass grows lush and verdant, and cattle and sheep scatter across the landscape, drifting like clouds. Whenever I visit his home, I love to sit on the long wooden bench by the front door, quietly gazing at the mountains across from me—sitting there for an entire afternoon. I believe this lingering is no mere coincidence. For the mountains across the way evoke a feeling that goes beyond mere "beauty"; they convey a distinct spiritual message—one of gravitas, steadfastness, and reliability—much like a silent elder brother who remains a constant, ever-present presence. They do not speak, nor do they offer a verbal response; yet, simply by virtue of their presence, the heart gradually finds its calm. Why do mountains evoke such feelings? Viewed from the deeper layers of human experience, this is not difficult to understand. In the earliest environments of human survival, great mountains, ancient trees, and massive boulders often signified shelter, stability, and sanctuary. They served as windbreaks and shields against danger, while also providing humans with a sense of direction. Conversely, things that shifted or changed with ease often signaled uncertainty and peril. Precisely for this reason, humanity’s reliance on "things that endure"—things that are solid and steadfast—has likely evolved beyond mere experiential memory to become a deeply ingrained psychological response. When we behold a mountain, we naturally experience a sense of stability—not solely because of its grandeur, but because, throughout the long history of life on Earth, the mountain has consistently stood as a symbol of sanctuary. In the realm of analytical psychology, Carl Jung often interpreted the mountain as a symbol associated with "paternity," "will," and "eternity." The mountain stands firm; it does not waver when you waver, nor does it crumble when you collapse. It offers no verbal consolation, yet it inherently embodies a sense of order—an order upon which one can rely. It is precisely this sense of order that elevates the mountain beyond a mere natural landscape, transforming it into something akin to a spiritual archetype. And this time, as I sat before Zhadui’s home, gazing at the mountain across from me, I suddenly realized: this very sensation bore a striking resemblance to that 14th-century portrait of a spiritual master. When I first encountered this portrait of the master, what initially captivated me was not the details, but the whole. It possessed an overwhelming sense of volume and presence. That sense of weightiness was not contrived through exaggerated dimensions, but rather emanated from the inherent stability within the form itself. It was as massive as a mountain, yet entirely free of bulkiness; as steadfast as rock, yet devoid of any rigidity. The master’s face—rounded yet with a hint of angularity—featured broad, open features and a gentle smile playing at the corners of the mouth, appearing both natural and approachable. His eyes, rendered with a sense of the divine, did not strive for the intense emotional expressiveness typical of secular portraiture; instead, they conveyed a gaze characterized by serenity, compassion, and unwavering stability. The broad contours of his ears complemented and supported his upright, substantial physique, endowing the entire statue with a truly rare aura: at once majestic and unadorned; possessed of great dignity, yet entirely approachable. In other words, the most moving aspect of this statue lies not in *who* it depicts, but in the fact that it stands, first and foremost, as a spiritual structure. Standing before it, one immediately experiences a profound sense of inner relaxation—not because one is awestruck or persuaded, but rather as if one has finally encountered an entity of such absolute stability that there is no longer any need to hold oneself in tension. It is precisely for this reason that I would describe this statue as being "like a mountain"—not merely because of its "massiveness," but because of its "steadfastness"; not because of its "imposing height," but because of its capacity to bring a sense of calm and stability to the beholder. Upon closer examination, one discovers that this power does not emerge from the ether; rather, it stems from the craftsman’s extraordinarily sophisticated organizational skill. Foremost among these skills is the craftsmanship itself. The execution of this master’s portrait is exquisitely refined. The silver-inlaid "pearl-pattern" borders are distinct and crisp, while the copper-inlaid decorative motifs are rendered with absolute clarity; even the most minute ornamental details—those easily overlooked—have not been treated with the slightest haste or negligence. Such craftsmanship signifies more than mere technical proficiency; it bespeaks a state of highly concentrated patience and a mastery of control honed through years of rigorous training. For it is precisely in the minutest details that one can discern whether the craftsman’s mind is steady and his hands reliable. Beyond mere "fineness of execution," a key highlight of this portrait lies in its sense of "hierarchical emphasis." Viewed from a distance, the overall structure of the statue appears remarkably lucid, with no individual element vying for dominance over the whole; yet, viewed up close, the details prove sufficiently rich to withstand repeated scrutiny. This demonstrates that, prior to commencing the sculpting process, the craftsman possessed a crystal-clear vision of the overall composition. He did not work by improvising and correcting as he went along; rather, from the very outset, he knew precisely what to accentuate, what to subordinate, what should serve as the visual focal point, and what need only play a supporting role. This represents a skill of the highest order. For a truly exceptional sculpture is never merely an accumulation of "detail upon detail," but rather a work in which an overarching order is established first, allowing the individual details to subsequently fall into their rightful places. Thus, the profound sense of stability exuded by this statue stems, fundamentally, from the inner stability of the craftsman himself. Order must first reside within his mind before it can manifest in his hands; only if his own spirit remains unperturbed can the statue itself remain free of chaos. Beyond its qualities of composure and strength, another aspect of this portrait of the Guru that delighted and surprised me was its possession of a rare—almost painterly—quality. Typically, sculpture derives its substance from volume, space, proportion, and the interplay of planes; painting, conversely, relies more heavily on line, surface, layering, and rhythm. Yet, in this particular work, the craftsman appears to have subtly—and seamlessly—merged these two distinct disciplines. The folds of the Guru’s robes are not merely piled up haphazardly; rather, they are arranged layer upon layer with striking clarity. From the lines tracing the inner vest and the flowing contours of the outer mantle, to the rhythmic ripples where the fabric settles upon the meditation cushion, every element conveys a sense of order akin to that of a brush-and-ink sketch. This is particularly evident in the drapery cascading down the front of the figure—a fluid stream of fabric that seems to have stepped right out of a *thangka* painting. It is neither rigid nor abrupt, but rather a form that has been meticulously organized—stroke by stroke—to appear both orderly and supple. This realization brings home a crucial point: in crafting this statue, the artisan evidently did not approach the task solely as an exercise in three-dimensional modeling. He appears to have simultaneously possessed a highly developed "linear sensibility." That is to say, he understood not only how to give form and volume to the figure, but also how to allow the lines—traced across that very volume—to continue their narrative and speak their own language. This is a feat of extraordinary difficulty. For in sculpture, if one overemphasizes line, the sense of volume is easily lost; conversely, if one emphasizes only volume, the work risks becoming heavy and ponderous. Yet the true brilliance of this particular statue lies precisely in the way it allows volume and line to mutually enhance one another: the form is substantial, yet the lines remain crisp; the structure is steadfast, yet the rhythm flows fluidly. Consequently, it evokes a truly unique sensation in the viewer—it does not feel merely "sculpted" in the conventional sense, but rather as if it had slowly stepped out of a painting and settled itself into the realm of reality. This brings us to a deeper question: Why is it that this portrait of the Guru ultimately evokes not merely awe, but something closer to a sense of inner peace and intimacy? I believe the answer lies, once again, in its temperament. Its solemnity is not the aloof, distant solemnity of one who stands above others; nor is its compassion the emotional, outwardly demonstrative kind. The impression it conveys is closer to that of a truly mature, reliable individual—someone capable of holding and absorbing the emotional burdens of others. It is like a wise elder brother: one who has experienced much, yet feels no urge to preach; one who possesses vast knowledge, yet feels no need to prove it. Standing before this figure, one instinctively feels: "Here, I can pause; here, I can lay my heart to rest." This sensation, in its very structure, mirrors the experience of encountering a mountain. A mountain does not actively reach out to embrace you, yet its mere presence constitutes a sanctuary. This statue functions in much the same way. It need not advance toward you; simply by sitting there, it creates a space capable of sheltering the human heart. Thus, the concept of "divinity" here is not merely a religious abstraction; rather, it manifests as a persona of the highest human quality—steadfast, expansive, benevolent, and substantial, yet never lacking in warmth. It is precisely for this reason that the statue feels so familiar to us—not because we have actually met the Guru, but because, deep within our hearts, we have always been searching for just such a presence. And so, as I gaze upon this portrait of the Guru and sense its mountain-like steadfastness, I recognize that this is no mere coincidence. It is, in fact, a reunion—deep within the psyche—between a natural archetype and an artistic form. And it is precisely at this juncture that truly great art comes into being. Its purpose is not to interpret the world *for* you, but to allow you—in a single, fleeting moment—to once again feel your own intrinsic interconnectedness with the world around you. This is a form of solace that precedes language, and a sanctuary that runs deeper than thought.
